docs.body: # Both Hands Full — ElevenLabs Audio Script v6 ## Personal essay version — biography as argument For audio generation. Paragraph breaks = natural pauses. No stage directions, no slide cues. This is Kris's story as the creative argument. --- ## Cult Baby I was born at home, in my parents' bed. My parents — Kelly and Debbie Krug — were nineteen years old. They were part of a religious commune, a tight-knit faith community that had a very particular view of the world: natural birth, no doctors, no outside intervention. God would provide. And when I got sick enough that I almost died — that's not metaphor, I literally almost died — they took me to the emergency room anyway. They chose their kid over the rules. And the church shunned them for it. Showed up at the door and told them they were out of the community they'd built their whole adult lives inside. So we packed up. We moved to California. We were the first-ever California Krugs. My mom and dad are in this room today. I love you. I grew up not knowing that a person could be a creative professional. Where we came from — evangelical, conservative, small-town California moving into Sacramento — the people who made art, who took photographs, who worked in film — those people were called worldly. That was not a compliment. It meant: not your people. Not your path. Not a real life. So I tried things. Ten years of trumpet. Drawing. Writing. I wasn't bad at any of it. I just had no map. At twenty, I met a photographer for the first time. Not famous. Just a person who did that for work. Something cracked open. I thought: these are real jobs. You can actually do this. But I still hadn't found my medium. That took eleven more years. And it didn't happen the way I expected. --- ## My Camera Saved My Life It happened in a hospital. My son Judah was born with serious health complications. Over the course of those first years, I performed CPR on my children more than twenty times. My daughter Sierra went through Oakland Children's Hospital. There was a morning I couldn't strap Judah's limp blue body into the car seat — so I went back inside and did rescue breathing again until he came back to me. I picked up a camera not for any artsy reason. The company needed photos and we were too broke to hire a pro. But something else happened. The camera got me out of the house. It gave me somewhere to put my attention when my attention was otherwise drowning. I could hold my grief up to the light instead of being swallowed by it. The camera was a survival device before it was a creative tool. It interrupted the despair. I posted the photos to Flickr. Raw, unfiltered. One of Judah's NICU photos ended up on Flickr's homepage. It's not about the technique. People are responding to the truth in the image. That was the first time I understood what any of this is actually for. Not for product launches. For connection. For making something real when the world goes fragile. That camera didn't leave my hands for the next twenty years. --- ## 145,000 Frames I released a hundred and forty-five thousand photographs under Creative Commons — specifically Attribution-ShareAlike, which means anyone can use them, share them, build on them, as long as they credit me and keep the same license open for the next person. You can't outgive the goddess. It turned out to be a benevolent curse. The critics said I was crazy. Piracy, they said. I told them they were thinking about it wrong: piracy isn't your biggest fear. Obscurity is. Those photos spread like a benevolent virus — each one carrying my name. The calls came from National Geographic, Rolling Stone, the New York Times. They didn't want the free stuff. They wanted the premium experience. Giving away made me invaluable. For twenty years that logic was sound. AI broke it. I'll come back to that. --- ## Dada, Punk, DJs, Hip Hop, AI Here's a framework. Because this story is not new. The Dadaists picked up the newspaper — a corporate mass-production technology — and tore it into collage. The Situationists called it détournement: taking the tools of corporate spectacle and turning them against the spectacle. William Burroughs cut up text and rearranged it on the floor. His whole point: arrangement and selection is the medium. The Xerox punks picked up the copy machine and made zines. Then in Jamaica, the hero of the dancehall wasn't the musician — it was the Selector. That idea took a boat to the Bronx and became Hip Hop. Every generation: the corporations build the infrastructure. The weirdos figure out what it's actually for. --- ## The Selector and Taste In Kingston, making the sound wasn't the hard part anymore. Knowing which sound the room needed at that exact moment — that was the skill. That was taste. And the Selector was listening before the first record dropped. Taste isn't just aesthetic preference. It's social intelligence. It emerges from being present in the room before you open your mouth. Generation is completely free now. AI can write a screenplay in ten seconds. What's scarce — genuinely scarce — is taste. Your specific weird eye. Your instinct for when something is technically fine but somehow wrong. Your refusal to use the five corporate adjectives everyone else reaches for. The Selector is the job. It always was. --- ## The Feedback Loop — A Gym for Taste Think about what mastery used to look like. Shoot a roll of film. Develop it. Hope a magazine publishes it. A reader writes a letter. That loop took a year. That's why mastery took fifteen. Then Flickr happened. I posted every single day. Feedback every day. I ran the loop again the next morning. It's like a gym for the development of taste — you don't get strong by going once. AI compresses the loop further. You can run taste cycles in an afternoon that used to take a season. But here's what nobody tells you: speed is not the point. Speed without judgment is kind of like a leaf blower — you're out there just moving the mess around, turning gas into noise. The

Slide 12 — Both Hands Full ← THE FRAME

docs.body: # Both Hands Full — Speaker Notes ## Creative Mornings Vancouver · May 1, 2026 · 22 slides · 20 min --- ## Slide 1 — Both Hands Full (Title) Theme: Walk out quiet. Let the room arrive. - No talking over the title — hold silence while people read it - Take a breath. Find Kelly and Debbie in the room before you speak - Transition: when the room settles, launch Cult Baby without preamble --- ## Slide 2 — Cult Baby Theme: Origin — family, faith, defiance as inherited DNA - "My mom and dad — they are in this room today" — look for them, pause - The commune shunning is the setup: chosen love over institution = the covenant - Land "first-ever California Krugs" lightly — warm, not heavy --- ## Slide 3 — My Camera Saved My Life Theme: Photography as survival device, not art career - CPR 20+ times — say it plainly, don't rush past it - "The camera interrupted the despair" — this is the line, let it breathe - Flickr homepage = first feedback loop; first proof that truth > technique --- ## Slide 4 — 145,000 Frames Theme: Radical openness as strategy — and the covenant that will be broken - "Obscurity is your enemy, not piracy" — the thesis of the next twenty years - "Giving away made me invaluable" — biggest recognition beat in Act I - "For twenty years that logic was sound. AI broke it. I'll come back to that." — plant the hook, then pivot to the pattern --- ## Slide 5 — Dada >> Punk >> DJs >> Hip Hop >> AI Theme: Every generation uses corporate infrastructure against the spectacle — this story is not new - Open with energy: "Here's a framework. Because this story is not new." - Détournement, Burroughs, Xerox punks, Selector, Hip Hop — land the rhythm, don't explain each one - "The weirdos figure out what it's actually for." — pause on this. This is the thesis. --- ## Slide 6 — The Selector / Taste Theme: Taste is the scarce resource now that generation is free - "Your specific weird eye" — give this room to land - "Instinct for when something is technically fine but somehow wrong" — this gets nods - "The Selector is the job. It always was." — period. Don't elaborate. --- ## Slide 7 — Feedback Loop / Mastery Gym Theme: Speed without judgment is noise — the gym only works if you show up - "Like a gym for the development of taste" — the metaphor that lands consistently - "Leaf blower — moving the mess around, turning gas into noise" — this gets laughs - "The AI assists within each phase. It must not determine what comes next. That's your job." — point at the room on "your job" --- ## Slide 8 — Cutting Room Floor Theme: What you reject defines your taste more than what you keep - Short beat — don't over-explain - "What did you throw away this week?" — rhetorical, but pause for it to sink in - Quick transition to Pattern --- ## Slide 9 — Pattern I Couldn't Name Theme: Encoded choice — taste has a shape, AI makes it legible - "That accumulation has a shape — even when you can't name it" — this lands on photographers and writers - "That's not replacement. That's revelation." — say it slow. Temperature about to drop. - "I'll show you exactly how to do that for yourself in a minute." — plant the Three Documents payoff, then advance --- ## Slide 10 — The AI Chapter Theme: The covenant broke — non-consensual, 1800 images scraped - "I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual." — let it land, hold the beat - "They took the file. They did not keep the relationship." — the clearest sentence in the talk - "I haven't resolved that. I'm living inside it." — do NOT rush. Authenticity is the whole slide. --- ## Slide 11 — Boosters vs Doomers Theme: Name the binary trap before refusing it - "Both feel clean. Both are incomplete." — pause between each - "You know the binary is lazy writing." — CMVan audience will feel this viscerally - Leave the third option unnamed — the next two slides ARE the answer. Hold silence before advancing. --- ## Slide 12 — Both Hands Full ← THE FRAME Theme: Name the frame — one sentence, then move - ONE sentence: "That's not contradiction. That's complexity." - "Both hands full. That's the only honest place to stand." — land it, then advance - This slide is the name. The next slide is the proof. --- ## Slide 13 — Three Fears + Audience Engagement Theme: Name the weight in the left hand before the surprise - AUDIENCE BEAT: "What scares you about this moment? Shout it out." — genuinely listen, 60 sec - Acknowledge themes from the room before naming your own three - "Remix has culture. Extraction has appetite." — say it like a verdict - "We owe young creatives honesty, not reassurance." — don't soften this - "These are real. I don't want to paper over them." — close firmly --- ## Slide 14 — Name What You See (Dr. Joy) Theme: Stop saying bias — get specific, get named - Left hand is now loaded — fears named by audience AND by you. Get specific now. - "Now that we've named what's in that left hand — let me get specific about what we're holding." - "Stop saying bias. Name what you're seeing." — firm, not preachy - The marketing professor story gets recognition in every room — let it breathe - "Bias laundering — discrimination that looks like math." — this phrase does the work - "The mirror reflects. It doesn't correct. Correcting the mirror is your job." — point at room --- ## Slide 15 — More Creative Than Ever ← THE CLIMAX Theme: The surprise testimony — only lands here because the full weight came first - The surprise hits hardest here. Acknowledge the weight: "Despite the extraction. Despite the misogyny laundered as math. Despite everything we just named..." - Pause before: "here's what I need you to sit with at the exact same time:" - "I am more creative, more productive, and more powerful than I have ever been in my entire life." — say it big, then go quiet - "Both of those statements are true. At the exact same time." — let the room hold both - "Action is the antidote to despair." — this is the call. Point at the room. - Big energy shift here. The rest of the talk is practica

docs.body: # CMVan May 1 RSVP List — Audience Intelligence Event: Creative Mornings Vancouver — Punk Rock AI Date: Thursday May 1, 2026 RSVPs as of 2026-04-29: 181 --- ## Talk-Relevant Connections People who already appear in the talk or have direct overlap with the project: | Name | Role | Connection | |------|------|------------| | Kevin Friel | Video Producer / AI Consultant — Kevin Friel Is A Pixel Wizard | Featured in the talk; source of the close line "Let's use these tools to f it up, y'all." "Pixel Wizard" confirmed — not "Pixel Vision." Handle is @mrpixelwizard everywhere. Dune Part One (Oscar/BAFTA), Detective Pikachu, Bullet Train credits. Now building AI video tools in Vancouver. | | Mark Busse | Producer and Host — CreativeMornings/Vancouver | The host. Confirmed discussion-first format. Has final say on format day-of. | | Rick Etkin | Photographer/Director/Floor Sweeper — Rick Etkin Productions | Co-organizer. Photographer. Likely doing event documentation. | | Kari Marken | University Lecturer / Educational Designer — Sauder School of Business / Consultant | Co-organizer. Her audience = business school creatives — lean into the "AI is not replacing you, it's giving you a bigger orchestra" frame. | | Sean Cranbury | Honcho — Sympathetic Technology | Founding member of BC+AI. Not just a literary organizer — he's now a professional AI governance consultant for nonprofits, healthcare, and arts orgs. Deep ally. | --- ## Detailed Profiles — Notable Attendees ### Philippe Pasquier SFU SIAT — Director, Metacreation Lab for Creative AI - The most credentialed creative-AI researcher in Vancouver. His entire career is devoted to AI as creative collaborator — generative music, visual art, interactive systems. 250+ peer-reviewed papers; 8 best-paper awards. - His lab actively studies authorship and ethics: "sense of authorship, ethics, and technological acceptance" in AI art tools. Consent and training data are live research questions for him, not abstract. - Recent talks directly relevant: "Creative AI & Co-Creation" at UVic (Nov 2025), Iowa State College of Design (Mar 2026). MUTEK Forum regular. - Stance: Practitioner from inside the field — neither booster nor critic. Frames AI as co-creation partner, which complicates the theft narrative in interesting ways. Expect nuanced pushback, not hostility. - For Kris: Most likely to engage the hardest on the ethics section. Worth a direct conversation post-talk — could be a future event co-presenter or collaborator on art + AI consent. - Web: metacreation.net · SFU profile · LinkedIn: philpass --- ### Morten Rand-Hendriksen LinkedIn Learning Senior Staff Instructor — "AI Demythifier" - Norwegian-Canadian, Vancouver. Former: created web and social media curriculum at BCIT. Now has millions of LinkedIn Learning students. - Directly aligned with the talk: Wrote "AI Training and the Slow Poison of Opt-Out" — argues AI companies should be required to get active opt-in consent from creators, not rely on opt-out. Called out WordPress/Tumblr for selling user content to OpenAI and Midjourney. TEDxSurrey: "Why We're Giving AI Too Much Credit." - Frames AI as "assistive technology: tools and materials we choose" — anti-anthropomorphization, pro-creator-rights. - Stance: The most ideologically aligned attendee with Kris's themes. His opt-in consent fight, his demythifying stance, his educator reach — all maps directly. - For Kris: Connect on Bluesky/Mastodon (he's departing X). Could amplify talk content to a very large educator audience. Worth a direct mention from the stage if you want a co-conspirator in the room. - Web: mor10.com · @mor10 (Mastodon preferred) · LinkedIn: mortenrandhendriksen --- ### Tom Froese Award-winning illustrator, Yarrow BC — Skillshare Top Teacher - Commercial clients: Airbnb, Yahoo!, GQ France, Wall Street Journal, Canadian Tire. Printmaking-inspired style. NSCAD grad. 120,000+ Skillshare students. Runs "Thoughts on Illustration" podcast and Substack. - Stance: Cautiously pragmatic with real anxiety. Did a full episode "Surviving the AI Illustration Apocalypse" — invoked the Luddites, landed on three reasons for hope. Uses AI for admin (contracts, comms) but not as a creative tool. Publicly wrestling with "Should You Be Embarrassed About Using AI?" - His audience is illustrators who are worried. He's watching this carefully. - For Kris: His Luddite episode mirrors the historical pattern Kris opens with. The talk will address exactly Tom's open questions. A conversation post-talk about the "taste as the moat" frame would land — his students need that message. - Web: tomfroese.com · Substack · Skillshare --- ### Zain Meghji Associate Director, Creative Strategy — RHEI (formerly BBTV) - 20+ years in Canadian TV: eTalk CTV, Breakfast Television Calgary, Citytv Vancouver. Interviewed Oprah, Beyoncé, Matt Damon. Knows how to work a room. - Now at RHEI growing YouTube channels for major studios and TV networks. His literal job is figuring out how audiences behave on algorithmic platforms — AI-driven content discovery is his day-to-day. - Hosts "Chaotic Creative" podcast: creatives navigating the tension between artistic intent and industry pressure. The "chaotic creative" frame resonates with punk-rock-meets-AI energy. - For Kris: Interviewer energy — he'll want to extend the conversation. Potential post-talk interview or feature for the Chaotic Creative podcast. Amplifier with media instincts. - Web: LinkedIn · Chaotic Creative podcast · Beacons --- ### Sean Cranbury Honcho — Sympathetic Technology / Founding Member — BC+AI - Founded Real Vancouver Writers' Series (15 years), Books on the Radio. Now: AI governance consultant for nonprofits, healthcare associations, and arts orgs across BC. Founding member of BC+AI (Canada's first grassroots AI industry association). - Harvard Medical School certs in AI in Healthcare and Leading Digital Transformation. Has built standalone AI tools for BC healthcare practitioners. - Stance: "Skepticism towa

docs.title: Research & Reading List: Punk Rock AI
docs.summary: An annotated bibliography of the core ideas, cultural touchpoints, and technical theories informing the "Punk Rock AI" Creative Mornings Vancouver 2026 Keynote.

headings.text: 3. Generative AI as The New Xerox

docs.body: # Research & Reading List: Punk Rock AI An annotated bibliography of the core ideas, cultural touchpoints, and technical theories informing the "Punk Rock AI" Creative Mornings Vancouver 2026 Keynote. ## 1. The Heritage of Sampling & Appropriation "Cut-Up Method" by William S. Burroughs and Brion Gysin (1959): The original concept of cutting printed paper and rearranging it into literature, showing that selection and rearrangement are the core of creativity. "The Society of the Spectacle" by Guy Debord (1967): The foundational text for the Situationists; introduced détournement, the hijacking of capitalist or corporate messaging to subvert its meaning. "Sniffin' Glue" Zine History (Mark Perry, 1976): Documenting how access to corporate Xerox machines allowed the punk aesthetic and community to bypass traditional gatekeepers entirely. ## 2. Digital Identity & The "Selector" "Can’t Stop Won’t Stop: A History of the Hip-Hop Generation" by Jeff Chang: Essential reading on how Jamaican "selectors" (Count Machuki, Kool Herc) brought the concept of taste-as-a-skill to the Bronx, accidentally inventing Hip Hop with two turntables. ## 3. Generative AI as The New Xerox "Your Taste is Your Moat" (Kris Krüg, KB Article): Articulates the idea that AI made generation cheap, so judgment and selection are the only things keeping creatives relevant. "Both Hands Full" (Kris Krüg, KB Article): The philosophical stance of maintaining deep critique of AI's theft while simultaneously embracing its unprecedented capabilities. ## 4. Vancouver Cultural Context Adbusters / Culture Jamming: The legacy of turning the master's tools against the master in Vancouver; the origins of Buy Nothing Day and Occupy Wall Street. "RIP: A Remix Manifesto" by Brett Gaylor (2008): Highlighting Canada's (and Vancouver's) role in the open-source and anti-copyright cultural movement.

headings.text: Creator Case Studies (To Use on Stage)

docs.body: # Research Dossier & Story Bank: Punk Rock AI ## The Avant-Garde & Punk Lineage (Historical Context) - 1916 (Dadaism): Dada poets in Zurich cutting up newspapers and rearranging them on the floor to form new poems. This was sampling decades before vinyl existed. - William S. Burroughs: Used the "cut-up technique" with scissors on existing texts and claimed it as literature. He proved that arrangement and selection is the medium. - The Situationists: French avant-garde who coined détournement (turning the tools of the spectacle against the spectacle). Using capitalist infrastructure to subvert it. - 1976 (The Xerox Machine): A London bank clerk named Mark Perry saw the Ramones, went home, and used a defense contractor's Xerox machine to print sixty copies of Sniffin' Glue. Punk rock finally had a press. - 1976 (The Jamaican Selector): In Kingston, the person picking the records at the sound system dance was called the "selector." The skill wasn't playing the instrument—it was knowing what track the room needed. Taste was the scarcity. Count Machuki chewed matchsticks, toasted over records, and accidentally invented rap. - 1520 Sedgwick Ave: Clive Campbell (Kool Herc) takes the Kingston selector concept to the Bronx. Hip-hop is born. - Sears Catalog ($49 Silvertone): Took the electric guitar out of the hands of the elite and put it in the bedrooms of teenagers. Garage rock explodes. - Indymedia (1999): Activists hacked together open-source publishing tools to out-traffic CNN during the Seattle WTO protests. The Historical Pattern: Corporations build the infrastructure. Weirdos, kids, and artists figure out what it's actually for. ## Personal & Vancouver Context (The Story Bank) - Kris's 1,800 Photos: AI companies scraped Flickr and Creative Commons datasets. Over 1,800 of Kris's photos were sucked into the models without permission or consent. It is theft. It is non-consensual. - The True Contradiction: Even operating post-theft, Kris is more creative, productive, and powerful than ever before. This is the Punk Condition: Holding critique in one hand, and capability in the other. - Vancouver as the Hub: Vancouver isn't a sleepy coastal town; it's the home of Greenpeace and Adbusters. It is ground zero for taking corporate megastructures and turning them into counterculture. ## Creator Case Studies (To Use on Stage) - Matlock (27 Years): Sat on cassette tapes for almost three decades because the friction of finishing was too high. AI gave him the final 15% to push his album R3ALMS across the line. - Luke Minaker: 30 years building physical monsters. The ratio was always 80% drudgery, 20% creative joy. AI flipped the ratio. "I don't care about the money. I am so excited that I get to do art again." - Belgium (Actress): Bedridden for 18 months due to illness. "AI film is my hope to be able to continue to exist and to tell something." Before we get abstract about ethics, we must look at what AI means for accessibility. - Kevin Friel: 25-year Hollywood VFX veteran. AI isn't the orchestra; it gives the conductor a bigger orchestra. "Let's use these tools to f it up y'all." - Vicki (Visual Artist): Uploaded 20 of her analog photos and asked AI to abstract the patterns. Realized her intuition was just deep pattern recognition. Taste can be articulated. ## The Philosophical Framework - Fair vs. Frequent: AI knows what's frequent on the internet. It does not know what is fair. Stop using the word "bias." Start using words like theft, racism, and misogyny. The mirror reflects; we correct. - Taste as the Moat: Now that AI can generate a poem or an image in 3 seconds, generation is a cheap commodity. The only thing left that is scarce is selection. Your taste. - The Three Documents: 1. Personal Policy 2. Style Guide 3. Worldview When you externalize these, you take control of the machine.

docs.body: # Kris Krüg: Creativity Biography ## Reference doc for the CMVan talk — scenes, lines, and moments to draw from Purpose: This is not a script. It's a curated archive of real scenes, specific details, and usable lines from Kris's 30-year creative practice. Pull from here when writing the prologue, truth bombs, or any biographical beats. Sources are the book draft ( ), the Bossier interview, and the Notion KB. The through-line: Every chapter is the same move — pick up a tool built by someone else, use it wrong, share it freely, build community around it. The tech changes. The move doesn't. --- ## SCENE 1: The first computer (Harrisburg, South Dakota, 1982) Hand-me-down Commodore 64. Stayed up late in the blue glow of the screen typing BASIC commands like incantations. "The poetry of potential." Also played trumpet for ten years. "Jazz, classical, didn't matter. It was all algorithms to me — patterns to be understood, then broken." Usable line: "I was programming before I knew what programming was. Ten years of trumpet — same thing. Find the pattern. Break it. Start again." --- ## SCENE 2: The website (Trinity Western University, September 1995) First week at university. Wanders into an open dorm room and finds Jun and Mikey, Korean exchange students, hunched over a computer. "Ever built a website?" Over the next weeks, fueled by instant noodles and insatiable curiosity, dives headfirst into web design. Also meets Sarah — philosophy major, part-time hacker — in the computer lab at 2 AM. She introduces him to Marshall McLuhan: the medium shapes the message. The decision moment: Standing in the empty computer lab, snowboard in the corner. "The snow will always be there. But this — this digital revolution — is once in a lifetime." Calls his parents: "I think I've found my calling." Usable line: "I didn't study web design. I learned it at 2am from Korean exchange students in a dorm room. That's how it's always gone — somebody shows you a thing, you stay up all night until you understand it, and then you teach everybody else." --- ## SCENE 3: spark-online (1998) — digital publishing + daughter born same year Co-founded spark-online with Robert Delamar. "Not just another dot-com wet dream, but a full-on digital acid trip." Writers, artists, philosophers, tech geeks. Asking the big questions: what does it mean to be human in a digital world? Sierra born June 23, 1998. $90 to his name when they found out about the pregnancy. Rugby team threw a kegger fundraiser to help buy a ring. The lesson: You can build something and raise a family at the same time. Neither one is an excuse not to do the other. --- ## SCENE 3.5: "Worldly" — the permission gap (1990s–2001) Growing up, Kris didn't know that a person could be a creative professional. Not in the abstract — literally didn't know any artists, photographers, filmmakers, designers, fashion people. People who worked in those worlds were called "worldly" — a loaded word from a religious conservative background that meant: not for you, not your people, not a real path. So he dabbled. Music. Drawing. Writing. Things he could never quite get right. Not because he lacked something — because he had no map. No models. No one who showed him that making things was a life you could actually build. At 20, he got to know a photographer for the first time. That was the insight: "Yo, there are people out there that work in these industries. These are real jobs. These are real lives." A permission structure cracked open. But it still didn't click — not the camera, not yet. More years of trying things. More dabbling. The medium he'd eventually live in for thirty years was still out there somewhere. It found him at 31, in a hospital, in a crisis. (→ Scene 4) Why this matters for the talk: The gatekeeping wasn't institutional — it was cultural, religious, built into how the world was named to him as a kid. "Worldly" is one word for it. The talk is about who gets permission to be a creative, and Kris didn't grow up with that permission. He found it sideways, late, in the last place anyone would look for it. Use with care: Kris's note — "we don't make too big a deal of it." A line or two in the open, not a set piece. It deepens the camera origin story without becoming its own story. Usable line: "I didn't grow up knowing creative professionals existed. Where I came from, those people were called worldly — which was not a compliment. I found photography at 31, in a hospital, and it was the first creative thing that ever actually worked for me. I didn't know until then that I'd been looking for it." --- ## SCENE 4: The camera (Silicon Valley / Stanford NICU, 2001) ← THE HUMAN ORIGIN STORY This is the scene that's missing from the current script. 2001. iVMG, an Accel Partners-backed startup in Silicon Valley. 9/11 hits. Then: Judah is born with serious health complications. Rushed to the NICU at Stanford. Kris is there every day, watching his newborn son hooked up to more wires than a server rack. "I pick up a camera. Not for some artsy bullshit reason, but because the company needs photos and we're too broke to hire a pro." He starts taking it everywhere — to the hospital, to the streets of San Francisco, trying to make sense of a world that feels fragile. Posting raw, unfiltered photos on Flickr. One of Judah's NICU photos ends up on Flickr's homepage. "Each click of the shutter is like a middle finger to the universe — yeah, life's a bitch, but I can still create something beautiful." "It's more than that. It's therapy. It's a way to process this shitstorm." The corporate world starts to lose its luster. Kris starts thinking about technology differently — not as an end in itself, but as a tool. A means to connect, to share, to make a difference. Usable line: "I didn't pick up a camera because I wanted to be a photographer. I picked it up in a hospital because my son was fighting for his life and I needed something to hold onto. And people started to respond. Not to the tech

docs.body: # Talk Framework v4: Punk Rock AI ## Discussion-First Format — Creative Mornings Vancouver, May 1, 2026 Format: Kris prompts → crowd discusses (3–5 min) → Kris responds with truth bombs Timing: 20–25 min total Primary script: This document Full prose fallback: (v3 monologue — all truth bombs written out in full) Biography reference: Mark's feedback: --- ## GUIDING PRINCIPLE This is not a presentation. It's a conversation Kris is prepared to lead. The truth bombs are not predetermined talking points — they're the things Kris knows are true, ready to deploy based on what actually comes up in the room. The crowd discussion isn't a gimmick; it's the whole point. Mark said it: "Let's let the crowd have their time to chat first, then you engage them and blow some minds." Read the room. Trust the material. The framework holds whether the room goes dark or light, scared or excited. --- ## OPEN (2 min) [No slides yet. Center stage. Morning light. Coffee cups. No podium.] Walk out. Take a breath. Don't rush. I'm going to tell you something true, and then I want to hear what you think. [Beat. Let the room settle.] I've been building things on the internet since 1995. Websites, companies, communities, photos — thirty years of making stuff with whatever tool was in front of me. Open-source software when nobody was doing that. A hundred and forty-five thousand photos on Flickr under Creative Commons because I believed sharing builds more than hoarding. A guerrilla newsroom at the Olympics with no press pass. A research institute on a tiny island. Hundreds of creative professionals trained to work with AI. The tool changes. The move is always the same: pick it up, use it wrong, share what you learn, build community around it. [Pause.] Here's where I am right now. I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual. I released those hundred and forty-five thousand Flickr photos freely — that was the deal I made. When I checked a training data search tool, I found roughly 1,800 of my images scraped into a major AI corpus. Nobody asked me. Nobody paid me. They just took it. And I'm more creative, more productive, and more powerful than I've ever been in my entire life. Both of those statements are true. At the exact same time. [Pause. Slower.] That's what I want to talk about this morning. But before I say another word — [Look around the room. Make contact.] What's actually on your mind? About AI, about your work, about where this is all going. No wrong answers. Tell me what you're sitting with. --- ## CROWD DISCUSSION (3–5 min) [Step back. Genuinely listen. Don't perform listening — actually listen.] Common themes that will likely come up: - Fear of replacement / job loss - "I don't know how to use these tools" - "The work feels fake now" / loss of craft meaning - Excitement mixed with guilt - Stolen work, consent, attribution - "Who benefits from this?" - "Is this just hype?" [Take mental notes. Pick 2–3 themes to respond to. Don't try to cover everything.] --- ## TRUTH BOMBS (15 min) These are modular. Deploy based on what the room raised. The clusters below are organized by theme — pick the ones that connect to the actual conversation. The order isn't fixed. Trust your instincts. --- ### CLUSTER A: Theft, Consent, Naming What You See Deploy if the room raises: stolen work, consent, fairness, "who benefits," AI trained on our stuff Truth bomb 1: Non-consensual I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual. I said that already. I want to sit with it. I released those photos because community mattered more to me than locking every file in a vault. That's a creative value I still hold. And it's also how I got scraped. The thing that made me who I am — the openness — is the thing that was exploited. I haven't resolved that. I'm living in it. Truth bomb 2: Stop saying bias We've said the word "bias" so many times it's lost all meaning. Stop saying bias. Name what you're seeing. When you type "CEO" into an image generator and get twenty white men in power poses — that's not bias. That's racism and misogyny encoded into mathematics. When it mimics an artist's exact style — name the theft. AI doesn't know what's fair. It only knows what's frequent on the internet. The mirror reflects. It doesn't correct. Correcting the mirror is your job. Three questions to carry into every AI interaction: Who is missing from this output? What assumptions did the model make? What would change if the training data weren't dominated by English-language internet? --- ### CLUSTER B: Liberation — What's Also True Deploy if the room raises: replacement fears, loss of meaning, or if the room is too doom-heavy Truth bomb 3: Luke Minaker Luke is an animator. Twelve years old, he sat in a theater watching Jurassic Park and said: I want to bring monsters to life. Thirty years later he's not bringing monsters to life. He's grinding out corporate animation. 80% drudgery, 20% creative. That's what the industry gave him. AI tools arrive. The ratio flips. 80% creative, 20% grind. Luke told me: "I don't care about the money. I am so excited that I get to do art again." The twelve-year-old who wanted to bring monsters to life? He's back. After thirty years. That's not replacement. That's liberation. Truth bomb 4: Belgium An actress who goes by Belgium was bedridden for eighteen months. Couldn't work. Couldn't perform. Couldn't do the thing that made her who she is. She started making AI avatars. Creating characters. Building stories from her bed. She said: "AI film is my hope to be able to continue to exist and to tell something." When the debates get abstract and ideological — remember Belgium. Remember what access actually means. Truth bomb 5: Matlock My friend Matt Lock — Matlock — is a musician. He makes incredible beats. For twenty-seven years, Matt had a box full of cassette tapes sitting in his house. Decades of unreleased music. Why didn't he release it? Because making the music is only 85% of the job. Finishing it — the

docs.body: # Talk Framework v5: Sequential Narrative ## Creative Mornings Vancouver — May 1, 2026 ## 20 minutes · Five acts · Slides cued throughout Format: Straight monologue. Biography as argument. Story first, ideas second. Timing: 20 min hard. Primary prose: — read that for the full version of every section. This document is the stage map. --- ## GUIDING PRINCIPLE The talk has one through-line: every chapter of a 30-year creative life is the same move — pick up the tool, use it wrong, share everything, build community. AI is the latest chapter. The argument is made by telling the story, not by explaining the argument. Start human. Stay human. The ideas land because the story lands first. --- ## ACT I — WHO I AM (4 min · Slides 1–5) The biographical foundation. By the end of this act the audience knows who Kris is and what he believes about sharing and openness — which makes the betrayal in Act III land harder. [SLIDE 1 — Title] Walk out. No rush. Let the room settle. I grew up not knowing that a person could be a creative professional. [SLIDE 2 — The Permission Gap] I mean that literally. Where I came from — religious conservative, small-town South Dakota — the people who made art, who took photographs, who worked in film — those people were called worldly. That was not a compliment. I tried things. Ten years of trumpet. Drawing. Writing. I wasn't bad at any of it. I just had no map. [SLIDE 3 — The Camera Origin] I found photography at thirty-one, in a hospital. My son Judah was born with serious health complications — rushed to the NICU at Stanford. I picked up a camera not for any artsy reason. The company needed photos and we were too broke to hire a pro. But something else happened. The camera got me out of the house. It gave me somewhere to put my attention when my attention was otherwise drowning. I could hold my grief up to the light instead of being swallowed by it. The camera was a survival device before it was a creative tool. It interrupted the despair. I posted the photos to Flickr. One of Judah's NICU photos ended up on the homepage. It's not about the technique. People are responding to the truth in the image. That's when I understood what any of this is actually for. [SLIDE 4 — 145,000 Frames] I released a hundred and forty-five thousand photographs under Creative Commons. People told me I was devaluing my work. I told them they were thinking about it wrong: piracy isn't your biggest fear. Obscurity is. Those photos spread like a benevolent virus — each one carrying my name. The calls came from National Geographic, Rolling Stone, the New York Times. They didn't want the free stuff. They wanted the premium experience. Giving away made me invaluable. For twenty years that logic was sound. AI broke it. I'll come back to that. [SLIDE 5 — Open Source as Values] Same principle when I moved back to Vancouver and co-founded Bryght with Boris Mann — the world's first Drupal services company. We built Dead.net for the Grateful Dead. A client asked: "You want me to pay for software that's free?" I said: you know how to drive a car. But if the engine explodes, you're not rebuilding it yourself. The software was free. The expertise wasn't. Open-source wasn't a business model. It was a refusal to build walls. Open source taught me: value doesn't live in the locked file. Value lives in the relationship, the expertise, the community, the trust. I couldn't see it yet, but that's the principle AI would later turn against me. --- ## ACT II — THE PATTERN (5 min · Slides 6–11) The lineage. This is where the talk earns the right to say "this story is not new." The biographical is now expanding into history — showing that every generation does the same thing with the infrastructure they inherit. [SLIDE 6 — Dada → Punks → DJs → AI] I'd been making this move before I knew it had a name. In 1998, I launched a community called Spark Online — "exploring electronic consciousness." Pre-blog, pre-social media, a small circle of people building public lives on the web. And the practice that came with it: document everything. Publish before, during, and after. Not to build an audience — to build infrastructure against forgetting. Before I get to what happened next — I need to give you a framework. Because this story is not new. The Dadaists picked up the newspaper — a corporate mass-production technology — and tore it into collage. The Xerox punks picked up the copy machine and made zines. The DJs in Jamaica picked up the sound system and invented a whole new art form. That idea took a boat to the Bronx and became Hip Hop. Every generation: the corporations build the infrastructure. The weirdos figure out what it's actually for. [SLIDE 7 — Burroughs / Situationists] The lineage goes deeper than Dada. Burroughs cut up text and rearranged it on the floor. His point: arrangement and selection is the medium. The Situationists coined détournement — taking the tools of corporate spectacle and turning them against the spectacle. Not rejecting the infrastructure. Redirecting it. Every time we talk about AI as unprecedented — we're forgetting centuries of artists who did exactly this. [SLIDE 8 — The Selector] In the dancehalls of Kingston, the hero wasn't the musician. It was the Selector — the person who chose the records. Because making the sound wasn't the hard part anymore. Knowing which sound the room needed at that exact moment — that was the skill. That was taste. And here's what I want you to notice: the Selector was listening first. Before the first record dropped. Taste isn't just aesthetic preference. It's social intelligence. It emerges from being present in the room before you open your mouth. Generation is completely free now. AI can write a screenplay in ten seconds. What's scarce — genuinely scarce — is taste. Your specific weird eye. Your instinct for when something is technically fine but somehow wrong. The Selector is the job. It always was. [SLIDE 9 — The Feedback Loop] Think about what mastery used t

docs.body: # Talk Framework v6: Sequential Narrative ## Creative Mornings Vancouver — May 1, 2026 ## 20 minutes · Seven acts · 22 slides Format: Straight monologue. Biography as argument. Story first, ideas second. Timing: 20 min hard. Primary prose: — read that for the full version of every section. This document is the stage map. --- ## GUIDING PRINCIPLE One through-line across 30 years: pick up the tool, use it wrong, share everything, build community. AI is the latest chapter. The argument is made by telling the story, not by explaining the argument. Start human. Stay human. The ideas land because the story lands first. --- ## ACT I — ORIGIN (3 min · Slides 1–4) Three biographical stories in a row. By slide 4 the audience knows: sharing freely was the covenant, openness was the identity. That's what makes the betrayal land. [SLIDE 1 — BOTH HANDS FULL] Walk out. No rush. Let the room settle. [SLIDE 2 — Cult Baby] I was born at home, in my parents' bed. My parents were nineteen. They were part of a religious commune that didn't believe in doctors. When I got sick enough that I almost died, they took me to the emergency room anyway. The church shunned them for it. We packed up and moved to California. We were the first-ever California Krugs. My mom and dad, Kelly and Debbie — they are in this room today. I grew up not knowing that a person could be a creative professional. Where we came from, the people who made art, who took photographs, who worked in film — those people were called worldly. That was not a compliment. I tried things. Ten years of trumpet. Drawing. Writing. I wasn't bad at any of it. I just had no map. [SLIDE 3 — My Camera Saved My Life] I found photography at thirty-one, in a hospital. My son Judah was born with serious health complications. I performed CPR on my children more than twenty times in those early years. My daughter Sierra went through Oakland Children's Hospital. There was a morning I couldn't strap Judah's limp blue body into the car seat — so I went back inside and did rescue breathing again until he came back. I picked up a camera not for any artsy reason. The company needed photos and we were too broke to hire a pro. But something else happened. The camera got me out of the house. It gave me somewhere to put my attention when my attention was otherwise drowning. I could hold my grief up to the light instead of being swallowed by it. The camera interrupted the despair. I posted photos to Flickr. One of Judah's NICU photos ended up on the homepage. That's when I understood what any of this is actually for. [SLIDE 4 — 145,000 Frames] I released a hundred and forty-five thousand photographs under Creative Commons — specifically Attribution-ShareAlike, which means anyone can use them, share them, build on them, as long as they credit me and keep the same license open for the next person. You can't outgive the goddess. It turned out to be a benevolent curse. People told me I was devaluing my work. I told them they were thinking about it wrong: piracy isn't your biggest fear. Obscurity is. Those photos spread like a virus — each one carrying my name. The calls came from National Geographic, Rolling Stone, the New York Times. They didn't want the free stuff. They wanted the premium experience. Giving away made me invaluable. For twenty years that logic was sound. AI broke it. I'll come back to that. --- ## ACT II — THE PATTERN (4 min · Slides 5–9) The lineage. Every generation uses the new corporate infrastructure against the spectacle. This earns the right to say AI is not new — and sets up the Selector as the frame for taste. [SLIDE 5 — Dada >> Punk >> DJs >> Hip Hop >> AI] The Dadaists picked up the newspaper — a corporate mass-production technology — and tore it into collage. The Situationists called it détournement — taking the tools of corporate spectacle and turning them against the spectacle. The Xerox punks picked up the copy machine and made zines. Burroughs cut up text and rearranged it on the floor: arrangement and selection is the medium. Then in Jamaica, the hero of the dancehall wasn't the musician — it was the Selector. That idea took a boat to the Bronx and became Hip Hop. Every generation: the corporations build the infrastructure. The weirdos figure out what it's actually for. [SLIDE 6 — The Selector / Taste] In Kingston, making the sound wasn't the hard part anymore. Knowing which sound the room needed at that exact moment — that was the skill. That was taste. And the Selector was listening before the first record dropped. Taste isn't just aesthetic preference. It's social intelligence. It emerges from being present in the room. Generation is completely free now. AI can write a screenplay in ten seconds. What's scarce — genuinely scarce — is taste. Your specific weird eye. Your instinct for when something is technically fine but somehow wrong. Your refusal to use the five corporate adjectives everyone else reaches for. The Selector is the job. It always was. [SLIDE 7 — The Feedback Loop / Mastery Gym] Think about what mastery used to look like. Shoot a roll of film. Develop it. Hope a magazine publishes it. A reader writes a letter. That loop took a year. That's why mastery took fifteen. Then Flickr happened. I posted every single day. Feedback every day. I ran the loop again the next morning. It's like a gym for the development of taste. You don't get strong by going once. AI compresses the loop further still. You can run taste cycles in an afternoon that used to take a season. But here's what nobody tells you: speed is not the point. Speed without judgment is kind of like a leaf blower — you're out there just moving the mess around, turning gas into noise. The AI assists within each phase. It must not determine what comes next. That's the human's job. Every time. [SLIDE 8 — The Cutting Room Floor] The loop is only as good as the taste you bring to it. The machine didn't teach me what good looks like. Twenty years of choosing did. Here's a question n

docs.body: # Both Hands Burning Type: Original song for CMVan keynote "Both Hands Full" Album: Too Weird to Die (LP1 bonus / standalone single) Written: 2026-04-30 — Kris Krug + Claude (Anthropic) Genre: Doom country / gothic outlaw / cowpunk Suno style prompt: --- ## Lyrics [VERSE 1] Left hand carrying the wreckage Right hand holding onto light Every preacher says to choose one I said both and walked into the night They built the rails, we found the rhythm They named the box, we found the sound The strange ones always catch the fire Before the money comes to town [CHORUS] Both hands full, still burning Both hands full, still here Not a booster, not a doomer Got no time for that kind of fear Swimming against the current That's what living fish will do Both hands full of fire and damage Both hands reaching out to you You coming? You coming? You coming with me? [VERSE 2] Gave it all away for thirty years Called obscurity my enemy by name Every frame I threw onto the pile Was the reason that the others came They scraped it for the training data Never asked and never stayed Find that non-consensual And I'm still here unafraid [VERSE 3] Speed without judgment is a leaf blower Noise and heat and moving mess Your taste lives in the cutting room In everything you wouldn't bless The selector heard the record Before the first one hit the floor You know a thing by knowing What you're never going for [VERSE 4] Dead fish float downstream easy Let the current do the work Live fish push against the river Through the cold and weight and hurt Build a posse not a platform Give before they know your name The weirdos figure out the future And the tools are never the same [CHORUS — OUT] Both hands full, still burning Both hands full, still here Not a booster, not a doomer Got no time for that kind of fear Swimming against the current That's what living fish will do Both hands full of fire and damage Both hands reaching out to you You coming? You coming? You coming with me? --- ## Notes - Written as a companion piece to the CMVan keynote "Both Hands Full" (Creative Mornings Vancouver, May 1, 2026) - Spirit of the talk rather than literal recap: holding contradiction, the weirdos-first lineage, giving everything away, consent violation without collapse, swimming upstream as identity - Picks up the "dead fish / live fish" Dr. Ronald Shaw quote (Sacramento youth pastor 1992) as the closing image - "Leaf blower" line echoes the mastery gym / taste / judgment section of the talk - "The strange ones always catch the fire / before the money comes to town" — the Dada → Punk → DJs → Hip Hop → AI lineage compressed to two lines - Suno section markers: , , etc. — use Custom Mode, generate 3–5 takes, trumpet solo can be coaxed with marker between verse 3 and 4

docs.title: Both Hands Full: How To Hold Critique And Curiosity Simultaneously
docs.summary: I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual.
docs.body: # Both Hands Full: How To Hold Critique And Curiosity Simultaneously I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual. LaSalle College, January 2026. 200 art and design students. Silence. "It didn't ask me to steal my work, to take over this stuff, to change everything around me. All I'm trying to do is keep my head above water and reinvent myself." The feeling: One day you're building a career through craft. Years of mastering your tools, developing your eye, earning your reputation. Next day: Someone's selling your style for $20/month. Subscribe to "draw like you" as a service. You didn't choose this. Nobody asked: "Hey, can we train AI on all your work?" Nobody said: "This will fundamentally alter your industry—cool with that?" It just... happened. That's what this is. A moment that didn't ask permission. And here's the thing: You can be pissed about that AND still engage. Both are true. Most conversations about AI force you to pick a side: The Boosters: "AI is amazing! Embrace it fully! Stop being a luddite!" The Doomers: "AI is destroying everything! Resist! Don't be complicit!" Both camps say: Pick a side. I'm saying: What if you don't? What if you hold the critique in your left hand—AI IS trained on stolen work, consent WAS violated, junior pipelines ARE collapsing— AND hold curiosity in your right hand—designers ARE compressing weeks into hours, new capabilities ARE unlocked, transformation IS happening— And keep walking forward with both hands full? This article is about that practice. --- ## Left Hand: The Critique What's actually true about the problems with AI. Not hypothetical fears. Real shit happening right now. Fear 1: Stolen Work Without Consent Training data equals humanity's creative output scraped without permission. Your photos. Your writing. Your code. Your art. Used without asking. No compensation. No attribution. This is theft. Call it what it is. I released thousands of photos on Flickr under Creative Commons over two decades. When I checked a dataset search tool, I found roughly 1,800 of my images in a major training corpus. That doesn't mean every AI model used them—training pipelines vary—but it means the consent story is exactly as casual as critics claim. Nobody asked me. They just took it. Fear 2: Race To The Bottom "Good enough" AI outputs devalue human craft. Why pay $5,000 for a designer when Midjourney costs $20/month? Why hire a writer when ChatGPT writes "adequate" copy? Market pressure toward commodification. Real. Happening now. Clients are saying it out loud: "Can't you just use AI for this?" Translation: "Why should I pay you when a tool can approximate it?" That pressure is fucking with people's livelihoods. Fear 3: Junior Pipeline Collapse Entry-level jobs vanishing. The roles that trained seniors are disappearing. Peter's example from Week 1 of every cohort: Ivy League grad can't find first job. Entry work done by AI now. How do people become seniors if they can't be juniors first? Nobody knows. This isn't theoretical. I'm watching it happen. Junior designers getting cut. Junior writers replaced by AI drafts that seniors edit. The pathway from novice to expert is eroding. We're creating a skills gap we don't know how to fill. Fear 4: Cognitive Dependency If you outsource thinking, do you atrophy? Use calculator daily → Mental math degrades. Use GPS constantly → Spatial memory weakens. Use AI for everything → Does critical thinking diminish? The muscle atrophy problem. Real concern. I don't know the answer yet. Neither does anyone else. We're all participants in an experiment we didn't sign up for. Fear 5: Commodification If anyone can generate anything, why do they need you? Expertise devalued. Craft dismissed. "We'll just use AI" becomes the answer. This is the fear underneath all others: Becoming obsolete. And it's not irrational. It's happening in pockets. Stock photography collapsed. Junior copywriting getting automated. Design work commodified. Not everywhere. Not all at once. But enough that the pattern is visible. At LaSalle, students added more fears: - Environmental impact (training models burns energy) - Emotional content (AI can't feel, but it fakes emotion) - Cultural homogenization (AI averages everything toward bland) All legitimate. What I tell students: These risks are real. I'm not dismissing them. I'm not saying "it'll be fine." I'm saying: These fears are legitimate. AND you still have agency. The binary trap: If you acknowledge these fears, you're expected to resist AI completely. That's the trap. You can see the problems clearly AND engage anyway. Critique doesn't require withdrawal. The left hand: Hold these fears. Don't drop them. Stay critical. They keep you honest. --- ## Right Hand: The Curiosity What's also true. Not hype. Actual transformations I've witnessed. Transformation 1: Maya Bruck (UX Designer) Former Etsy. Self-described "strategic skeptic." Reinvented her workflow using Figma + ChatGPT. Her trajectory: Resistant → Experimental → Fluent → Teaching others. Not replaced. Transformed. She's now teaching AI integration to other designers. Her value went up, not down. Because she learned to use AI to amplify her judgment, not replace it. Transformation 2: Kevin Friel (VFX Veteran) 25 years Hollywood. Dune. Detective Pikachu. Built "Vibe Lounge"—real-time generative visuals. Uses AI as competitive differentiator, not replacement. Kevin's not worried about AI taking his job. He's using it to do things that weren't possible before. Creating work that stands out because of what he brings to the tools. Transformation 3: My Own Work Trained a model on 2,000 cross-processed portraits. My own photos. Not using others' stolen work. Result: - Presented at Ars Electronica - Honorable mention - Published peer-reviewed research - Own inputs, own outputs Not a consumer of ChatGPT. A producer of models. Proving that alternative approaches exist. You can engage with AI without participating in the extraction model. Trans

docs.title: Both Hands Full: How To Hold Critique And Curiosity Simultaneously
docs.summary: I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual.
docs.body: # Both Hands Full: How To Hold Critique And Curiosity Simultaneously I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual. That's what I told 200 art and design students at LaSalle College in January 2026. Not as provocation. As testimony. The room went silent in that specific way rooms go silent when someone names the thing everyone's been feeling but nobody's been saying. "It didn't ask me," I continued. "Didn't ask to steal my work, to take over this stuff, to change everything around me. All I'm trying to do is keep my head above water and reinvent myself." One day you're building a career through craft. Years of mastering your tools. Developing your eye. Earning your reputation the slow way, the hard way, the only way that used to exist. You've put in the hours. You've paid the dues. You've built something that feels solid. Next day, someone's selling your style for $20 a month. Subscribe to "draw like you" as a service. Your voice, your aesthetic, your hard-won expertise, packaged and productized and available to anyone with a credit card. You didn't choose this. Nobody asked. Nobody said "Hey, can we train AI on everything you've ever made?" Nobody said "This will fundamentally alter your industry—you cool with that?" It just happened. While you were paying your bills and raising your kids and doing your work, the world changed underneath you. That's what I mean by non-consensual. This moment didn't ask permission. And here's the thing that makes most people's heads explode: You can be pissed about that—rightfully, legitimately pissed—AND still engage. Both are true. Both matter. Both are necessary. Most conversations about AI want to force you to pick a side. You've been in these conversations. We all have. On one side, the Boosters: "AI is amazing! Embrace it fully! Stop being a luddite! The future is here and you're either on the train or under it!" They've got venture capital backing and tech conference keynotes and a dismissiveness that makes your teeth hurt. On the other side, the Doomers: "AI is destroying everything! Resist! Don't be complicit! This is theft and we should burn it all down!" They've got legitimate grievances and moral clarity and a purity that leaves no room for nuance. Both camps say the same thing underneath all the rhetoric: Pick a side. Choose your team. All in or all out. I'm saying: What if you don't? What if you hold the critique in your left hand—AI IS trained on stolen work, consent WAS violated, junior pipelines ARE collapsing, this IS extraction masquerading as innovation—and hold curiosity in your right hand—designers ARE compressing weeks into hours, new capabilities ARE being unlocked, transformation IS happening whether we like it or not—and you just keep walking forward with both hands full? That's the practice I want to offer you. Not because it's comfortable. It's deeply uncomfortable. But because it's the only honest position I've found. Let me show you what's in my left hand. The critique. The fears. The problems that are real and happening right now, not hypothetical concerns we can debate in theory. First, the foundational theft. Everything AI learned came from us. Every model was trained on humanity's creative output, scraped without permission, used without compensation, deployed without consent. Your photos. Your writing. Your code. Your art. Used without asking. This isn't a gray area. This is theft. Call it what it is. I released thousands of photos on Flickr under Creative Commons over two decades. When I checked a dataset search tool, I found roughly 1,800 of my images in a major training corpus. That doesn't mean every AI model used them—training pipelines vary—but it means the consent story is exactly as casual as the critics claim. Nobody asked me. They just took it. Second, the race to the bottom. "Good enough" AI outputs are devaluing human craft in real time. Why pay $5,000 for a designer when Midjourney costs $20 a month? Why hire a writer when ChatGPT writes "adequate" copy for free? Market pressure toward commodification. Real. Happening now. I watch students lose bids to clients who've decided AI is good enough. That pressure is messing with real people's livelihoods. Third, the junior pipeline collapse. Entry-level jobs vanishing. The roles that used to train senior creatives are disappearing faster than anyone wants to admit. Peter brings this up Week 1 of every cohort: Ivy League grad can't find first job because entry work gets done by AI now. How do people become seniors if they can't be juniors first? Nobody knows. We're creating a skills gap we don't know how to fill, and we're all just hoping someone figures it out before we need the next generation of experts. Fourth, cognitive dependency. If you outsource thinking, does the muscle atrophy? Use a calculator daily, mental math degrades. Use GPS constantly, spatial memory weakens. Use AI for everything—does critical thinking diminish? I don't know the answer yet. Neither does anyone else. We're all participants in an experiment we didn't sign up for, and the results won't be clear for years. Fifth, commodification. If anyone can generate anything, why do they need you? Expertise devalued. Craft dismissed. "We'll just use AI" becomes the client's answer to everything. This is the fear underneath all the others: becoming obsolete. And it's not irrational. Stock photography collapsed. Junior copywriting is getting automated. Design work is being commodified. Not everywhere. Not all at once. But enough that the pattern is visible. Enough that the fear is rational. At LaSalle, students added more. Environmental impact—training models burns obscene amounts of energy. Emotional content—AI can't feel but it fakes emotion convincingly enough to manipulate. Cultural homogenization—AI averages everything toward bland. All legitimate concerns. All worth holding. I tell students: These risks are real. I'm not dismissing them. I'm not saying "it'll be fine" or "don't worr

LaSalle College, January 2026. 200 art and design students. Silence. "It didn't ask me to steal my work, to take over this stuff, to change everything around me. All I'm trying to do is keep my head above water and reinvent myself." The Feeling: One day you're building a career through craft. Years of mastering your tools, developing your eye, earning your reputation. Next day: Someone's selling your style for $20/month. Subscribe to "draw like you" as a service. You didn't choose this. Nobody asked: "Hey, can we train AI on all your work?" Nobody said: "This will fundamentally alter your industry—cool with that?" It just... happened. The Non-Consensual Moment: That's what this is. A moment that didn't ask permission. And here's the thing: You can be pissed about that AND still engage. Both are true. The Binary Trap: Most conversations about AI force you to pick: The Boosters: "AI is amazing! Embrace it fully! Stop being a luddite!" The Doomers: "AI is destroying everything! Resist! Don't be complicit!" Both camps say: Pick a side. I'm saying: What if you don't? What if you hold the critique in your left hand—AI IS trained on stolen work, consent WAS violated, juniors pipelines ARE collapsing— AND hold curiosity in your right hand—designers ARE compressing weeks into hours, new capabilities ARE unlocked, transformation IS happening— And keep walking forward with both hands full? This article is about that practice. --- ## Section 1: Left Hand - The Critique (The Five Fears) (500 words) What's Actually True: ### Fear 1: Stolen Work Without Consent Training data = Humanity's creative output scraped without permission. Your photos. Your writing. Your code. Your art. Used without asking. No compensation. No attribution. This is theft. Call it what it is. ### Fear 2: Race To The Bottom "Good enough" AI outputs devalue human craft. Why pay $5,000 for a designer when Midjourney costs $20/month? Why hire a writer when ChatGPT writes "adequate" copy? Market pressure toward commodification. ### Fear 3: Junior Pipeline Collapse Entry-level jobs vanishing. The roles that trained seniors are disappearing. Peter's example: Ivy League grad can't find first job. Entry work done by AI now. How do people become seniors if they can't be juniors first? Nobody knows. ### Fear 4: Cognitive Dependency If you outsource thinking, do you atrophy? Use calculator daily → Mental math degrades Use GPS constantly → Spatial memory weakens Use AI for everything → Does critical thinking diminish? The muscle atrophy problem. Real concern. ### Fear 5: Commodification If anyone can generate anything, why do they need YOU? Expertise devalued. Craft dismissed. "We'll just use AI" becomes the answer. This is the fear underneath all others: Becoming obsolete. What I Tell Students: All five fears are LEGITIMATE. I'm not dismissing them. I'm not saying "it'll be fine." I'm saying: These risks are real. AND you still have agency. The Binary Trap: If you acknowledge these fears, you're expected to RESIST AI completely. That's the trap. You can see the problems clearly AND engage anyway. Critique doesn't require withdrawal. The Left Hand: Hold these fears. Don't drop them. Stay critical. They keep you honest. Evidence to Pull: - Five fears framework from LaSalle keynote - Student additions (environmental impact, emotional content, etc.) - Junior pipeline data (Ivy League example) - Each fear grounded in real evidence - My validation: "These are legitimate" --- ## Section 2: Right Hand - The Curiosity (The Transformations) (500 words) What's ALSO True: ### Transformation 1: Maya Bruck (UX Designer) Former Etsy. Reinvented workflow using Figma + ChatGPT. "Strategic skeptic" who now teaches AI integration. Her trajectory: Resistant → Experimental → Fluent → Teaching others Not replaced. Transformed. ### Transformation 2: Kevin Friel (VFX Veteran) 25 years Hollywood (Dune, Detective Pikachu). Built "Vibe Lounge"—real-time generative visuals. Uses AI as competitive DIFFERENTIATOR, not replacement. ### Transformation 3: My Own Work Trained model on 2,000 cross-processed portraits (my own photos). Result: - Presented at Ars Electronica - Honorable mention - Published peer-reviewed research - Own inputs, own outputs (not using others' stolen work) Not a consumer of ChatGPT. A producer of models. ### Transformation 4: Students Across 80+ Cohorts Week 1: "Will I be replaced?" Week 6: Building production systems, launching businesses, getting promoted Pattern: Fear → Capability → Strategy → Empowerment ### The Capability Explosion Real things happening: - Designers compressing 3-week projects into 4 hours - Journalists analyzing datasets they couldn't touch before - Sales leaders multiplying content 53x - Educators building tools their districts want to buy This isn't hype. This is documented. ### The Creative Power Increase My testimony: "AI is trained on stolen work without consent. And I'm more creative, more productive, and more powerful than I've ever been in my whole life." Both statements are true. The Right Hand: Hold the transformation. Don't drop it for ideological purity. This is REAL. People's lives are changing. The Both/And: You can critique the theft AND use the capability. You can resist extraction AND build with the tools. You can be pissed about non-consent AND leverage the moment. Not contradiction. Complexity. Evidence to Pull: - Maya Bruck story - Kevin Friel story - My Ars Electronica work - 80+ student transformation da

docs.summary: Week 3. Common complaint.

headings.text: The Students Who Transform vs. Those Who Stay Stuck

docs.body: # Clarity As Exposure: AI Will Reveal Everyone Who Doesn't Know Who They Are Week 3. Common complaint. Student (name varies, problem is universal): "I'm giving AI clear instructions and it keeps giving me generic corporate garbage. I don't understand. Is ChatGPT just bad at my domain?" My question: "Show me what you're asking it." Their prompt: "Write a professional email to a client about project delays." My response: "Okay. Define 'professional' for me." Them: "You know... professional." Me: "I really don't. Does 'professional' mean formal? Warm? Apologetic? Matter-of-fact? Concerned? Reassuring? Those are six different tones, all arguably 'professional.'" Silence. Then: "I... I don't know. I just want it to sound like how I'd write it." Me: "Cool. How do you write? Formal or casual? Long sentences or short? Apologetic or factual? Do you use emojis? Exclamation points? How do you usually handle this?" Longer silence. Them: "I've... never thought about it. I just write." The problem revealed: They want AI to sound like them. But they've never articulated what "them" sounds like. The AI isn't failing. Their self-knowledge is. The uncomfortable truth: AI is a mirror. Bring confusion, get generic slop. Bring clarity, get something that sounds like you. This article is about AI as exposure mechanism for self-knowledge gaps. If you don't know who you are, AI makes that painfully visible. --- ## The Mirror Effect Three students, same pattern: Student A (Designer): Request: "Make this design more professional." AI output: Generic corporate aesthetic. Their reaction: "That's not my style at all." My question: "What is your style?" Them: "I... minimalist? But not cold. Warm minimalism?" Me: "Show me 5 examples of 'warm minimalism' you love." They struggled to find examples. The revelation: They'd been saying "my style" without ever defining it. AI exposed the gap. The fix: Spent a week curating examples. Articulated what made them "warm minimalist" specifically. Documented patterns. Colors. Typography. Spacing. Feeling. Then gave AI those criteria. Result: Outputs matched their taste. What changed: Not the AI. Their clarity. Student B (Writer): Request: "Write in my voice." AI output: Corporate. Flat. Safe. Their reaction: "This sounds nothing like me." My question: "How do you sound?" Them: "Casual but smart. Funny but not trying too hard. Accessible but not dumbed down." Me: "Give me 3 examples of sentences that nail that balance." They couldn't. The revelation: They had a vague sense of voice. Not a documented understanding. The fix: Analyzed their own past writing. Found patterns: - Sentence length: 10-18 words average - Humor: Self-deprecating, not sarcastic - Structure: Story first, takeaway last - Language: "You" and "we", never "one should" Documented it. Fed to AI. Result: AI outputs actually sounded like them. Student C (Sales Professional): Request: "Write a proposal for this client." AI output: Generic sales speak. Buzzwords. Features list. Their reaction: "This won't work for this client." My question: "What would work for this client?" Them: "Something more... I don't know. This client cares about ROI, not features." Me: "Okay. So tell AI that. 'This client prioritizes ROI. Lead with cost savings, not feature lists. Use data, not adjectives.'" They did. Result: Much better output. The revelation: They knew what the client needed. They just didn't think to tell AI that context. They assumed AI would "just know." The common pattern: All three students had expertise, taste, judgment. They just hadn't externalized it. AI made the gap visible. Not because AI is magic. Because AI does exactly what it's told—no more, no less. If your instructions are vague, outputs are vague. If your self-knowledge is unclear, outputs are unclear. The mirror doesn't lie. --- ## What Self-Knowledge Actually Means Not therapy. Not philosophy. Practical definition. Self-knowledge equals ability to articulate: 1. Your Process "Here's how I do this work, step by step." Most people: Do great work, can't explain how. Simon's revelation (from cohort): "15 years doing lead qualification. Tried to document it for AI. Realized I couldn't explain my own process." The gap: Success despite unexplained expertise. He spent three weeks documenting how he actually qualified leads. Turned out he was using 12 criteria unconsciously. Once documented, he could teach them to AI and to junior reps. Before: One-year apprenticeship to learn his approach. After: One-week training with documented framework. 2. Your Taste "Here's why I choose option A over option B." Most people: "I just know what's good." Vicki's revelation (photographer): "AI showed me I prioritize eyes over composition. I didn't know that was my pattern." The gap: Intuition without articulation. She uploaded 20 of her favorite photos. AI analyzed them. Showed patterns she'd never noticed. Eyes. Warm tones. Emotional vulnerability. Once she saw the patterns, she could teach them. Her taste became transferable. 3. Your Values "Here's what I care about, what I won't compromise." Most people: Have values, don't document them. Designer student: "I believe accessibility matters more than aesthetics. I just never wrote that down before AI forced me to." The gap: Held beliefs without explicit statement. Once documented, AI could make design decisions aligned with her values. Before, every decision required her direct input. 4. Your Voice "Here's how I sound—word choice, sentence rhythm, tone." Most people: Have distinctive voice, can't describe it. Writer student: "I didn't know I end every piece with a question until AI pointed it out." The gap: Pattern-making without pattern-recognition. Once she saw it, she could decide: Is that intentional? Do I want to keep doing it? Turned out she did. Questions invite engagement. That's her thing. The AI requirement: You have to externalize all four for AI to replicate your work quality. The benefit: This isn't just for AI. It's fo
