headings.text: Workflow 2: Capture → Transcribe → Knowledge Base → Retrieval
Workflow 3: Rapid Prototyping with Client Feedback

docs.body: --- type: article date: 2026-01-14 title: "Both Hands Full: What Creatives Actually Need to Know About AI" author: Kris Krüg wordcount: 2000 tags: [AI, creative-professionals, thought-leadership, both-hands-full] basedon: LaSalle College Vancouver keynote --- # Both Hands Full: What Creatives Actually Need to Know About AI I didn't ask for this. None of us did. One day you're a photographer, designer, writer, filmmaker—building a career on skills you spent years developing. The next day, someone in Silicon Valley has scraped your work into a dataset, trained a model on it, and is selling the ability to generate something "like yours" to anyone with twenty bucks a month. My work is in there. I released thousands of photos on Flickr under Creative Commons over the past two decades, and when I checked a dataset search tool, I found roughly 1,800 of my images in a major training corpus. That doesn't mean every AI model used them—training pipelines vary, and the tool shows evidence, not certainty—but it means the consent story around AI is exactly as casual as critics claim. I'm sympathetic to the people who want to resist. I understand the impulse to refuse, to opt out, to fight back. But I've also spent the last two years working with hundreds of creative professionals who are trying to figure out what to actually do in this moment. And what I've learned is that neither pure resistance nor uncritical adoption gets you anywhere useful. So I'm asking you to walk forward with both hands full. --- ## The Non-Consensual Moment Let's name what's real. The consent problem is real. AI systems were trained on the creative output of humanity—photographs, writing, code, music, art—largely without permission. Some of us released work under licenses that technically allowed it. Many didn't. The fact that companies are now settling billion-dollar lawsuits over training data tells you everything you need to know about how legitimate those practices were. The junior pipeline problem is real. The entry-level work that used to train senior creatives is disappearing fast. You'd get hired at a studio, spend six months converting approved assets into different formats, and in the process learn the systems, the clients, the back-and-forth. That's how juniors became seniors. When AI handles that work, we lose the pathway—and we don't yet know what replaces it. The dependency question is real. If you don't use a muscle, you lose it. What happens when we stop doing the first stages of creative work ourselves? When we outsource idea generation, research synthesis, early drafts? We're all participants in an experiment we didn't sign up for, and the results aren't in. These aren't hypothetical concerns. They're happening now. Anyone who tells you they've got it all figured out is either selling something or not paying attention. And yet. I've spent two years rebuilding my creative practice around these tools, and I've never felt more capable. Not because the tools are magic—they fail constantly, in ways I'll get to—but because learning to work with them has forced me to get clearer about what I actually bring to the work. The parts that are mine. Both things are true. The system that created these tools violated consent on a massive scale, and working with them has made me a better creative professional. I'm asking you to hold that contradiction and keep walking. --- ## Agency Via Workflows Here's where most AI advice goes wrong: it's all about tools. "Use ChatGPT for this, Midjourney for that, here's my stack of twelve subscriptions, connect everything with APIs." That approach creates two problems. First, it overwhelms people who just want to know how to do their work. Second, it misses the point. Tools change constantly. The one I'm using today might not exist next year. What matters is the workflow—the process that gets you from input to output, with checkpoints along the way. Let me share three workflows that have actually changed how I work. I'll mention tools only in passing, because the specific tools matter less than the pattern. ### Workflow 1: Research → Synthesis → Briefing The old way: Spend days reading documents, taking notes, trying to synthesize a complex topic. The new way: Drop the source materials into an AI tool that can work with documents, have it generate a synthesis, then interrogate that synthesis for gaps and errors. I used this recently with a 485-page federal budget. I needed to understand the AI policy implications. Reading it cover-to-cover would have taken weeks. Instead, I dropped it into a document AI, asked targeted questions, had it generate an interactive summary I could query further. Where it fails: The AI will confidently synthesize things that aren't quite right. It'll miss nuance, flatten complexity, occasionally hallucinate connections that don't exist. You can't use the output without verification. I treat it as a first draft that needs checking, not a finished product. The guardrail: Always go back to source documents for anything that matters. Use the AI to find where in 485 pages the relevant information lives, then read those sections yourself. ### Workflow 2: Capture → Transcribe → Knowledge Base → Retrieval Everything I say in meetings, talks, and conversations gets recorded (with consent), transcribed, and stored in a searchable knowledge base. When I need to write something, I'm not starting from blank—I'm retrieving my own past thinking on the topic. The talk I gave last night? Parts of it will become this article. Not because an AI is writing it for me, but because I have access to my own words, my own examples, my own way of explaining things. The AI helps me find and recombine my own material. Where it fails: Garbage in, garbage out. If your recordings are rambling, your retrievals will be rambling. If you haven't actually thought through a topic, having transcripts of your half-baked ideas doesn't help. The guardrail: The knowledge base is only as good as the th

docs.body: # LaSalle College Vancouver — Speaker Opportunity Meeting Date: October 3, 2025 Attendees: Kris Krüg, Linda Folster (Manager of Career Services), Miriam Subject: Planning alumni event on AI's impact on creative careers Note: The original summary referenced "January 2024" as the proposed timing — this was a typo. The event being planned is January 14, 2026. --- ## Meeting Overview - Meeting between Kris Krüg (AI expert/creative professional) and LaSalle College Vancouver representatives (Linda Folster, Manager of Career Services, and Miriam) - Purpose: Discuss potential speaking engagement for Kris to present to LaSalle alumni about AI's impact on creative professions - Proposed timing: January 2026 (avoiding Jan 28-30 when Kris is already booked) ### About LaSalle College Vancouver - Formerly The Art Institute of Vancouver (acquired around 2017) - Applied arts college offering programs in media arts and design fields - Recently moved to a new campus near Renfrew Skytrain Station - In early stages of launching alumni programming initiatives ### Speaking Engagement Proposal - Topic: AI's impact on creative careers (high student interest noted during previous alumni panels) - Target audience: LaSalle alumni (100 attendees expected) - Format: Presentation followed by networking opportunity - Potential to open event to non-alumni community members for a fee - Kris suggested this could launch a series of continuing education events ### Compensation Discussion - LaSalle facing financial constraints due to immigration policy changes affecting international enrollment - Proposed arrangement combining: - Modest honorarium - Access to LaSalle spaces for Kris's events - Photo/video documentation of the presentation - Kris proposed potential sponsorship arrangement where LaSalle could sponsor his AI creativity events for 2026 ### Broader Collaboration Possibilities - Potential collaboration with AI labs at SFU or UBC - Kris mentioned his organization and International Experiential Art Conference - Kris informed LaSalle about Signals, a creative technology festival by DigiBC that might interest their media arts programs --- ## Action Items - [ ] LaSalle to email Kris with potential January dates for the speaking engagement - [ ] Kris to share transcript of the meeting - [ ] LaSalle to introduce Kris to their marketing manager/director of admissions regarding potential sponsorship - [ ] Linda to pass information about the Signals festival to Rob (program director for media arts) - [ ] Kris to prepare a proposal for LaSalle's marketing department --- ## Transcript Linda Folster & Kris Krüg | LaSalle College Vancouver Speaker Opportunity October 3, 2025 (PDT) Hi, Chris. Hello. Hi, it's great to meet you. It's gone. There you are. All right. So nice to finally meet you, Chris. My name is Linda Folster and I'm the manager of career services at LaSalle College, Vancouver. Nice to meet you. Yes. Nice to meet you. Nice to meet you. I'm Miriam. I connected with you through LinkedIn a while back. How are you? Nice to meet you. It's great to hear from you guys. Yeah. Sal's been on my radar for the last couple years. I've been seeing some of the things that you've been doing and stuff, but I don't have a lot of direct exposure myself, but yeah, happy to hear from you all. Happy to have a conversation, see if I can help in some way and yeah. Amazing, thank you. So great that you've heard of us. Do you know much about us or do you want me to give you a little bit of a summary? You've changed your name from something to something, right? Yes. What was the school called? The Art Institute. Yeah. So that acquisition happened, was it 2017, Miriam? I am not sure. What was it called before? The Art Institute of Vancouver. Great. OK, I'm with you now. We purchased. Yeah. So we're an applied arts college and We have media arts programs such as professional recording arts, animation and 3D modeling, game programming and game design. In addition, we have other design programs such as graphic design, fashion design, interior design. And jewelry design. Am I missing anything, Miriam? Amazing. No, I think you're good. Broad cross-section of creative professions, yeah. Yeah, we're kind of a boutique. model, if you will, Boutique College. And anyways, we have just recently moved into a new campus, which is right next door to our previous campus. which is right across from the Renfrew Skytrain Station. It's gorgeous, state-of-the-art building, all glass, just, you know, a lot of purpose-built spaces, very customized for our audience of creatives. Congratulations, that must be cool. Thank you. We've been in here since July and we'd love to give you a tour someday. It's absolutely stunning. I've never been inside but I drove past it after it was this summer and I was like, it made an impression. So yeah, I do know what you're referring to. Okay, so you know where it is. So just to give you some context, we're really at the very early stages of launching some alumni programming. And, you know, we're planning what we want to do for our alumni, making sure that we can offer value to them. and trying to be as tuned in as we can be and continue to gather data about what exactly they would be interested in us in the way of offerings. And so we do a lot of events at LaSalle. And in the past year, we've offered two alumni panel events where we had alumni from our institution who are now out in the world of work and establishing themselves as creative professionals. It was a moderated panel where I had prepared some questions and asked them. The audience was current students who are approaching graduation. And there was an opportunity as well for a bit of dialogue between You know, Q&A between the alumni panel and the current students, and we did some speed networking. In any case, when we opened up the floor for the Q&A, what we noticed was a trend where, no surprise, Our students are very interested in learning about the impact of AI on th

docs.body: # LaSalle College Vancouver Alumni Talk — Jan 14, 2026 ## Event Details | Field | Value | |-------|-------| | Date | January 14, 2026 | | Time | 6:00 PM | | Location | LaSalle College Vancouver (new campus near Renfrew Skytrain Station) | | Audience | LaSalle alumni (100 expected), potentially open to community members | | Format | Presentation + networking | | Host contacts | Linda Folster (Manager of Career Services), Miriam | --- ## Talk Theme "How are creatives working with AI?" Promises from the event announcement: - A real framework for using AI as your creative partner - Insider insight into how designers, artists, and creative professionals are using it right now - Clear steps to future-proof your skills and creative edge --- ## Announcement Source LaSalle College | Vancouver (Instagram, early Jan 2026) ✨ How are creatives working with AI? Find out at our alumni event with @kriskrug, a creative technologist, researcher, and founder of the 1,000+ member @bc.ai.ecosystem Here's what you'll get: 👉 A real framework for using AI as your creative partner. 👉 Insider insight into how designers, artists and creative professionals are using it right now. 👉 Clear steps to future-proof your skills and creative edge. PLUS: Reconnect with your LCV fellows, tour the new campus, and grab some food & drinks. 🗓️ Jan 14, 2026 | 🕕 6 PM 📍 LaSalle College Vancouver Limited seats – grab yours via the link in bio 👇 See you there! --- ## Working Documents | Document | Purpose | |----------|---------| | Meeting Notes — Oct 3, 2025 | Planning call with Linda Folster & Miriam; includes summary, action items, and full transcript | | Talk Outline | Working scaffold for developing the presentation | | Video Assets | Reusable video clips with sources and context | --- ## Video Assets (Reusable) Located in : | File | Content | |------|---------| | | Dwarkesh Patel podcast — "Write for the Bot" concept | | | Dwarkesh Patel podcast — AI and human values continuation | | | AutoLoom GAN trained on Kris's street portraits (Alpha Prism) | | | RIP: A Remix Manifesto documentary trailer | See VIDEO-ASSETS.md for full documentation. --- ## Background: LaSalle College Vancouver - Formerly The Art Institute of Vancouver (acquired 2017) - Applied arts college: animation, 3D modeling, game programming/design, graphic design, fashion design, interior design, jewelry design, professional recording arts - Recently moved to a new, purpose-built campus across from Renfrew Skytrain Station (opened July 2025) - Currently launching alumni programming initiatives --- ## Compensation Arrangement (discussed Oct 2025) - Modest honorarium + access to LaSalle spaces for future events + photo/video documentation - Potential sponsorship arrangement: LaSalle sponsors BC + AI Ecosystem events in exchange for speaker engagement - Pending: intro to LaSalle marketing manager / director of admissions to discuss sponsorship proposal --- ## Related KB Resources - Kris Krüg profile — Bio, publications, community roles - Future Proof Creatives (Portfolio.YVR) — Core messaging & mission - Quotable Library (Creative Pros) — Reusable quotes for creative-professional audiences - EA Creative Innovation Keynote — Recent corporate talk with related themes --- #industry:creative #industry:education #business:dev:partnerships #business:revenue:sponsorship #org:partner:lasalle-college-vancouver

docs.summary: type: recap date: 2026-01-14 event: "Both Hands Full: How Creatives Are Actually Working with AI" venue: LaSalle College Vancouver speaker: Kris Krüg duration: 50 minutes + 30 min Q&A audience: Art and design students, a
docs.body: --- type: recap date: 2026-01-14 event: "Both Hands Full: How Creatives Are Actually Working with AI" venue: LaSalle College Vancouver speaker: Kris Krüg duration: 50 minutes + 30 min Q&A audience: Art and design students, alumni, educators source: transcript tags: [keynote, AI, creative-professionals, both-hands-full] --- # Both Hands Full — LaSalle College Keynote Recap ## Executive Summary Kris Krüg delivered a keynote to LaSalle College Vancouver alumni and students on what it actually feels like to be a creative professional in the AI era. The core thesis: walk forward with "both hands full"—one hand holding critique and resistance to AI's harms, the other holding curiosity and willingness to engage. The talk covered real fears (stolen work, junior pipeline collapse, cognitive dependency), transformation stories from creative professionals, and practical approaches to building workflows that maintain human agency. --- ## Core Thesis: Both Hands Full "AI is trained on the stolen work of mankind without consent, and I'm more creative, more productive, and more powerful than I've ever been in my whole life. Both of these things are true." The framework: - Left hand: The fears, critique, resistance - Right hand: The reality, curiosity, transformation - The call: Hold both. Keep walking. --- ## Key Themes ### 1. The Five Fears (Left Hand) 1. Stolen work / consent: Trained on mankind's creative output without permission 2. Race to bottom: "Good enough" AI output devalues human creative work 3. Junior pipeline: Entry-level roles that build seniors are vanishing 4. Cognitive dependency: What happens when we outsource early-stage thinking? 5. Commodification: If anyone can generate anything, why do they need us? Audience additions during talk: - Environmental impact (water, power) - Reliability / quality concerns - Students never learning to write - Child safety / abuse potential - Emotional content in art - "Killer robots" / militarization ### 2. Transformation Stories (Right Hand) Maya Bruck — UX designer, former Etsy. Reinvented workflow using Figma Make + ChatGPT. "Strategic skeptic" who now teaches. Kevin Friel — 25-year Hollywood VFX veteran (Dune, Detective Pikachu). Built "Vibe Lounge" for real-time generative visuals. Uses AI as competitive differentiator. ### 3. Personal Testimony - Trained model on 2,000 cross-processed street portraits - Owns inputs and outputs (not using others' stolen work) - Presented at Ars Electronica, won honorable mention - Published peer-reviewed research - "Not a consumer of ChatGPT from Silicon Valley—a producer of creativity and new models" ### 4. The Human Moat Not "creativity" abstractly—it's judgment, taste, discernment. "If you can teach an AI why you choose one thing instead of another, you're teaching it something very useful." Key documents to create: - Writing style guide (how you sound) - Worldview document (what you believe) - Glossary and anti-glossary - Decision logs with reasoning ### 5. Write for the Bot Featured video: Anonymous OpenAI employee on Dwarkesh Patel podcast: "If there are values you have which are not expressed yet in text, and if there are things that you like or want, if they aren't reflected online, then to the AI, they basically don't exist. And that is dangerously close to won't exist." The homework: - Record and transcribe your ideas - Build knowledge bases of your thinking - Make your values machine-readable ### 6. The Selector Frame RiP: A Remix Manifesto connection: - "Culture always builds on the past. The past tries to control the future." - DJs navigated this tension—they were called selectors - Generation is cheap. Taste is not. Your taste is your moat. --- ## Quotable Moments On the non-consensual moment: "I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual. It didn't ask me to steal my work, to take over this stuff, to change everything all around me. All I'm trying to do is keep my head above water and reinvent myself." On skeptics: "The most skeptical people about AI—I need you to educate yourself the most because we need your voices at the table. If you choose to resist all the way and not engage, it means that the decisions of the future are going to be made by all the people that are on the hype side." On certainty: "I don't have answers to any of these questions. I teach this shit for a living and I don't know. Anyone who will stand up and tell you that they've got all these things figured out—those are the people to not listen to." On the moment: "This moment of changing everything where on one hand there's a whole bunch of stuff to be concerned about and afraid of, and on the other hand there's so much opportunity and curiosity and transformation taking place that it's an impossibility to look away." --- ## Q&A Highlights Key audience concerns raised: 1. Children and AI — Safety protocols, developmental impact, learning dependency 2. Water/environmental impact — Data center resource consumption 3. Education/assessment — All students now performing at A-level; hard to know who's behind the AI 4. Reliability — Code quality, hallucinations, verification 5. Data privacy — Where does information go when using cloud tools? 6. Keeping up — Overwhelming pace of change, tool fatigue Notable exchange with skeptic: - Skeptic: "Are you not destroying yourself inside by saturating your life with AI?" - Kris: "Not in my case. I feel more creative, productive, and powerful than I've ever been." - (Critique note: This landed defensive rather than validating the rational fear) On keeping up: "Just swim in the ocean. You're not going to get ahead of the ocean or try to control it. But if you just go for a swim in it, that's all that's really required." --- ## People Mentioned | Person | Role | Context | |--------|------|---------| | Maya Bruck | UX Designer (ex-Etsy) | Transformation story, AI Upgrade instructor | | Kevin Friel | VFX Veteran (25 years) | Vibe Lounge creator, film club lead | | Seb | BC + AI communi

docs.body: --- type: seo-and-images sourceevent: "Both Hands Full: How Creatives Are Actually Working with AI" date: 2026-01-14 article: feature-article.md --- # Both Hands Full — SEO & Image Prompts ## SEO Metadata Title (58 chars): Both Hands Full: What Creatives Need to Know About AI Meta Description (155 chars): AI is trained on stolen work—and I've never been more creative. Learn the framework for holding critique and curiosity while building your creative future. Primary Keyword: AI for creative professionals Secondary Keywords: - AI creative workflow - AI ethics for artists - creative professional AI adoption - human judgment in AI era - AI and design professionals Slug: both-hands-full-creatives-ai --- ## Image Prompts ### 1. Both Hands Full — Hero Image For: Article header, LinkedIn featured image Prompt: A creative professional standing at the center of the frame, one hand raised on each side. The left hand holds abstract dark shapes representing concerns and fears (fragmented images, question marks, shadows). The right hand holds glowing abstract shapes representing possibility (light streams, connected nodes, creative tools). The figure stands confidently between both. Dark background with teal and purple accent lighting. Modern editorial illustration style, high contrast, conceptual portrait. --- ### 2. The Binary Trap — Rejected For: Section on "Both are lazy" / the false binary Prompt: Two opposing doors or pathways, one labeled with hype icons (rockets, sparkles, money signs) and one with doom icons (skull, broken tools, chains). Both doors are shown crossed out or fading. In the center, a third path opens forward with warm teal light. Dark background, high contrast, conceptual illustration showing rejection of binary thinking. Modern editorial style. --- ### 3. The Human Moat — Taste as Differentiator For: Section on judgment, taste, discernment Prompt: A creative professional surrounded by dozens of floating AI-generated outputs (images, documents, designs). They're selecting just one with a confident gesture while the others fade away. The selected piece glows with warm light. Visual metaphor for curation over generation. Dark background with teal and purple accents, modern conceptual illustration, editorial photography aesthetic. --- ### 4. Write for the Bot — Making Values Machine-Readable For: Section on documenting worldview, style guides Prompt: Hands typing on a keyboard with words and values floating up from the screen like particles or light streams, forming a constellation or knowledge graph above. The words include terms like "values," "style," "worldview," "judgment." The particles feed into a gentle AI presence represented as ambient light. Dark background, teal accent on the floating text, purple glow around the AI presence. Modern tech-forward illustration, warm human-AI collaboration theme. --- ### 5. Creative DNA — The Last Thing You'd Keep For: Closing reflection section Prompt: A close-up of a person's hands holding a small glowing core—their "creative DNA"—while around them, tools, tasks, and processes float away or dissolve. The glowing core pulses with warm light, representing the irreducible human essence. Meditative composition, centered focus, dark background with warm highlights on the hands and core. Conceptual portrait, editorial illustration style. --- ## Notion Portal Structure Page Title: Both Hands Full: What Creatives Actually Need to Know About AI Icon: 🤲 (open hands) Cover: Unsplash search: "hands holding contrast light dark" Sections: 1. Subtitle + Byline — "Navigating AI as a creative professional" by Kris Krüg 2. [Divider] 3. Hero Image — Generated from prompt #1 4. [Divider] 5. Full Article — feature-article.md 6. [Divider] 7. Author Bio — Kris Krüg is Executive Director of BC + AI Ecosystem and teaches creative professionals how to apply AI through TheUpgrade.ai 8. [Toggle: SOCIAL CONTENT] — All LinkedIn posts, Twitter threads, Instagram carousel from social-content.md 9. [Toggle: IMAGE PROMPTS] — All 5 prompts above 10. [Toggle: SEO] — Metadata above --- ## Related Content - recap.md — Full keynote recap with Q&A - feature-article.md — 2000-word thought leadership article - social-content.md — Social media posts - speaker-script.md — Original keynote script

docs.body: --- type: social-content sourceevent: "Both Hands Full: How Creatives Are Actually Working with AI" venue: LaSalle College Vancouver date: 2026-01-14 speaker: Kris Krüg tags: [social, linkedin, twitter, instagram, keynote, AI] --- # Both Hands Full — Social Content Library Social content extracted from the LaSalle College keynote. Screenshots available in . --- ## LinkedIn Posts ### Option 1: The Thesis Post I gave a talk to art and design students this week, and I led with this: "AI is trained on stolen work without consent. And I'm more creative, powerful, and productive than I've ever been because of these tools." Both things are true. We're past the point where you can just pick a side. The boosters who tell you "get on board or get left behind"? Lazy. The doomers who say "refuse and resist"? Also lazy. The real position is harder: hold both hands full. Left hand: the fears are real. Consent was violated. Junior pipelines are collapsing. We're outsourcing cognition without knowing the cost. Right hand: the transformation is real. I've watched designers compress weeks into hours. VFX veterans build tools they couldn't have dreamed of. My own work has never been more ambitious. The people I need most in this conversation? The skeptics. The critics. The ones with genuine concerns. Because if you opt out entirely, the decisions about this technology get made without you. Governance is set by whoever shows up. So here's my ask: engage even if you're skeptical. Learn enough to know what you're criticizing. Walk forward with both hands full. What are you holding in each hand right now? --- ### Option 2: The Skeptic Post The people most critical of AI are the ones I need in the room the most. Here's why: If the skeptics opt out—refuse to engage, refuse to learn the tools, refuse to participate—then every decision about how this technology develops gets made by the true believers. The governance. The norms. The policies. All set by people who think AI is the greatest thing ever. That's not a balanced conversation. That's a runaway train. I run an AI community with 1,800+ members. The most valuable voices aren't the hype people. They're the ones who push back. Who ask uncomfortable questions. Who won't let us handwave past the real problems. Consent violations in training data? Real problem. Junior pipeline collapse? Happening now. Cognitive dependency? We're all guinea pigs in an experiment we didn't sign up for. I don't have answers to these questions. I teach this for a living and I still don't know. But I know this: the room is making decisions whether you're in it or not. Skeptics: we need you at the table. Engage even if you refuse to adopt. Your critique is the counterweight this conversation desperately needs. --- ### Option 3: The Selector Post DJs figured this out decades ago. When sampling technology exploded, the music industry had the same fight we're having now about AI. Culture built on the past. The past trying to control the future. The DJs who thrived weren't the ones who could sample the most. They were the ones with taste. The ones who could hear a 4-bar loop from a 1972 funk record and know it would work. They were called selectors. Generation is cheap. Selection is not. That's the creative moat in the AI era. Not "creativity" in some abstract sense—everyone claims that. The moat is judgment. Taste. Discernment. The ability to look at ten AI outputs and know which one is right. And be able to articulate why. I've started building documents that capture this: a writing style guide, a worldview doc, a glossary of words I use and words I never use. Not so AI can replace me. So AI can understand what makes my work mine. Close your eyes for a second. Think of one thing you do in your creative work that you would never give up to AI—not because you couldn't automate it, but because it's the part that makes the work yours. That's your creative DNA. That's the piece worth protecting. What's yours? --- ## Twitter/X Threads ### Thread 1: Both Hands Full (5 posts) [1/5] Gave a keynote to art students this week. My thesis: "AI is trained on stolen work. And I've never been more creative or productive." Both things are true. Here's what that means: [2/5] The binary takes are lazy. Boosters: "Get on board or get left behind" Doomers: "Refuse and resist" Both let you avoid the hard work of holding contradiction. [3/5] Left hand (the fears): - Consent violated at scale - Junior roles vanishing - Cognitive dependency unknown - Race to "good enough" Right hand (the reality): - Designers compressing weeks → hours - VFX vets building impossible tools - New creative possibilities [4/5] The people I need most? The skeptics. If critics opt out, governance gets set by true believers. The room makes decisions whether you're in it or not. Engage even if you refuse to adopt. [5/5] Walk forward with both hands full. Left hand: hold the critique Right hand: hold the curiosity The moment didn't ask permission. Neither did photography, digital tools, or the internet. What's in your hands? --- ### Thread 2: Five Fears (7 posts) [1/7] I teach AI for a living and I'll tell you straight: I don't have these figured out. The 5 fears creative professionals actually have about AI: 🧵 [2/7] 1. STOLEN WORK AI was trained on humanity's creative output. Without consent. I found 1,800 of my photos in a training dataset. That doesn't mean every model used them—but the consent story is exactly as casual as critics claim. [3/7] 2. RACE TO BOTTOM When "good enough" costs $20/month, the floor drops out. Client budgets compress. Expectations shift. The work that used to pay bills becomes commodity. [4/7] 3. JUNIOR PIPELINE COLLAPSE Entry-level tasks that train seniors are vanishing. You'd spend 6 months converting assets, learning the systems, the clients, the back-and-forth. That's how juniors became seniors. What replaces it? [5/7] 4. COGNITIVE DEPENDENCY If you don't use a muscle, you lose it. What happens when we outs

docs.title: Both Hands Full — Verbatim Monologue
docs.summary: For audio practice. Read aloud at natural speaking pace. 45 minutes.
docs.body: # Both Hands Full — Verbatim Monologue For audio practice. Read aloud at natural speaking pace. 45 minutes. --- Both Hands Full. How creatives are actually working with AI. I want to talk about what it actually feels like to be a creative professional right now. Not what the LinkedIn gurus are saying. Not what the tech executives are promising. What it actually feels like to wake up every day and do creative work in the middle of this thing that none of us asked for but all of us have to figure out. I'm Kris Krüg. I run AI Upgrade, which is a training program for creative professionals. I'm part of BC + AI Ecosystem Association, which is a nonprofit building community around creative and ethical AI. And I host the Vancouver AI Meetup, which is about 1,800 people now, meeting every month at the Space Centre. So I spend a lot of time in rooms like this one, talking to people about AI. And here's what I've noticed. Every AI conversation you've ever been in probably sounded like one of two camps. On one side, you've got the Boosters. "AI is the greatest thing ever." "Stop whining, get on board." "If you're not using AI, you're already behind." These are usually tech people, executives, investors. They've got a financial incentive to be excited. And some of them genuinely are excited. But there's a dismissiveness in that position, isn't there? A kind of "your concerns don't matter, just adapt" energy. On the other side, you've got the Doomers. "AI is stealing our work." "It's destroying jobs." "We should resist." And honestly, I'm more sympathetic to this camp. Their concerns are real. I'll get to that. But there's something in this position too that lets you off the hook. If you're just resisting, you don't have to figure out what to actually do. Here's the thing: both positions are lazy. Both let you avoid the harder work of holding complexity. So let me tell you where I actually stand. AI is trained on stolen work. All the work of mankind, scraped and slurped up without consent, without compensation, with no visibility into what they took or how they're using it. That's true. That's real. Your work might be in there. Mine definitely is. And I'm more creative, powerful, and productive than I've ever been because of these tools. I have smashed through technical ceilings I couldn't break through before. I'm building things I never could have built. I'm exploring ideas faster than ever. Both things are true. And here's the framework I want to offer you tonight: we walk forward holding both. This isn't about resolving the tension. It's about carrying it. In one hand, you're holding the fears, the concerns, the ethical problems. In the other hand, you're holding the possibilities, the transformations, the things you've discovered you can do. Neither hand is empty. We don't pretend the fears don't exist. We don't pretend the possibilities aren't real. We walk forward holding both. So let me show you what's in each hand, starting with the fears. Fear number one: trained on stolen work. This is the foundational ethical problem. All the visual art, writing, code, music that trained these models came from us, from creators, without consent. Whose values does AI encode? Whose worldview is baked into these systems? These aren't paranoid questions. They're essential ones. Fear number two: race to the bottom. Clients can now get "good enough" for cheap. The floor just dropped. If you were competing on speed or volume, AI just undercut you. That's real. We have to acknowledge it. Fear number three: the junior pipeline collapse. This one keeps me up at night. Entry-level tasks, the ones that used to build senior creatives, media monitoring, basic research, first-draft layouts, those are disappearing. AI is eating them. So how do we create the next generation of senior creatives if there's no entry-level work to train them on? I teach AI for a living, and I don't have this solved. Anyone who tells you they do is lying. Fear number four: cognitive dependency. What happens when we outsource thinking? When we stop drafting and only edit? When we lose the muscle because we never use it? We don't know yet. But it's a real concern. Fear number five: commodification. If everyone can generate, what's left? If a client can make their own logo in 30 seconds, why do they need you? This is the existential question. Don't dismiss these fears. They're real. They're legitimate. We have to hold them. But now let me show you what's in the other hand. I have never felt as creative or productive or powerful as when I started messing with this stuff. It has helped me smash through technical ceilings I had not been able to break through before. Let me show you what I mean. I trained a model on 2,000 of my street portraits. Cross-process slide film. Work I've been doing for 20 years. And I used that to explore what my body of work actually means. Not stealing from others, learning from myself. Understanding my own aesthetic at a level I never had before. I dropped Canada's 485-page federal budget into a tool and said "I want to click around and understand this thing." Charts. Diagrams. Filters. Interactive dashboard. Done in hours, not weeks. I record everything. Transcribe it. Commit it to my knowledge base. Deploy AI on top of it. My past self becomes my future asset. Everything I've ever said, written, thought, organized and searchable and usable. I vibe code something almost every day. It's only 9:40 today, and I already built a web app. In the bathtub. That's not a joke. That's a Wednesday. This is the AutoLoom project. These are my portraits, the source material. 2,000 images of street photography. This is what I fed into the model. This is the interface. AutoLoom, built by Philippe Pasquier's Metacreation Lab at SFU. You feed it your images, it learns your aesthetic. No coding required. It's a GAN-based visual synthesizer. And this is the output. Left column: real portraits I took. Right column: AI-generated transformatio

docs.body: # BOTH HANDS FULL — Speaker Script ## LaSalle College Vancouver · Creative AI · January 14, 2026 ### Full Script (50 minutes + Q&A) --- ## SLIDE 1: Cover — BOTH HANDS FULL [1 min] [Let the title land. Pause.] "Good evening, LaSalle. Thank you for having me. I want to talk about what it actually feels like to be a creative professional right now. Not the LinkedIn version. The real one." --- ## SLIDE 2: About Me [1.5 min] "Quick intro. I'm Kris Krüg. I run AI Upgrade—AI training specifically for creative professionals. I co-founded BC + AI, which is building British Columbia's AI ecosystem. And I host the Vancouver AI Meetup, about 1,800 members now, monthly at the planetarium. Before all this AI stuff, I was a photographer for 20 years. Still am. Creative technologist. I've been in the trenches with these tools. So let me tell you about every AI conversation you've ever been in..." --- ## SLIDE 3: The Binary Trap [2 min] "Every time AI comes up, you get pushed toward one of two camps. The Boosters say: 'AI is the greatest thing ever. Stop whining and get on board. If you're not using it, you're already behind.' The Doomers say: 'AI is stealing our work. It's destroying jobs. We should resist.' Both of these are lazy. They're lazy because they let you off the hook. If AI is just evil, you don't have to engage with it. If AI is just magic, you don't have to think critically about it. Both positions avoid the harder work—which is holding complexity." --- ## SLIDE 4: The Thesis [1.5 min] [Read slowly. Let it land.] "AI is trained on stolen work without consent. And I'm more creative, powerful, and productive than I've ever been because of these tools. Both things are true. That's the complexity. That's what this talk is about." --- ## SLIDE 5: Both Hands Full [1 min] "This is the metaphor. Both hands full. In your left hand—the fears. The legitimate concerns. In your right hand—the possibilities. What people are actually doing. The goal isn't to pick a side. It's to keep walking while holding both. Let me show you what's in each hand." --- ## SLIDE 6: The Five Fears [5 min] "Let me name the fears. Because they're real. 1. Stolen Work. AI has been trained on the work of mankind. Without consent. No compensation. Your work might be in there. Mine definitely is. 2. Race to the Bottom. Clients can get 'good enough' for cheap. A logo that used to cost five thousand dollars? Someone generates one in 30 seconds. If you were competing on speed or volume, AI just undercut you. 3. Junior Pipeline Collapse. This is the one that keeps me up at night. Entry-level tasks that build senior creatives—media monitoring, basic research, first drafts—they're vanishing. How do we build seniors if juniors never get to do the reps? I teach AI for a living, and I don't have this solved. Anyone who tells you they do is lying. 4. Cognitive Dependency. What happens when we outsource thinking? When we stop drafting and only edit? When we lose the muscle because we never use it? 5. Commodification. If everyone can generate, what's left? If a client can make their own logo in 30 seconds, why do they need you? I'm not dismissing these. I'm naming them. They're in my left hand too." --- ## SLIDE 7: Transformation Stories — Maya + Kevin [4 min] "Now let me show you what's in the right hand. Maya Bruck. UX designer. Joined our program as a 'strategic skeptic.' Seasoned professional who'd heard the hype but wanted real workflow value. She mastered Figma Make—compressed weeks of design into hours. Built a complete marketing dashboard from a single client call recording. She told me: 'I can do on a one-hour phone call what used to take two weeks.' By the end of the program, she became an instructor. She didn't just learn—she became good enough to teach. Kevin Friel. 25-year Hollywood VFX veteran. Dune, Detective Pikachu, Sonic, Bullet Train. Worked at MPC, DNEG—the big studios. Now he's teaching indie creators to achieve blockbuster-quality production with AI tools. The $25K VFX setup from ten years ago? Now it's a laptop. Kevin said to me: 'There's something very punk rock about taking a tool that's staring you in the face and using it to tell the stories that matter.' These aren't tech bros. They're creatives like you. Animators, VFX artists, game designers, filmmakers. They went to schools like this one." --- ## SLIDE 8: Personal Testimony [3 min] "Here's my confession. 'I have never felt as creative or productive or powerful as when I've started messing with this shit. It's helped me smash through technical ceilings I had not been able to break through before.' Let me show you what that looks like. I trained a model on 2,000 of my street portraits—20 years of cross-process slide film work. Not to replace photographers. To explore what my body of work actually means. I dropped Canada's 485-page federal budget into a vibe coding tool. 'I want to click around and understand this thing.' Charts, diagrams, filters. Done in hours. I record every meeting, transcribe it, commit it to a knowledge base, and deploy AI on top of that. My past self becomes my future asset. I vibe code something almost every day. It's only 9:40 today, and I already built a web app. In the bathtub. Building is how you discover. What you think you want to build isn't what you actually need." --- ## SLIDE 8b: AutoLoom Video [1-2 min] [Let the video play] "These are my portraits. 2,000 images from the last 20 years. Cross-process slide film. This is what I fed into the model. This is AutoLoom—built by Philippe Pasquier's Metacreation Lab at SFU. A tool designed specifically for artists to train on their own work." --- ## SLIDE 8c: AutoLoom Interface [30 sec] "This is the interface. GAN-based visual synthesizer. No coding required. You feed it your images. It learns your aesthetic. What comes out isn't theft—it's exploration. Understanding what my body of work actually means." --- ## SLIDE 8d: AutoLoom Faces [30 sec] "Real portraits on the left. AI-generated tr

docs.body: # Both Hands Full — LaSalle College Vancouver Talk Event: LaSalle Alumni Event — "How are creatives working with AI?" Date: January 14, 2026 @ 6 PM Duration: 45 min presentation + Q&A + networking Audience: 100 LaSalle alumni (animation, game design, graphic design, fashion, interior design, recording arts) Slides: 27 total --- ## Timing Checkpoints | Slide | Mark | Section | |-------|------|---------| | 4 | 5 min | Thesis delivered | | 6 | 10 min | Fears complete | | 14 | 22 min | Right Hand evidence complete | | 18 | 30 min | Principles complete | | 22 | 40 min | Creative DNA reflection | | 27 | 45 min | Q&A begins | --- ## Core Thesis "AI is trained on stolen work without consent. And I'm more creative, powerful, and productive than I've ever been because of these tools. Both things are true. We walk forward holding both." The deeper insight: Self-knowledge is the prerequisite to AI mastery. --- ## Slide-by-Slide Structure ### ACT 1: THE PROBLEM (Slides 1-6) | # | Slide | Content | |---|-------|---------| | 1 | Cover | "Both Hands Full" | | 2 | About Me | Quick credibility | | 3 | Binary Trap | Boosters vs Doomers — "Both are lazy" | | 4 | Thesis | "Both things are true" | | 5 | Both Hands | Visual framework — Left Hand / Right Hand | | 6 | The Fears | Five fears: stolen work, race to bottom, junior pipeline, cognitive dependency, commodification | ### ACT 2: THE EVIDENCE — Your Examples (Slides 7-14) | # | Slide | Content | |---|-------|---------| | 7 | Personal Testimony | "I've never felt as creative..." — your experiences | | 8 | AutoLoom Video | 2,000 cross-process portraits → custom model | | 9 | AutoLoom Interface | The tool itself | | 10 | AutoLoom Faces | Real portraits → AI transformations | | 11 | Alpha Prism | Ars Electronica recognition | | 12 | AI Mindset | Tech skills + Human skills (compressed) | | 13 | Stack | Your tools — Notion, Plaud, Claude Code, etc. | | 14 | Vibe Coding | Prototype-first building — months → hours | ### ACT 3: THE PRINCIPLES (Slides 15-18) | # | Slide | Content | |---|-------|---------| | 15 | Four Principles | Overview of all four | | 16 | RiP Manifesto | Full-bleed image — "Has anyone seen this?" | | 17 | Selector | Principle 3 deep dive + selector skills | | 18 | Write for the Bot | Principle 4 + homework assignment | ### ACT 4: THE INSIGHT (Slides 19-22) | # | Slide | Content | |---|-------|---------| | 19 | The Real Question | "What does AI reveal about who you are?" | | 20 | AI is a Mirror | Core insight — self-knowledge prerequisite | | 21 | What AI Can't Eat | Stubbornly human inventory | | 22 | Creative DNA | Reflection moment — "What would you never give up?" | ### ACT 5: CTAs + CLOSE (Slides 23-27) | # | Slide | Content | |---|-------|---------| | 23 | Ethos Lab | Black/Indigenous youth program | | 24 | Vancouver AI | Meetup invite — Jan 28th | | 25 | BC + AI | Ecosystem Association QR | | 26 | AI Upgrade | Course offer + LaSalle rate | | 27 | Q&A | Resources + "Write 3 sentences tonight" | --- ## Four Principles 1. Use the tools while acknowledging the problems — Critical AND engaged. Keep a kill switch. 2. Train AI on your own work — You own the model. You own the output. 3. Become a selector — Generation is cheap. Taste is not. 4. Write for the bot — If your values aren't in text, to AI they don't exist. --- ## Key Quotes "AI is trained on stolen work without consent. And I'm more creative, powerful, and productive than I've ever been because of these tools. Both things are true." "I have never felt as creative or productive or powerful as when I've started messing with this shit." "DJs were originally called 'selectors' — that's our future role." "The AI can't eat ice cream for you — only you can determine your preferences." "You're the original intelligence, AI is the amplifier." "Self-knowledge is the prerequisite to AI mastery." "I vibe code something almost every day. It's only 9:40 today, and I already built a web app. In the bathtub." --- ## Q&A Prep Common questions: - "Is my degree worthless?" — No, but augment it. Degree = taste, judgment, process. AI = execution. - "What about copyright?" — Real problem. Train on your own work. Advocate for policy. - "How do I start?" — Pick one tool, one project, one week. Ship it ugly. - "Will clients devalue my work?" — They care about quality and timeline, not method. 3 Rules for Starting: - Map before you automate - 10x or skip - You decide, AI assists --- ## Resources - BC + AI Ecosystem Association — bc-ai.ca - Vancouver AI Meetup — lu.ma/vancouver-ai - AI Upgrade — theupgrade.ai - Metacreation Lab at SFU — Philippe Pasquier - kriskrug.co --- #industry:creative #industry:education #business:dev:partnerships #org:partner:lasalle-college-vancouver

And that is dangerously close to won't exist." - Size: 60MB - Used in: Slide 19 ### dwarkesh-patel-gorn-followup.mov - Source: Dwarkesh Patel podcast (continued) - Topic: Follow-up discussion on AI and human values — "The AI can't eat ice cream for you" - Size: 120MB - Used in: Slide 24 ### autoloom-kk-portraits.mov - Source: Kris Krüg / SFU Metacreation Lab (Philippe Pasquier) - Tool: AutoLoom — GAN-based neurovisual synthesizer - Content: 2,000 cross-processed street portraits transformed by AI model trained on Kris's photography - Context: Part of Alpha Prism project, awarded Honorable Mention at Ars Electronica Festival (September 2025) - Size: 29MB - Used in: Slide 8 ### rip-remix-manifesto-trailer.mp4 - Source: YouTube — documentary trailer - Topic: Copyright, remix culture, creative commons, Girl Talk - Size: 9MB - Used in: Opening/context slides --- ## Reuse Guidelines These clips are cleared for reuse in Kris Krüg's presentations on: - AI and creative practice - Writing for the bot / knowledge base development - Ethical AI training on personal work - Remix culture and copyright --- #asset:video #keynote:lasalle #topic:ai #topic:creative-practice

docs.body: --- type: youtube-seo-package sourceevent: "Both Hands Full: How Creatives Are Actually Working with AI" venue: LaSalle College Vancouver date: 2026-01-14 speaker: Kris Krüg duration: 75 min (45 min talk + 30 min Q&A) tags: [youtube, seo, keynote, AI, creative-professionals] --- # Both Hands Full — YouTube SEO Package LaSalle College Vancouver Alumni Keynote — January 14, 2026 --- ## 🎬 Video Title Options ### Primary Title (62 characters): Both Hands Full: What Creatives Actually Need to Know About AI ### Alternative Titles: - AI Stole My Work—And I've Never Been More Creative | Keynote (57 chars) - The Framework for Creatives Navigating AI in 2026 | Full Talk (62 chars) - How Creative Professionals Are Actually Using AI | Kris Krüg (60 chars) - AI for Creatives: Hold the Critique AND the Curiosity (54 chars) - What No One Tells Creative Professionals About AI (50 chars) --- ## 📝 YouTube Description "AI is trained on stolen work without consent. And I've never been more creative, productive, or powerful." Both things are true—here's how to navigate that contradiction. 🎯 WHAT YOU'LL LEARN: - Why both the AI hype AND the AI doom takes are lazy - The "both hands full" framework for creative professionals - Real examples: designers compressing weeks into hours, VFX artists building new tools - How to train AI on your own work (and why it matters) - The difference between generation (cheap) and selection (valuable) - Why self-knowledge is the prerequisite to AI mastery - Practical homework: building your style guide, worldview doc, and creative DNA 🎤 ABOUT THIS TALK: Kris Krüg delivers a keynote to 100 LaSalle College Vancouver alumni (animation, game design, graphic design, fashion, interior design, recording arts). This isn't another "AI is amazing" or "AI will destroy us" talk—it's a practical framework for holding critique and curiosity simultaneously while building creative work that matters. Kris shares his journey from finding 1,800 of his photographs in AI training datasets to winning an Ars Electronica honorable mention for research training models on his own portrait photography. Along the way: live audience Q&A on everything from classroom assessment to VFX pipeline overwhelm to water consumption concerns. 🛠️ TOOLS MENTIONED: - Notion (second brain + AI access to Claude, GPT, Gemini, Grok) - Plaud (wearable recorder for transcription) - NotebookLM (free Google tool for knowledge synthesis) - Perplexity (research tool—free year with PayPal account) - Claude Code / Claude Co-work (agentic coding and knowledge management) - MidJourney (imagination platform) - 11Labs (voice cloning for rehearsal and content) - Lovable (vibe coding tool) - AutoLoom (neural visual synthesizer from SFU) 📚 RESOURCES FROM THE TALK: - HaveIBeenTrained.com — Check if your work is in AI training data - TheUpgrade.ai — AI training for creative professionals (use code LASALLE for discount) - BC + AI Ecosystem Association — bc-ai.ca - Vancouver AI Meetup — lu.ma/vancouver-ai --- CHAPTERS: 0:00 — Creative Reality Check 2:30 — Boosters vs Doomers 4:04 — "Both Hands Full" 4:34 — Stolen Work & Fears 10:51 — Training AI on Myself 13:50 — AutoLoom & Ars Electronica 18:20 — Building a Second Brain 26:10 — Vibe Coding & Prototyping 31:13 — Write for the Bot 40:48 — Your Creative DNA 45:02 — Q&A: Education & Skeptics 54:30 — Q&A: AI & Environment 1:04:37 — Q&A: Automation & Verification --- SPEAKER: Kris Krüg — Executive Director of BC + AI Ecosystem Association, founder of TheUpgrade.ai, host of CBC's Sandboxing AI. Has trained 200+ creative professionals through seven cohorts of AI workflow transformation. His photography research won an honorable mention at Ars Electronica 2025. docs.title: Life, Love & the Internet
docs.summary: The Kris Krug Biography: KK discusses his extensive career and personal experiences, including his work in AI, photography, journalism, and community building. He shares details about various projects, collaborations, an
docs.body: # Life, Love & the Internet --- The Kris Krug Biography: KK discusses his extensive career and personal experiences, including his work in AI, photography, journalism, and community building. He shares details about various projects, collaborations, and significant life events. --- ### Outline - Introduction (5 pages) - Opening Scene: A compelling narrative of a pivotal moment where the convergence of life, love, and technology shapes your path (e.g., a life-changing decision during a Future Proof Creatives event or a transformative experience on Galiano Island). - Thesis Statement: Introduction to Kris Krug as a digital pioneer, an artist at the intersection of technology and human experience, and a seeker of meaning in a hyperconnected world. - Overview of Themes: Exploration of the central themes of life, love, and the internet, and how these elements have intertwined throughout your journey. - Part 1: The Roots of a Digital Romantic - Chapter 1: Childhood in Harrisburg - A World of Possibilities - Family and Early Influences: A deep dive into your upbringing, the values instilled by your parents, and the entrepreneurial spirit that took root in your small-town environment. - First Ventures: Stories about your childhood businesses, like the wasp nest venture and selling golf balls, with a focus on how these early experiences foreshadowed your future pursuits in technology and creativity. - Chapter 2: The Seeds of Love and Technology - Formative Relationships: Reflections on early friendships and romantic relationships that shaped your understanding of love and connection. - Introduction to Technology: Your first encounters with technology, how it fascinated you, and how it began to influence your worldview during your time at Trinity Western University. - Part 2: Love and Ambition in Silicon Valley - The Birth of spark-online and Sierra’s Arrival: Just as your daughter Sierra was born, you co-founded spark-online with Robert Delamar in 1998. This period marked a profound intersection of personal and professional life, where the early days of digital publishing ignited your passion for exploring "electronic consciousness" and reshaping digital media. - The Move to Silicon Valley: Building on the momentum from spark-online, you and Robert Delamar took a leap into the heart of innovation in Silicon Valley. At the Accel Partners-funded startup iVMG, you dove into the fast-paced tech world, navigating the challenges and opportunities that came with being at the forefront of the digital revolution. - Balancing Work and Relationships: Amid the whirlwind of startups and the cultural shift of Silicon Valley, you faced the delicate act of maintaining meaningful personal connections while chasing ambitious professional dreams. ### Chapter 4: Bryght, Raincity Studios, and the Rise of Open Source (15 pages) - Founding Bryght: The passion behind creating Bryght with Boris Mann, the excitement of the open-source movement, and how love for collaboration and innovation drove you forward. - Cultural Exchange in China: Your experiences hosting BarCamp in China, the cultural impact, and how these experiences broadened your understanding of global connections. - Transition to Raincity Studios: The acquisition by Raincity Studios and how this period of your life was marked by growth in both your professional and personal life, as well as your global footprint. - Part 3: A Love Affair with Photography and the World - Chapter 5: Chapter 5: Pixels, Protests, and the Power of Digital Disruption - Photography as a Medium of Connection: How you used photography to explore and document the world, capturing both the beauty and the struggles of human life. - Iconic Projects: Highlight key projects, such as the NatGeo, Barack, Bill Clinton, Mikhail Gorbachev, Albatross Midway Journey, and how these experiences deepened your connection to the world and your understanding of love, loss, and environmental impact. - Chapter 6: The Tri-Coastal Lifestyle and Personal Evolution - Living Between Worlds: The challenges and joys of balancing life between Vancouver, Galiano Island, and global travel. How these experiences shaped your understanding of home, community, and love. - Personal Relationships: Reflection on how your lifestyle influenced your relationships and the ways in which love and technology intersected in your personal life during this period. - Part 4: Reinvention and the Rise of AI - Chapter 7: The Pandemic and the Search for Meaning - Isolation on Galiano Island: How the pandemic prompted a period of introspection, where you explored the intersections of love, life, and technology in a more profound way. - Rediscovering Technology: Your return to the tech world, driven by a newfound passion for AI, and how this reinvention was both a professional and personal renaissance. - Chapter 8: Building Future Proof Creatives - Founding Future Proof Creatives: The vision behind creating a community that blends creativity with technology, and how love for innovation and people fueled this endeavor. - Community Building: The importance of building a community where love, respect, and collaboration are the foundation. How your workshops, meetups, and events reflect this philosophy. - The Role of AI: Your thoughts on AI as both a tool for creative expression and a challenge to the traditional notions of human connection and love in the digital age. - Part 5: Love, Legacy, and the Future - Chapter 9: The Crux of Connection – Meetups, Future Proof Creatives, and Ethical AI in Analog Photography Introduction: The Spark of a New Beginning • Begin with the inception of the Vancouver AI Community Meetups, leading to the creation of Future Proof Creatives, and introduce the idea of integrating AI with analog photography as a natural progression in your exploration of technology and art. --- The Vancouver AI Community Meetups: A Movement Grows • Detail the evolution of the meetups, emphasizing the importance of authentic community

headings.text: INTRODUCTION (by Louise Heartland, CTV Vancouver Island)
SILICON VALLEY AND THE DARK TIME

docs.body: # Life, Love, and the Internet — Original Talk Transcript ## Social Media Camp, Victoria BC · 2012 ## Speaker: Kris Krüg Source: YouTube auto-transcript, cleaned of timestamps and transcription artifacts. Original talk title was "Action Is the Antidote to Despair: Empowerment Through Art, Activism and Technology" — Kris renamed it "Life, Love, and the Internet" the week before. Note: Kris received the Derek K. Miller Award at this event. --- ## INTRODUCTION (by Louise Heartland, CTV Vancouver Island) My name is Louise Heartland and I'm a reporter at CTV Vancouver Island. I've been covering Social Media Camp all week and I've learned so much — but I think one of the most important lessons I've taken away is the power of social media and the empowerment social media gives every person the power to use their voice in any way they choose. And the most powerful way of course is to influence others and try and promote change. And from talking to our guest speaker today — that is exactly what Chris Krüg is doing. He was recognized last night for his work with the Derek K. Miller Award. Please, without any further ado, welcome Chris Krüg. --- ## KRIS'S TALK Thanks for coming. I get nervous when I speak in public, so these notes are a crutch and I'm going to try not to use them too much. You may have noticed the talk had a bit of a confusing title — it was called something like "Action Is the Antidote to Despair: Empowerment Through Art and Activism and Technology." But I kind of scratched that last week. So this talk's now called "Life, Love, and the Internet." When I talk at things like this I usually talk about technical stuff — how to use social media, a campaign I've done, photography. I very rarely talk really much about myself or who I am or where I've come from and why I have this perspective that in some ways stands in opposition to other people. So I hope to share a little bit about my personal story today — hopefully that meets you where you're at and doesn't just sound like me talking about myself too much. --- ## WHERE I CAME FROM I'm American. I grew up in California. I grew up in a Christian home — my mom was a schoolteacher and my dad was a bureaucrat. Intellectualism, creativity, asking questions — they weren't highly valued in my upbringing. But my dad was a super geek, so we always had technology around. My dad brought home an Apple 2e in the 80s with a 600 baud modem. So from like 1986 I was online — bulletin boards, hacking into other systems. Technology has definitely been a part of my DNA. I realized this week as I was preparing this talk that I've been online for like 30 years. I moved to Canada at 17 — in search of powder and mountains, went to university at Trinity Western in 1995. The internet is just taking off. First graphical web browser had just been released. I had a strong technical background. I was able to slide right into the demand: every professor on campus needed someone to build them a website. So I arrived in Canada and from the moment I got here, I hit the ground running with technology. I was building websites for professors. I built the first website for my university. It opened up a lot of doors. --- ## SPARK ONLINE — 1998 In 1998, after I'd been doing this for a couple years — this is ancient times on the internet, 14-15 years ago — me and five buddies started an online magazine called Spark Online. The tagline was: "Exploring electronic consciousness." This magazine was formative for me, and maybe even for parts of the internet, in some ways. We were running an online community way before this kind of stuff existed. There were no content management systems available for free like WordPress. There was no idea of blog posts and multiple authors and comments and a discussion thing going on. We duct-taped together hand-crafted HTML pages with a bulletin board system tagged onto the bottom of each post. We had a net art gallery showcasing people experimenting with new digital mediums. We were talking philosophy — what does it mean to have a digital self? How is the internet changing perceptions of friendship and geography? For me and the guys and girls who ran that project — it got us off on the right foot. We got to know internet people. San Francisco people. New York people. It blew up and grew and really gave us a great foothold. It ran for three years. It grew pretty big. And it crushed under the weight of its own success — we had to hand-code everything, we were taking old paradigms from the old world like a monthly magazine with sections and articles and applying that to the internet. This was the day when every ecommerce shop on the internet looked like a shopping mall. So we let it go. But I was fully off to the races at this point. --- ## SILICON VALLEY AND THE DARK TIME In 1999 I moved to California. Took a job with a Silicon Valley startup. Started ascending the ranks of corporate marketing. I had a young family — I have a daughter who came with me to the awards last night. I was doing what I was told. Graduated. Had a baby. Had a wife. Had a fat corporate job. Ascending the ranks of marketing at a Silicon Valley startup. I was pretty unhappy. Commuting a couple hours every day. They'd feed you at work so I wouldn't leave my desk. I got over 200 pounds. I was very unhappy. I wasn't in a very good place. I was barely keeping my head above water — just trying to do all the things the world told me I was supposed to do. And at the moment when I was probably at my weakest, tragedy hit my family. We had a son. He was helicoptered to Stanford University's Neonatal Intensive Care Unit as soon as he was born. He spent the next three weeks in there. Many times close to dying. Seizure disorders. Surgeries. A host of problems. So here I am — young dad, young marketing executive, working my ass off, being somewhat successful, barely keeping my head above water, being unsatisfied with the results of my work. And then this. --- ## THE CAMERA SAVED MY LIFE Th
