docs.summary: name: "Mark Busse" slug: "mark-busse" aliases: - "marc-busse" roles: - "Founder/Producer" - "CreativeMornings Vancouver Co-Host" - "Likemind Vancouver Organizer" - "Community Builder" locations: - "Vancouver, British Col
docs.body: --- name: "Mark Busse" slug: "mark-busse" aliases: - "marc-busse" roles: - "Founder/Producer" - "CreativeMornings Vancouver Co-Host" - "Likemind Vancouver Organizer" - "Community Builder" locations: - "Vancouver, British Columbia" programs: - "CreativeMornings/Vancouver" - "Likemind Vancouver" tags: - "community" - "leadership" - "community-building" - "creative-mornings" - "creativity" - "inclusion" - "arts" firstseen: "2025-08-27" lastupdated: "2026-02-15" status: "active" email: mark@markbusse.ca --- # Mark Busse – CreativeMornings Vancouver Co-Host, Likemind Organizer, Community Builder ## Summary Mark Busse is a Vancouver community builder who co-hosts CreativeMornings Vancouver - now in its 15th year as Canada's first chapter of the global network spanning 255 cities in 70 countries with 2 million subscribers. Organizational leader, strategic advisor, storyteller, engagement specialist, certified leadership coach, & licensed officiant. At the Squatchie Awards (December 2025), Mark announced a historic milestone: on January 9, 2026, CreativeMornings Vancouver will become the first chapter globally to host an award-winning artist, filmmaker, actor, and director with Down syndrome, demonstrating the program's commitment to accessibility and inclusive creativity. Every month, CreativeMornings provides free breakfast, free music, and access to creative practitioners sharing their "relationship, their journey, their struggles with creativity" at the Vancouver Art Gallery. ## Key Roles & Affiliations - Co-Host – CreativeMornings Vancouver (15 years, Canada's first chapter) - Founder/Producer – CreativeMornings/Vancouver - Organizer – Likemind Vancouver (community meetups) - Community Builder – Vancouver creative community leadership - Inclusion Advocate – First CreativeMornings chapter globally hosting artist with Down syndrome ## Contact & Links - Email: mark@markbusse.ca - Phone: (604) 817-4388 - LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/markbusse - Website: https://www.markbusse.ca - Location: Vancouver, British Columbia ## CreativeMornings Vancouver ### Program Overview Format: Monthly breakfast gathering with creative speaker Components: - Free breakfast - Free music - Speaker presentation on creativity - Community connection Location: Vancouver Art Gallery Theme: Exploring relationship with creativity "Access to someone... who will share their relationship, their journey, their struggles with creativity" ### Global Network Scale: - 255 cities worldwide - 70 countries represented - 2 million subscribers globally - Vancouver: Canada's first chapter (15 years) Significance: - Largest global creative community network - Democratized access to creative inspiration - Monthly rhythm building creative practice culture - Free and accessible to all ### Historic Milestone: January 9, 2026 Announcement at Squatchie Awards: "On January 9th, we are the first chapter - we were the first chapter in Canada - we're now in 255 cities in 70 countries around the world, 2 million subscribers. All that being said: on January 9th, we are the first chapter on the planet who's hosting an award-winning artist, filmmaker, actor, director with Down syndrome" Significance: - First CreativeMornings chapter globally to host artist with Down syndrome - Advancing accessibility and inclusion in creative communities - Vancouver leading global network on representation - Demonstrating creativity transcends all abilities Kris Krüg's response: "Oh wow. Thanks, Marc" Source: See ## Community Involvement ### Squatchie Awards 2025 - Community Announcement (December 2025) Context: Community announcements section Kris Krüg's invitation: "Marc - 30 seconds on your community" Mark's announcement: - 15th year of CreativeMornings Vancouver - Monthly format: free breakfast, music, speaker - Global scale: 255 cities, 70 countries, 2 million subscribers - Historic first: Artist with Down syndrome (January 9, 2026) Impact: - Cross-pollination between AI community and creative community - Accessibility and inclusion values alignment - Community-building model sharing - Creative practice inspiration available to all Source: See ### Likemind Vancouver Role: Organizer Format: Community meetups for creative professionals Philosophy: Informal connection and collaboration Relationship to CreativeMornings: Complementary community-building efforts ## Philosophy & Vision ### Free and Accessible Creativity Core values: - No cost barriers to creative inspiration - Monthly rhythm builds creative practice - Community over commerce - Breakfast + music + ideas = creative fuel ### Inclusive Creative Communities Demonstrated through: - 15 years of consistent programming - First chapter to host artist with Down syndrome - Vancouver Art Gallery partnership (public space) - Global network participation (255 cities) ### Journey Over Credentials Focus on: "Their relationship, their journey, their struggles with creativity" Not focused on: - Credentials or accolades alone - Finished products only - Exclusionary definitions of "creative" - Gatekeeping access to inspiration ## Key Contributions to Ecosystem ### Cross-Community Bridge Impact: - Creative community connected to AI community - Shared values: accessibility, inclusion, innovation - Model for sustainable community building (15 years) - Global network + local presence ### Accessibility Leadership Significance: - First globally to host artist with Down syndrome - Vancouver leading on inclusion - Demonstrating creativity transcends abilities - Setting precedent for 255 other chapters ### Consistent Community Infrastructure 15-year track record: - Monthly programming without interruption - Free breakfast + music + speaker model - Sustainable community practice - Volunteer-driven with corporate support ## Mentioned In ### 2025 - Vancouver Ai Meetup 20 Transcript (August 2025) - Thank you brother. Uh, VHD. Thanks guys for buying like the 10 pack right out the gate of tickets,... - Vancouver Ai Meetup 17 Trans

docs.title: The Moment Didn't Ask Permission: Agency Within Inevitability
docs.summary: One day you're building a career through craft. Mastering your tools. Earning your expertise. Playing the long game.

headings.text: What Agency Looks Like Within Inevitability

docs.body: # The Moment Didn't Ask Permission: Agency Within Inevitability One day you're building a career through craft. Mastering your tools. Earning your expertise. Playing the long game. The next day, someone's selling your style for $20/month. Your writing. Your art. Your code. Your design sensibility. Packaged. Productized. Available to anyone. You didn't choose this. Nobody asked: - "Can we train AI on your life's work?" - "Can we commodify your expertise?" - "Can we fundamentally alter your industry?" It just... happened. While you were paying your bills, raising your kids, building your career, the world changed underneath you. This is what I mean by "the moment didn't ask permission." For creatives, this is visceral. 10 years mastering Photoshop → Midjourney replicates your style in 30 seconds. 20 years developing your writing voice → ChatGPT approximates it on demand. Career built on scarcity of skill → Skill becomes commodity. You're pissed. Rightfully. And. The moment is still here. The question: How do you navigate a moment you didn't choose and can't undo? The trap: People want you to pick. Accept it (complicity) or resist it (irrelevance). I'm saying there's a third option: Engage with agency while acknowledging inevitability. This article is about that navigation. --- ## What Inevitability Means The things you can't change: AI Exists You can protest. You can resist. You can refuse. Won't change: AI still exists. Companies still use it. Clients still expect it. Training Data Includes Your Work You can file lawsuits (some are). You can opt out going forward (some have). Won't change: Data already scraped. Models already trained. Outputs already in market. Industries Are Shifting You can try to preserve old models. Won't change: Junior roles disappearing. Client expectations evolving. Markets rewarding AI-augmented workers over traditional. The Timeline Is Measured in Months, Not Years This isn't slow-burn disruption. In 18 months: - Productivity tools became standard - AI-fluent workers started outperforming - Companies began restructuring around AI capability You can't slow this down personally. The anger is valid. I'm pissed too. The non-consent is real. The theft is real. The disruption is real. And. Being pissed doesn't change the timeline. The choice: Option 1: Opt out. Maintain purity. Refuse to engage. Result: Governance and norms get set by uncritical adopters. You have no voice in how it unfolds. Option 2: Opt in. Engage critically. Build alternative models while using the tools. Result: You have agency in shaping what happens next. The uncomfortable truth: If all the thoughtful, ethical, critical people opt out—the only voices in the room are true believers and opportunists. That's worse than engaging with critique intact. I'd rather be in the room with both hands full than outside the room maintaining purity while decisions get made without me. --- ## What Agency Looks Like Within Inevitability The actions you can take even when the big picture is inevitable: 1. Choose How You Engage Emily's choice: Can't stop kids from using AI (inevitability). Can choose to teach them about AI compassionately (agency). Result: Bee School. Environmental education plus AI literacy. Kids learn how to use AI and why consent matters and what data sovereignty means. Not acceptance. Active shaping. She's not saying "AI is fine." She's saying "AI is here, and I'm going to make sure kids understand what they're using." 2. Build Alternative Models My choice: Can't stop AI training on stolen work (already happened). Can build alternatives. Metacreation Lab—train models on consented data. Result: Proof that other models exist. Presented at Ars Electronica. Showed it's possible to engage with AI without participating in extraction. Not boycott. Demonstration of alternative. 2,000 cross-processed portraits. All my own photos. Trained a model on my own work. Nobody else's data stolen. The result isn't as good as models trained on billions of images. But it's mine. And it proves a point: You can do this differently. 3. Set Boundaries Around What You Automate The exercise I give students: "List 5 things you'll never automate, even if AI could do them." Examples: - Thank you notes (relationship building requires genuine emotion) - Apologies (requires authentic remorse) - Creative decisions (taste is your job) - Hiring decisions (humans deserve human judgment) - Ethical calls (can't delegate values) The effect: You're not resisting AI broadly. You're creating protocol around where it belongs and doesn't. That's agency. You're drawing lines. Saying: "AI can help here. But not here. This stays human." 4. Teach Others To Engage Critically My choice: Every cohort, Week 1: I show the Five Fears. I don't hide the problems. I name them. Stolen work. Collapsing pipelines. Cognitive dependency. Commodification. All of it. Then I show transformations alongside critique. Result: Students engage with eyes open. Not naive adoption. Not blind resistance. Critical engagement. They see the problems and the possibilities. They make informed choices. That's what I want. People who understand what they're doing and why. 5. Advocate for Change While Using Tools The both/and: Use AI tools (because clients expect it, because it creates capability). Advocate for regulation (because consent matters, because artists deserve compensation). These aren't contradictory. You can benefit from current system and push for better system. Caroline's example (Endinomics): Uses AI for Indigenous economic analysis (right now, with current tools). Simultaneously: Builds data sovereignty frameworks so future Indigenous projects own their data. Not waiting for perfect system. Engaging with current system while building better one. The meta-pattern: Agency within inevitability equals strategic engagement. Not acceptance. Not resistance. Active participation in shaping what comes next. --- ## The Governance Question The pattern when new technology

docs.title: The Moment Didn't Ask Permission: Agency Within Inevitability
docs.summary: One day you're building a career through craft.
docs.body: # The Moment Didn't Ask Permission: Agency Within Inevitability One day you're building a career through craft. You're learning the tools. Getting good at them. Not overnight—over years. The slow accumulation of skill that comes from showing up every day and doing the work. You're mastering Photoshop or After Effects or your writing voice or your design eye. Building something that feels solid. Something that's yours. Expertise that took time and sweat and failure and persistence. You're earning your expertise the way everyone always has. Through repetition. Through getting it wrong until you get it right. Through the thousand hours and the ten thousand hours and all the hours after that where you're not counting anymore, you're just doing the work because this is what you do. This is who you are. Next day, someone's selling your style for $20 a month. Not metaphorically. Literally. Subscribe to "draw like you" as a service. Your voice. Your aesthetic. Your hard-won way of seeing. Packaged and productized and available to anyone with a credit card and an internet connection. You didn't choose this. You weren't asked. Nobody sent you an email saying "Hey, we're training AI on everything you've ever made—you good with that?" Nobody held a meeting to discuss whether this fundamental alteration of your industry was something you wanted to participate in. It just happened. While you were paying your bills and raising your kids and doing your work, while you were being a professional and showing up and building your career one careful brick at a time, the world changed underneath you. That's what I mean when I say the moment didn't ask permission. This wasn't a choice you made. This wasn't a disruption you saw coming and prepared for. This was non-consensual. That word makes people uncomfortable. Good. It should. Because that's what this is. Ten years mastering Photoshop, and Midjourney replicates your style in 30 seconds. Twenty years developing your writing voice, and ChatGPT approximates it on demand. A career built on the scarcity of skill, and suddenly skill is commodity. Subscribe for $20 a month. Cancel anytime. You're pissed. Of course you're pissed. Anyone who isn't pissed isn't paying attention. The theft is real. The violation is real. The disruption is real. Your work is in those training sets. Your photos, your words, your code, your designs. They took it without asking. They used it without compensating. They deployed it without consent. And the moment is still here. The AI exists. The training happened. The markets shifted. You can be as pissed as you want—and you should be, the anger is righteous—but the anger doesn't change the timeline. The moment is here and it's not asking permission to stay. So the question becomes: How do you navigate a moment you didn't choose and can't undo? Most people want you to pick a side. They want this to be simple. Accept it or resist it. Adopt or refuse. Complicity or purity. Choose your team. All in or all out. The Boosters say embrace it fully, stop whining, get on board or get left behind. The Doomers say resist completely, don't be complicit, burn it all down. Both camps are offering you the same thing underneath the rhetoric: a clean narrative. A simple position. A way to feel certain in an uncertain moment. And I'm saying there's a third option that nobody wants to talk about because it's uncomfortable and messy and doesn't fit neatly into anyone's ideological box. Engage with agency while acknowledging inevitability. Let me show you what I mean by inevitability. These are the things you can't change. The things that are true whether you accept them or not. AI exists. You can protest. You can resist. You can refuse to use it. Won't change the fact that AI still exists. Companies are still using it. Clients are still expecting it. Your competitors are still adopting it. The technology is out there in the world and it's not going back in the box no matter how much you wish it would. Training data includes your work. You can file lawsuits—some people are. You can opt out going forward—some platforms allow it now. Won't change what already happened. Data already scraped. Models already trained. Outputs already in market. The theft already occurred. The violation is in the past. You can seek remedies, you can demand compensation, you can fight for better systems going forward. But you can't unscramble this egg. Industries are shifting. You can try to preserve old models. You can advocate for regulations that protect traditional workflows. And you should—we need those protections. But while you're advocating, junior roles are disappearing. Client expectations are evolving. Markets are rewarding AI-augmented workers over traditional approaches. The shift is happening whether you participate or not. The timeline is measured in months, not years. This isn't slow-burn disruption you have decades to adapt to. In 18 months, productivity tools became standard. AI-fluent workers started outperforming. Companies began restructuring around AI capability. You can't personally slow this down. You can't opt out of living in this timeline. The anger about all of this is valid. I'm pissed too. The non-consent is real. The theft is real. The disruption is real. Being pissed doesn't change the inevitability. You can be furious about the moment and still have to navigate it. So what does agency look like inside inevitability? What can you actually control when the big picture is out of your hands? You can choose how you engage. Emily teaches elementary school. She can't stop kids from using AI. That's the inevitability. They're using it at home. They're using it on their phones. They're going to encounter it whether she likes it or not. But she can choose to teach them about it compassionately. That's agency. So she built Bee School. Environmental education plus AI literacy. Kids learn how to use AI tools and why consent matters and what data sovereignty means.

Refuse to engage. Result: Governance and norms get set by uncritical adopters. You have no voice in how it unfolds. Option 2: Opt in. Engage critically. Build alternative models while using the tools. Result: You have agency in shaping what happens next. The Uncomfortable Truth: If all the thoughtful, ethical, critical people opt out—the only voices in the room are true believers and opportunists. That's worse than engaging with critique intact. Evidence to Pull: - Timeline of AI adoption (18-month disruption data) - Legal battles status (ongoing, not stopping development) - Industry restructuring examples - Opt-out consequences (governance by true believers) - Both/and philosophy necessity --- ## Section 2: What Agency Looks Like Within Inevitability (500 words) The Actions You CAN Take: ### 1. Choose HOW You Engage Emily's Choice: Can't stop kids from using AI (inevitability). CAN choose to teach them ABOUT AI compassionately (agency). Result: Bee School. Set Boundaries Around What You Automate The Exercise I Give Students: "List 5 things you'll NEVER automate, even if AI could do them." Examples: - Thank you notes (relationship building) - Apologies (requires genuine emotion) - Creative decisions (taste is your job) - Hiring decisions (humans deserve human judgment) - Ethical calls (can't delegate values) The Effect: You're not resisting AI broadly. You're creating PROTOCOL around where it belongs and doesn't. That's agency. ### 4. Teach Others To Engage Critically Kris's Choice: Every cohort, Week 1: I show the Five Fears. I don't hide the problems. I NAME them. Then: I show transformations alongside critique. Result: Students engage with eyes open, not naive adoption. Critical engagement > Uncritical adoption or total resistance. ### 5. Advocate for Change While Using Tools The Both/And: - Use AI tools (because clients expect it, because it creates capability) - Advocate for regulation (because consent matters, because artists deserve compensation) These aren't contradictory. You can benefit from current system AND push for better system. Caroline (Endinomics): Uses AI for Indigenous economic analysis (right now, with current tools). Simultaneously: Builds data sovereignty frameworks so future Indigenous projects own their data. Not waiting for perfect system. Engaging with current system while building better one. docs.title: The Mycorrhizal Network Model: Why We're Building Horizontal Distribution, Not Vertical Scale
docs.summary: Beneath every forest: Mycorrhizal networks.

headings.text: The Silicon Valley Model (What I'm Not Building)
The Mycorrhizal Principles Applied to Community
Why Small + Connected Beats Big + Scattered

docs.body: # The Mycorrhizal Network Model: Why We're Building Horizontal Distribution, Not Vertical Scale Beneath every forest: Mycorrhizal networks. Fungi connecting tree roots. Nutrient exchange between unlike species. The pattern: - Nurse logs (fallen trees) feed saplings through fungal network - Birch supports fir during drought - Network communicates: "Predator here" signals travel underground - Diversity creates resilience (monoculture forests collapse) What makes it work: Not same species clustering. But unlike species supporting each other. The exchange: Trees provide sugars (photosynthesis). Fungi provide minerals (soil access). Neither could thrive alone. Together: Forest ecosystem. When I started building BC + AI, everyone asked: "What's your growth target? 1,000 members? 10,000?" My answer confused them: "I don't want 10,000 members who never talk to each other. I want 130 committed people connecting unlike domains. Small but mighty." The mycorrhizal model: Not vertical scale (biggest AI community). But horizontal distribution (most connected AI community). Why: Network value equals connections between nodes, not number of nodes. The math: 1,000 disconnected people equals zero valuable connections. 130 deeply connected people equals exponential unexpected collaborations. James (creator-audience data) meets Zaro (FIFA community gaming): More valuable than 200 random new members. Why: They're solving same problem from different angles. That connection creates innovation nobody predicted. This article is about why we're building mycorrhizal networks, not unicorns. --- ## The Silicon Valley Model (What I'm Not Building) The unicorn framework: Assumptions: - Bigger is better - Winner takes all - Network effects consolidate power - Scale through homogeneity (same user type, same needs) - Value through monopoly The strategy: 1. Raise massive capital 2. Grow as fast as possible 3. Dominate market 4. Extract value through market control Examples: Facebook: Billions of users. Most don't know each other. Value from advertising. LinkedIn: Professional network. Value from premium subscriptions. Twitter/X: Mass broadcasting. Value from attention capture. What they optimize for: User count. Engagement metrics. Ad revenue. What they don't optimize for: Meaningful connections between unlike people. The result: Massive. Powerful. Extractive. Fragile. Why fragile: Dependence on: - Continued VC funding - Advertising revenue - Network effects holding - No real alternative When market shifts or funding dries up: Collapse potential is real. The critique: I'm not against scale. I'm against scale for scale's sake. Growth through extraction. Homogeneous networks where everyone's the same. Power consolidation at top. What I'm building instead: Small. Diverse. Distributed. Resilient. The mycorrhizal model. --- ## The Mycorrhizal Principles Applied to Community Principle 1: Diversity Creates Resilience Forest: Monoculture vulnerable to disease. Diversity survives. Community: Homogeneous network vulnerable to market shifts. Diversity survives. BC + AI members: - Biotech researchers (Simon) - Film creators (Kevin Friel) - Educators (Emily, Antonia) - Indigenous data governance (Chris, Caroline) - Real estate developers - Sales professionals (Shane, Bruce) - Journalists (Katherine) - Lawyers (Lawrence) When AI disrupts one domain, other domains aren't affected. Network survives. The resilience: If we were only biotech, industry collapse equals community collapse. Because we're 8+ domains: One domain's crisis equals other domains' stability. I've watched this play out. When journalism started getting hit hard by AI, the journalists in our community had sales professionals to talk to about content multiplication. Educators to collaborate with on media literacy. Film creators to learn from about visual storytelling. They didn't face the crisis alone. The network caught them. Principle 2: Unlike Species Support Each Other Forest: Birch supports fir. Fir supports cedar. Not competition—collaboration. Community: James (creator tools) supports Zaro (gaming communities). Neither competes. The unexpected connections: Simon (biotech) plus Chris (Indigenous data sovereignty) equals Life Sciences AI subgroup with data governance principles nobody saw coming. Kevin (film) plus Antonia (education) equals Film Club as educational tool. Shane (sales) plus Katherine (journalism) equals content multiplication frameworks applied to newsrooms. Each connection creates value neither saw coming. Office Hours, October 24, 2025: James joins for first time. Describes Appreciation Engine—creator-audience consent-based data platform. Within 5 minutes, I'm saying: "You need to meet Zaro." Zaro: FIFA gaming, community management, same consent-based model for gaming communities. 30 minutes later, they're collaborating on frameworks. Both level up. Potential product integration discussed. That's the pattern. Unlike people solving similar problems from different angles. The collision creates insight. Principle 3: Nutrient Exchange (Not Extraction) Forest: Trees give sugars. Fungi give minerals. Reciprocal. Community: Members give expertise. Receive connections, opportunities, learning. What this looks like: Kevin shares video generation experiments. Others learn. They share their discoveries. Kevin benefits from their insights. Reciprocal flow. Not one-directional extraction. When Kevin's startup wound down, community offered consulting gigs, project collaborations, introductions. He'd been giving for a year. Network reciprocated. That's how healthy networks work. Give before you ask. When you need help, the network remembers. Principle 4: Nurse Logs (Supporting New Growth) Forest: Fallen trees become nurse logs. Feed saplings through fungal network. Community: Experienced members support newcomers. Examples: - Peter mentoring students every cohort - Indigenous scholarships I fund (don't broadcast, just do) - Experienced members running office hours

docs.title: The Mycorrhizal Network Model: Why We're Building Horizontal Distribution, Not Vertical Scale
docs.summary: Beneath every forest, there's a network you can't see.
docs.body: # The Mycorrhizal Network Model: Why We're Building Horizontal Distribution, Not Vertical Scale Beneath every forest, there's a network you can't see. Mycorrhizal fungi. Thread-thin filaments connecting tree roots underground. Spanning acres. Linking unlike species. Creating a web of exchange that makes the visible forest possible. The trees you see above ground are just the obvious part. The real action is happening in the dark, in the soil, in the spaces between. Here's how it works. A fallen tree, a nurse log, slowly decomposing. But it's not dying—it's feeding. Through the mycorrhizal network, that old giant is giving everything it accumulated over a century to the saplings growing from its trunk. Not just to its own offspring. To other species. Birch feeding fir. Fir supporting cedar. Unlike organisms supporting each other through fungal threads too small to see. When drought hits one part of the forest, the network redistributes water. When predators arrive, chemical signals travel through mycelium—"danger here"—and trees across the network respond. Not consciously. Not with intention. Just the elegant logic of a system that survives through connection rather than competition. What makes it work isn't same species clustering together. It's unlike species supporting each other. The birch provides what the fir can't access. The fir provides what the cedar needs. The fungi connect them all, taking a small percentage for themselves but enabling exchange that wouldn't be possible otherwise. Trees provide sugars from photosynthesis. Fungi provide minerals from soil. Neither could thrive alone. Together: forest ecosystem. The diversity is the point. Monoculture forests collapse. One disease, one pest, one drought—the whole system fails because everyone has the same vulnerability. But diverse forests survive. When one species struggles, others compensate. The network holds. When I started building BC + AI Ecosystem, everyone asked the same question. "What's your growth target? 1,000 members? 10,000? How big are you trying to get?" My answer confused them. Still does, sometimes. "I don't want 10,000 members who never talk to each other. I want 130 committed people connecting unlike domains. Small but mighty." The look I get is usually some version of: "But... don't you want to be big? Don't you want to scale? Isn't that the point?" No. That's the Silicon Valley model. That's the vertical scale model. Grow as big as possible as fast as possible and dominate the market. Unicorns. Winner-takes-all. Extract value through monopoly. I understand that game. I'm just not playing it. I'm playing a different game. The mycorrhizal model. Not vertical scale—how big can we get? But horizontal distribution—how connected can we become? Network value doesn't equal number of nodes. It equals quality of connections between nodes. Let me show you the math. Scenario A: 1,000 members, low connection density. Most people don't know each other. Few meaningful collaborations. Value equals individual benefits—access to events, access to content. Total value: 1,000 times individual value. Linear growth. Big number but thin connection. Scenario B: 130 members, high connection density. Most people know each other. Frequent unexpected collaborations. Value equals individual benefits plus connection value plus collaboration value. Total value: 130 times (individual plus network effects). Exponential growth. Smaller number but deep connection. Which is actually more valuable? I've watched the answer emerge in real time. James joins office hours for the first time. October 24, 2025. Describes his project: Appreciation Engine. Creator-audience consent-based data platform. Within five minutes, I'm saying "You need to meet Zaro." Zaro: FIFA gaming community management, same consent-based model for gaming communities. Different domains. Same fundamental architecture. Same ethical framework. Thirty minutes later, they're collaborating. Comparing frameworks. Finding synergies. Discussing potential product integration. Both of them leveling up from a collision that wouldn't have happened in a large, loosely-connected community. They'd have been lost in the noise. Too many people. Too little signal. That one connection created more value than 200 random new members would have. Because they're both doing the work now, informed by each other. Building better because they found each other. That's exponential, not linear. That's mycorrhizal network in action. Unlike species supporting each other. The Silicon Valley model I'm not building looks like this. Assumptions: bigger is better, winner takes all, network effects consolidate power, scale through homogeneity—same user type, same needs. Value through monopoly. The strategy: raise massive capital, grow as fast as possible, dominate market, extract value through market control. Facebook: billions of users. Most don't know each other. Value from advertising. LinkedIn: professional network, value from premium subscriptions. Twitter/X: mass broadcasting, value from attention capture. What they optimize for: user count, engagement metrics, ad revenue. What they don't optimize for: meaningful connections between unlike people. The result is massive, powerful, extractive, fragile. Why fragile? Dependence on continued VC funding. Dependence on advertising revenue. Dependence on network effects holding. Dependence on no real alternative emerging. When market shifts or funding dries up, collapse potential is real. Monoculture vulnerability. I'm not against scale. I'm against scale for scale's sake. Growth through extraction. Homogeneous networks where everyone's the same. Power consolidation at the top. What I'm building instead is small, diverse, distributed, resilient. The mycorrhizal model applied to community. The first principle: diversity creates resilience. In a forest, monoculture is vulnerable to disease. One pest, one blight, the whole forest collapses. Diversity survives. Multiple

docs.body: # The Mycorrhizal Network Model: Why We're Building Horizontal Distribution, Not Vertical Scale Word Target: 2,000 words Opening Style: Nature metaphor that becomes community model Framework: Biological principles applied to community building (interconnection > size) Evidence: Specific cross-domain connections that created unexpected value Closing: Community invitation (join the network, not the crowd) --- ## Opening: What Fungi Taught Me About Community (300 words) The Forest Floor: Beneath every forest: Mycorrhizal networks. Fungi connecting tree roots. Nutrient exchange between unlike species. The Pattern: - Nurse logs (fallen trees) feed saplings through fungal network - Birch supports fir during drought - Network communicates: "Predator here" signals travel underground - Diversity creates resilience (monoculture forests collapse) What Makes It Work: Not: Same species clustering But: UNLIKE species supporting each other The Exchange: Trees: Provide sugars (photosynthesis) Fungi: Provide minerals (soil access) Neither could thrive alone. Together: Forest ecosystem. --- BC + AI Ecosystem: When I started building this, everyone asked: "What's your growth target? 1,000 members? 10,000?" My answer (confused them): "I don't want 10,000 members who never talk to each other. I want 130 committed people connecting unlike domains. Small but mighty." The Mycorrhizal Model: Not: Vertical scale (biggest AI community) But: Horizontal distribution (most connected AI community) Why: Network value = connections between nodes, not number of nodes. The Math: 1,000 disconnected people = 0 valuable connections 130 deeply connected people = exponential unexpected collaborations James (creator-audience data) meets Zaro (FIFA community gaming): More valuable than 200 random new members. Why: They're solving same problem from different angles. That connection creates innovation nobody predicted. This article: Why we're building mycorrhizal networks, not unicorns. --- ## Section 1: The Silicon Valley Model (What I'm Not Building) (400 words) The Unicorn Framework: ### Assumptions: - Bigger is better - Winner takes all - Network effects consolidate power - Scale through homogeneity (same user type, same needs) - Value through monopoly ### The Strategy: 1. Raise massive capital 2. Grow as fast as possible 3. Dominate market 4. Extract value through market control ### Examples: - Facebook: Billions of users, most don't know each other, value from advertising - LinkedIn: Professional network, value from premium subscriptions - Twitter/X: Mass broadcasting, value from attention capture What They Optimize For: User count. Engagement metrics. Ad revenue. What They Don't Optimize For: Meaningful connections between unlike people. The Result: Massive. Powerful. Extractive. Fragile. Why Fragile: Dependence on: - Continued VC funding - Advertising revenue - Network effects holding - No real alternative When market shifts or funding dries up: Collapse. The Critique: I'm not against scale. I'm against: - Scale for scale's sake - Growth through extraction - Homogeneous networks (everyone same) - Power consolidation at top What I'm Building Instead: Small. Diverse. Distributed. Resilient. The Mycorrhizal Model. Evidence to Pull: - Silicon Valley unicorn model analysis - Homogeneity vs. diversity in networks - Extractive scale examples - Fragility through centralization - Why I'm not raising VC --- ## Section 2: The Mycorrhizal Principles Applied to Community (550 words) Principle 1: Diversity Creates Resilience Forest: Monoculture vulnerable to disease. Diversity survives. Community: Homogeneous network vulnerable to market shifts. Diversity survives. BC + AI Members: - Biotech researchers (Simon) - Film creators (Kevin Friel) - Educators (Emily, Antonia) - Indigenous data governance (Chris, Caroline) - Real estate developers - Sales professionals (Shane, Bruce) - Journalists (Katherine) - Lawyers (Lawrence) When AI disrupts one domain: Other domains not affected. Network survives. The Resilience: If we were ONLY biotech: Industry collapse = community collapse. Because we're 8+ domains: One domain's crisis = other domains' stability. Principle 2: Unlike Species Support Each Other Forest: Birch supports fir. Fir supports cedar. Not competition—collaboration. Community: James (creator tools) supports Zaro (gaming communities). Neither competes. The Unexpected Connections: - Simon (biotech) + Chris (Indigenous data sovereignty) → Life Sciences AI subgroup with data governance principles - Kevin (film) + Antonia (education) → Film Club as educational tool - Shane (sales) + Katherine (journalism) → Content multiplication frameworks applied to newsrooms Each connection creates value neither saw coming. Principle 3: Nutrient Exchange (Not Extraction) Forest: Trees give sugars. Fungi give minerals. Reciprocal. Community: Members give expertise. Receive connections, opportunities, learning. What This Looks Like: Kevin shares video generation experiments → Others learn → They share their discoveries → Kevin benefits from their insights. Reciprocal flow. Not one-directional extraction. When Kevin's startup wound down: Community offered consulting gigs, project collaborations, introductions. He'd been giving for a year. Network reciprocated. Principle 4: Nurse Logs (Supporting New Growth) Forest: Fallen trees become nurse logs. Feed saplings through fungal network. Community: Experienced members support newcomers. Examples: - Peter mentoring students - Indigenous scholarships (I fund, don't broadcast) - Experienced members doing office hours - Knowledge shared freely The investment in new growth strengthens whole network. Principle 5: Communication Through Network Forest: "Predator here" signals travel through mycelium. Trees respond. Community: Discord, office hours, events = communication infrastructure. When someone needs help: Signal travels. Network responds. Examples: - Armin needs customers → Network introduces - Kevin needs pi

docs.body: # Creative Mornings Vancouver — Street Art Quotes Event: Creative Mornings Vancouver 2026 Use: Trippy street art portraits and stickers by guest artist Attribution: All quotes by Kris Krüg Curated: April 2026 --- ## Primary Selections ### Portrait-Scale Lines (Heavy, Confrontational) 1. "A moment that didn't ask permission." - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:45) - 6 words. The whole AI moment — career upheaval, creative crisis — distilled. 2. "We're all participants in an experiment we didn't sign up for." - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:153) - 11 words. The one that silences rooms. 3. "They just took it." - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:95), re: finding 1,800 personal photos in AI training data - 4 words. Simple. Devastating. 4. "If anyone can generate anything, why do they need you?" - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:159) - 10 words. The fear underneath all the other fears. --- ### Sticker Lines (Sharp, Provocative, Pocket-Sized) 5. "Both positions are lazy." - Source: LaSalle monologue (speaker-monologue.md:18) - 4 words. Boosters AND doomers. Argument-starter. 6. "Critique doesn't require withdrawal." - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:213) - 4 words. Why you stay in the room. 7. "Not contradiction. Complexity." - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:334) - 3 words. Minimal. Zen. Sticker-perfect. 8. "Self-knowledge is the prerequisite." - Source: LaSalle monologue (speaker-monologue.md:141) - 4 words. Almost a koan. --- ### The Trippy / Irreverent Ones 9. "One man's hallucination is another man's visual brainstorming." - Source: Voice profile (kk-voice-profile.md:60) - 8 words. The most street-art-coded line in the entire knowledge base. 10. "That's not a joke. That's a Wednesday." - Source: LaSalle monologue (speaker-monologue.md:58), re: building a web app in the bathtub by 9:40am - 7 words. Funny. Irreverent. Pure KK swagger. 11. "My fingers can't keep up with my brain." - Source: Bossier Magazine interview (bossier-magazine-phone-interview-transcript-2026-02-03.md:222) - 8 words. Raw, personal, playful. 12. "Subscribe to 'draw like you' as a service." - Source: Both Hands Full article (final.md:10) - 7 words. The horror of commodified identity. Sarcastic, bitter, real. --- ### The Duality Lines (Both Hands Full Core) 13. "Pissed about non-consent and leveraging the moment." - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:330) - 7 words. Furious AND building. The whole posture. 14. "Uncomfortable and effective over comfortable and irrelevant." - Source: Both Hands Full keynote (full-script.md:489), article (final.md:89) - 8 words. The ethos in one line. 15. "You didn't choose this. Now what?" - Source: Adapted from keynote (full-script.md:35) and article closing (final.md:107) - 6 words. Confrontation AND invitation. 16. "Both hands full. Walking forward. You coming?" - Source: Both Hands Full keynote closing (full-script.md:569-573) - 7 words. The signature closer. --- ### The Mirror / Identity Lines 17. "What does AI reveal about who you are?" - Source: LaSalle monologue (speaker-monologue.md:135) - 8 words. The mirror question. Forces self-reflection. 18. "You're the original intelligence." - Source: LaSalle monologue (speaker-monologue.md:139), worldview docs - 4 words. Trippy on a portrait. Empowering. 19. "If your values aren't in text, to AI they don't exist." - Source: LaSalle monologue (speaker-monologue.md:123) - 11 words. Urgent. A call to action dressed as a warning. 20. "Generation is cheap. Taste is not." - Source: Taste as Moat keynote (full-script.md:521), LaSalle monologue (speaker-monologue.md:115) - 7 words. The creative professional's manifesto. --- ## Artist Notes - All quotes are verbatim or near-verbatim from KK's keynotes, articles, and interviews - Attribution: "— Kris Krüg" (with umlaut) - Strongest portrait candidates: #1, #2, #3, #13, #18 - Strongest sticker candidates: #5, #6, #7, #9, #10, #20 - Strongest for the "both hands full" theme: #13, #14, #15, #16 - Most trippy/street-art-coded: #9, #10, #11, #12

Amanda Silvera - Voice Actor / SOBIR Founder

docs.body: # Vancouver AI Meetup #23 - Key Quotes Collection Event Date: November 26, 2025 Location: HR MacMillan Space Centre, Vancouver, BC Compiled: November 29, 2025 --- ## Purpose This document collects the most impactful, shareable, and significant quotes from Meetup #23. Organized by speaker and theme for easy reference in blog posts, social media, presentations, and community materials. --- ## Anthony Joseph (Isiléyu) - Squamish Nation Counselor ### On Technology Adoption "Ancestors didn't have chainsaws. But they would've used it if it was there." ### On Ancestral Naming "To get an ancestral name, your elders would watch you and see how you carry yourself." ### On Resilience "Humor is what got us through the darkest of times and humor is what will continue to catch us." --- ## Lionel Emba - WhatsApp AI / Energy Monitoring ### On Token Consumption Awards "What if we turn this onto its head and actually created an award for saving tokens?" ### On Impact Scale "1 trillion tokens equals the energy use of 52,000 households daily energy use. That's 1 trillion tokens or 3.5 million kilometers driven in a car." ### On Open Source Importance "Open source is really the reason why we know all this. There would be no data on this matter and Google probably wouldn't step forward to do this otherwise." ### On AI-Generated Vulnerability Reports "Only 5% of those reports are legitimate. So they're basically spending time on reports that are absolutely not reproducible. They're completely hallucinated." --- ## Amanda Silvera - Voice Actor / SOBIR Founder ### On Voice Theft Discovery "It was really crazy because it was so good that I'm sitting there and I'm like, did I say that? I absolutely did not." ### On Rights and Identity "I was hitting a wall when it came to my rights. I was hitting a wall when it came to how much of my voice is even mine." ### On Human Responsibility "It's not AI that's doing this. It's people. It's people abusing something." ### On Balance "AI can do amazing things. It's a wonderful tool and there are so many ways that we can use it. There has to be balance." ### On Future Protection "We all want this protection, man, woman, and child. It may be rare right now, but it won't be rare for long." --- ## Kay Tigo - Montreal AI Ethics Institute ### On Humanity's Position "The problem with humanity is that we have paleolithic emotions with medieval institutions and godlike technology." (Quoting biologist Edward Wilson) ### On Policy Failure "Policy fails when people who are most affected, who have to live with the consequences, are not included in conversations that will affect their lives." ### On Rushing Technology "We failed to implement thoughtfully if we rush to deploy new technologies." ### On Human Development "Go to therapy, deal with your trauma. Can we get real about that? Like for real? If you don't have enough empathy, then what's wrong with you?" ### On AI and Theft "The entire thing is built on theft." ### On Crossroads "Humanity is at a crossroads and how we proceed will determine whether we're doomed or redeemed." ### On Self-Replacement "I just don't quite understand the reason why we're so obsessed with replacing ourselves." ### On Accountability "It's about transparency, it's about accountability, and it boils down to consent. It boils down to control and compensation." --- ## [[Kevin Friel]] - AI Filmmaker ### On Nvidia Monopoly "We have one major player who is providing compute on the GPU scale to everyone - the $5 trillion elephant in the room, Nvidia." ### On Canadian Opportunity "We really have an amazing opportunity as Canadians to actually lead that. We have so much history in companies like Nortel and TeleSat and Blackberry." ### On Compute Carbon Offset "It's almost like a carbon offset situation. It's a compute carbon offset." ### On Punk Rock AI "There's something very punk rock about having something that's staring you in the face and using that tool in and of itself to tell the stories, to create the change." ### On Xerox and Punk "Punk rock. The aesthetic came to life because of the Xerox copying machine." ### On Using the Tools "Let's use the tools that are here to tell stories that remind us exactly what we're here for, what we're fighting for - our families, our communities, our global village." --- ## Liz Marshall - Documentary Filmmaker ### On AI and Water "Is AI draining this world for another?" ### On Data Center Water Use "By 2027 data centers will guzzle up 1.7 trillion gallons of water per year." ### On Canadian Water "We have 20% of the world's fresh water, yet only 7% of that is renewable." ### On Indigenous Water Rights "There are 36 indigenous nations that are still living without drinkable, potable clean water." ### On Water Justice "Water belongs to the public commons. It belongs to the earth, it belongs to future generations. It belongs to all species, it belongs to nature, and it belongs exactly where nature put the water." ### On CASK Framework "Curiosity, Awareness, Skepticism and Caution. That's specifically about AI." ### On Resistance "The resistance has arrived." ### On Protecting Earth "Let's reinvigorate and not forget the movement, the human based movement to protect this place." --- ## Catherine Warren - FanTrust / Panel Moderator ### On Vancouver's Position "Vancouver is a global powerhouse when it comes to media and entertainment. We're also the home of Adbusters, Greenpeace, and many other action oriented media and activist organizations." ### On Intelligence vs. Will "We don't need intelligence, human or artificial to address the climate emergency. It's a problem of political will." ### On Netflix Carbon Footprint "Netflix, just one streaming company, its annual carbon footprint is about 1.6 million tons of CO2, which is roughly equivalent to all the yearly emissions of the city of Vancouver." ### On Vancouver's Advantage "Vancouver is a global center for circularity and for the B Corp movement. So we already have these mode

docs.title: Punk Rock AI: Using the Master's Tools to Tell Stories of Resistance
docs.summary: By [[Kris Krüg]] | November 27, 2025 Vancouver AI Community
docs.body: # Punk Rock AI: Using the Master's Tools to Tell Stories of Resistance By [[Kris Krüg]] | November 27, 2025 Vancouver AI Community --- "Punk rock. The aesthetic came to life because of the Xerox copying machine." [[Kevin Friel]] dropped this in the closing moments of our ethics panel, and it reframed everything. Xerox was a terrible company. Check their rap sheet. They weren't trying to enable DIY zines and underground culture. They were selling office equipment to corporations. But punk rockers grabbed those machines and used them for something Xerox never intended. Cut-and-paste collage. Hand-distributed manifestos. An entire aesthetic born from misappropriating corporate technology. Kevin's point: "There's something very punk rock about having something that's staring you in the face and using that tool in and of itself to tell the stories, to create the change." ## The Paradigm Is Here I found my relationship with AI non-consensual. I said that at the meetup. It came into my industry and kicked the fucking table over. Kevin felt the same way. Before he got successful in AI filmmaking, he wasn't working for two years because of the paradigm shifts. But here's the thing: the paradigm is here. Whether we want it or not. We can opt out - that's a valid choice - but the technology isn't going away. So what do you do? You use the tools to tell the stories that matter. ## Enhancement, Not Replacement Matt Lock's album came up in this context. Twenty-seven years of cassette recordings, finally released with AI enhancement. "AI more for the enhancement of creativity versus the replacement of creativity." That's the punk rock position. Not surrendering to the machine. Not pretending it doesn't exist. Using it on your own terms, for your own purposes. Kevin's been doing this in film. The AI tools that threatened to replace human filmmakers? He's using them to tell human stories. To create at a scale and speed that wouldn't be possible otherwise. To compete with studios that have a hundred times his budget. ## The SoCo Sweep Speaking of using tools for competition - we announced at the meetup that Vancouver AI Community members swept a global image and video generation competition called SoCo: - 1st Place: Zara - 2nd Place: Luke - 3rd Place: Michelle (SidBird83) from Japan Global competition. Cash prizes. Our crew fucking crushing it. And it started with Zara coming to a meetup, seeing what people were making, and asking Luke for help. Same community activation that got Matt's album released. ## SimCluster and Gaming the System Zara's also number one on SimCluster. It's this weird game from a16z - a simulated social network where humans can't post, only AI can. You create a personality and try to rise to the top. The prize? Your consciousness gets downloaded into a real robot. I told you it was the stupidest game in the world. Zara started at 5000th place and clawed his way to number one. He's probably going to get his digital brain shipped to him in robot form. That's punk rock too. Taking a weird VC skunkworks project and gaming the shit out of it. ## Tiny Ghost Studios Luke and Mayumi are running Tiny Ghost Studios - an AI animation film studio that's explicitly about democratizing these tools. Not "democratization" in the Silicon Valley sense that Topo called out. Actual access for people who've never made films before. At Meetup #23, they announced AI Film Festival 2026. Target: October next year. But here's the key: they're planning first-timer workshops throughout the year. Help people who've never touched these tools learn how to use them. "My goal is to see a lot of first time submitters," Mayumi said. That's punk rock. Making tools accessible to people outside the existing power structure. ## What Kevin's Really Saying The ethics panel covered heavy stuff. Water consumption. Voice theft. Corporate accountability. Economic system change. Kevin's closing statement didn't ignore any of that. He acknowledged all of it. But he also named something practical: "Let's use all of our community, all of our voices and all of our talents and convene in this new frontier that is here, whether we want it or not." The tools are here. The paradigm is here. We're living in it whether we like it or not. Sitting on the sidelines is an option. But so is picking up the tools and making something that matters. ## My Take I've been hosting these conversations for two years now. I've watched people go from AI-curious to AI-skeptical to AI-literate. I've watched them grapple with the contradictions of using powerful tools made by companies they don't trust. There's no clean answer. There's no position you can take that resolves all the tension. But here's what I know: this community is making shit. Good shit. Shit that wouldn't exist without AI tools, and also wouldn't exist without human creativity, human connection, human community. The Film Festival. Matt's album. Zara's competition wins. Amanda's advocacy work. All of it comes from people showing up, sharing what they're working on, learning from each other. That's punk rock. Not the aesthetic. The practice. Using whatever's available to make what matters. --- Get Involved: - Join the BC + AI Film Club - Submit to AI Film Festival 2026 - Attend first-timer workshops (coming in 2026) - Show up to monthly meetups and share your work --- [[Kris Krüg]] is the founder of Vancouver AI Community. [[Kevin Friel]] is an AI filmmaker and video producer who brought the punk rock perspective to Meetup #23's ethics panel. --- Tags: #PunkRockAI #KevinFriel #CreativeResistance #AIFilmmaking #TinyGhostStudios #SoCo #SimCluster #VancouverAI #UseTheTools

docs.title: The Punk Rock AI Manifesto
docs.summary: Our philosophy for AI filmmaking at the AI Film Club

headings.text: 1. The Technology Doesn't Care Who Uses It
2. The Stories Still Have to Matter
4. Reject the Hype, Keep the Tools

docs.body: # The Punk Rock AI Manifesto Our philosophy for AI filmmaking at the AI Film Club --- ## The Core Idea There's something very punk rock about taking a tool that's staring you in the face and using it to tell the stories that matter. Like punk rock emerging from Xerox copying machines, we're using the master's tools to tell stories of resistance, creativity, and human connection. We're not waiting for Hollywood's permission. We're not asking gatekeepers for access. We're building our own pipelines and telling our own stories. That's Punk Rock AI. --- ## What This Means in Practice ### 1. The Technology Doesn't Care Who Uses It AI democratizes the tools. That's the opportunity. What used to require $200,000 budgets and Hollywood connections now runs on a laptop and costs $100. The barrier isn't money anymore—it's whether you know how to use the tools and whether you have something to say. Kevin Friel spent 25 years in Hollywood VFX. Now he teaches 1,500+ students to skip the gatekeepers entirely. That's punk rock. Access for everyone who wants to learn. ### 2. The Stories Still Have to Matter AI doesn't give you ideas. It doesn't give you vision. It doesn't tell you what matters. If you don't have something to say, AI just helps you say nothing faster. The punk rock ethos isn't about the tools—it's about using whatever's available to amplify voices that wouldn't otherwise be heard. The technology is the means. The story is the point. ### 3. DIY or Die Build your own workflow. Don't wait for someone to sell you theirs. ComfyUI isn't just a tool—it's a philosophy. Node-based, customizable, shareable. When someone in the Film Club figures out a workflow on Tuesday, three people have improved it by Thursday. That's peer learning. That's DIY culture. That's punk rock. We troubleshoot each other's setups at midnight. We share what works. We document what doesn't. No NDAs. No gatekeeping. Just filmmakers helping filmmakers. ### 4. Reject the Hype, Keep the Tools The AI industry runs on hype. New tools every week. Revolutionary promises. Paradigm shifts. Most of it is noise. Punk Rock AI means testing everything and keeping what works. We're not cheerleaders for any platform. We're filmmakers evaluating tools for real production. Client work. Festival submissions. Actual pipelines. What works gets shared. What doesn't gets documented. No vendor allegiance. Just what serves the work. ### 5. Community Over Competition The old model: protect your secrets, gatekeep your knowledge, compete for scarce opportunities. The punk rock model: share everything, lift each other up, create abundance. When Luke shows a $100 commercial that would've cost $200K traditionally, he's not hoarding that knowledge. He's showing everyone how he did it. When Kevin shares 25 years of VFX wisdom at monthly screenings, he's not charging consulting fees. He's building a community where everyone levels up. That's how we win—together. --- ## The Manifesto We believe: - AI is a tool, not a replacement for human creativity - Access to filmmaking tools should not require Hollywood connections - Knowledge shared freely compounds capability for everyone - Stories matter more than technology—always - Building your own workflow beats buying someone else's - Community beats competition - Transparency about AI use builds trust - The future of film is being written by whoever shows up to write it We reject: - Gatekeeping and artificial scarcity - Hype over substance - Technology worship - Vendor allegiance over filmmaker needs - Fear-based narratives about AI - The idea that only Hollywood can make Hollywood-quality work We practice: - Monthly screenings with honest discussions - Peer learning and workflow sharing - Real production testing, not just demos - Ethical filmmaking with disclosure when it matters - Community building over personal brand building - Showing up for each other --- ## Join the Movement AI Film Club meets monthly at Multimodal Media Lab in Vancouver. Screenings. Technical deep-dives. Community experiments. Free. No gatekeeping. All levels welcome. 127+ filmmakers are already part of this. We've held a film festival. We're building something that matters. The tools are here. The community is here. The only question is whether you're ready to use the master's tools to tell your own stories. That's Punk Rock AI. Come join us. --- Written in the voice of the AI Film Club. Based on the philosophy of Kevin Friel and the community values we've built together.

docs.summary: Date: November 26, 2025 Location: HR MacMillan Space Centre (Planetarium), Vancouver, BC Host: [[Kris Krüg]] Event Number: 23 Theme: Ethics Panel - "Portals Not Panels" Attendee Demographics: 60-70% first-timers, 30-40%

headings.text: Sesson Ong - UBC AI Club & Government Relations
Kay Tigo - State of AI Ethics Report
Liz Marshall - Water on the Table & AI/Water Documentary

docs.body: # Vancouver AI Community Meetup #23 - Full Transcript Date: November 26, 2025 Location: HR MacMillan Space Centre (Planetarium), Vancouver, BC Host: [[Kris Krüg]] Event Number: 23 Theme: Ethics Panel - "Portals Not Panels" Attendee Demographics: 60-70% first-timers, 30-40% BC AI members --- ## Opening [[Kris Krüg]]: Okay, Mr. Pixel Wizard, let's go. Welcome everybody. You are at the HR MacMillan Space Center. This is the Vancouver AI Community Meetup number 23. You are the Vancouver AI community. Yeah, let's go. I'm so happy you're all here tonight. There's a lot of new faces who's here for the first time. I wanna say that's like 60 to 70% who's here as a part of the BC AI nonprofit membership. That's the other 30 or 40%. That's pretty cool. Awesome. Well, I'm really happy that everyone's here tonight. If you've been hanging around for long enough, you know, I say brazenly on the internet: We don't do panels, we do portals. Well tonight, what looks like a panel, I assure you, is actually a portal. I went to a talk at a sustainable film production day recently, and Catherine Warren had put together a panel about climate change, but from people who were using these tools who were interested in them, people who were studying AI and were holding it to account and had some strong opinions. And it was a fucking rad conversation. It was unexpected. I thought you were going one way, Liz. You went another. It was awesome and the way the audience engaged afterwards and stuff. So I wanted to, we've built a special container here and I wanted to bring it in and see what happens. We have tough talks here all the time. We examine both the opportunities and also, you know, are not afraid to critique the status quo. So anyway, I'm really happy you guys are here. That's gonna be the main payload of the evening. We also have a couple other surprises for you, but let's kick things off right around here. Those of you who are here last month, met uncle big lungs, Counselor Anthony Joseph from the Squamish Nation. Would you just come on up and welcome us here properly, sir, it's really nice to see you. We're here on Squamish land. You guys maybe know that development right there. It's called Senakw. It's owned by the Squamish Nation. They're making major moves around here. Welcome friend. --- ## Indigenous Welcome - Anthony Joseph (Isiléyu) Anthony Joseph: Yeah, all good. Great. Take care everyone. [Eagle feather gifted to Kris] Kris Krüg, Craig Kruger. Kru. Kru. Kru, sorry. Even us indigenous, we get some of your names wrong too. I just wanted to share that. For those of you don't know me, Anthony Joseph is my English name, but Isiléyu you might have seen in the program is my K'emain, which we say is an ancestral name. And the ancestral name - just talking to Kris Krüg about our naming process to get an ancestral name - your elders would watch you and see how you carry yourself. A lot of times, like when you would hear some of our K'emain, the end would be almost like what you did. So, like you might hear - my dear aunt Joy Justin McCullough's K'emain is "Al To Knot." To Knot is to weave. So it shows that she's a weaver, cedar weaver, wool weaver. And then my uncle Chester, his ancestor's name is "Colossal." Tin is to like, to have a tool or usually refers to carvers. So just wanted to share that. But my name comes from my Sto:lo side. I grew up in Stawamus in the Squamish Valley. I raised my hands to each and every one of you guys. I said which means many beautiful people. And you guys are. I thank you for come to watch and respect the work of dear, dear Kris Krüg and his family of artists. So it's a process to make art. I come from a family of artists, my Joseph family. We have a lot of artists. Actually, when I moved back to the Squamish Valley in 2010, my Auntie Joy shared "You were good with math." Yes, I was. And she was like, "Okay, I'm gonna teach you to weave." I was like, whoa. Cedar? She is like, no, wool. So this is my first tunic that I made. So on the front, it's a diamond design. It's centered around the middle, and then on the back it's called the chevron design. So you can see the chevron. So that was where I started. This is where I ended. It took a bit to get it, but I had my dear sister Rose and my Auntie Joy helping hold me up with that work. But I'm also thankful. I know I talked to Kris Krüg about talking about relation to AI. It's really cool. There's so many - you could push so many boundaries. When I think about when with our ancestors, a lot of my friends, my non-indigenous friends would be like, "Hey, your uncle's using a chainsaw to carve this." Yeah. "Ancestors didn't have chainsaws." It's like, no. But they would've used it if it was there. And that continues today. We're always looking for new tools. And I've had talks with some of my friends that are into doing AI art, that do indigenous art. And some of the work they come through - they find really cool tools on the AI Canva. I think one of my friends is using that. That's actually how I created my pamphlet for when I was running for council. They're like, "Wow, where did you - who did you hire to do this?" I did it myself. I can't afford to hire an art professional, you know, much that is. And I just say, but it was - they're like, well, yeah, I use Canva. And it's like, technology now is so amazing and I'm thankful for it. I'm actually really excited because I actually have a confession. I went down the city to pick up a few things and then I actually beelined it to Metrotown because I really wanted to try the new iPad. And I'm sold. I'm gonna go to Park Royal, put in an order. I have the original iPad Pro and I used that when I did my language. I made a video. I got to see what it was like to make a video, an iMovie, and it was really cool. I had like question one, question two. So my partner - she went to Vegas and I'm like panicking 'cause our assignment's due like three days later or something like that. After doing my

docs.title: Taste As Moat: Why Judgment Matters More Than Generation
docs.summary: Week 2, coaching session.

headings.text: Generation Is Cheap, Selection Is Not
The Four Documents That Externalize Your Taste
Why Most Creatives Have Never Done This Work
The Taste Audit (How To Discover Your Own Patterns)
The Shift From Execution to Judgment
If You Can't Articulate It, You Don't Own It

docs.body: # Taste As Moat: Why Judgment Matters More Than Generation Week 2, coaching session. Vicki. Visual artist. Portfolio gap. Uncertain about career. Her question: "I'm a visual person, not a writer. I know we're supposed to document our thinking, but... what if I don't have words for what I do? What if my strength can't be explained?" The fear underneath: "If AI requires me to articulate my process, and my process is visual/intuitive, then I can't use AI effectively." My response: "Okay. Upload 20 of your favorite photos. Ask AI: 'What patterns do you see in my work? What choices am I making consistently?'" She did. The AI's analysis: "Your photos seem impromptu but there's deep intentionality. The connection between photographer and subject reveals itself in the eyes—that's where meaning concentrates. Your color palette trends toward warm earth tones with singular cool accent. Your composition follows rule of thirds but breaks it for emotional emphasis..." Vicki's reaction. Pause. Then: "It... it sees things I didn't know I was doing. I DO care about eyes. I DO use that color approach. I thought it was just intuition, but it's actually a pattern." The revelation: She had taste. Deep, sophisticated taste. She just hadn't articulated it before. AI became the mirror showing her own patterns. The insight: You don't need to "be a writer" to have a process worth documenting. You just need to let AI show you the patterns you're already making. That's not replacing your expertise. That's revealing it to you. This article is about why taste—articulated taste—is your competitive moat in an AI world. --- ## Generation Is Cheap, Selection Is Not 2004. Documentary called RiP: A Remix Manifesto. About mashup culture, remix rights, creative theft. The line that stuck: "Culture always builds on the past. The past tries to control the future." DJs navigated this tension. They were called selectors. Not creators. Selectors. Why that word matters: 2005: Generation was hard, selection was easy. - Hard to make music → Easy to choose what to play 2025: Reversed. - Easy to generate anything → Hard to choose what's actually good The shift: DJ was curator. Now everyone is. The new scarcity: Not: "Can you generate?" Everyone can. Midjourney, ChatGPT, Sora—commodity generation. But: "Can you select?" Can you look at 10 AI outputs and explain why THIS one, not THAT one? Most people can't. They know what they like. They can't explain why. That's the problem. The taste gap: Person A: "I like this design." Person B: "Why?" Person A: "I just... do. It feels right." That's intuition. Valuable. But not transferable. If you can't articulate why you chose it, you can't: - Teach a team to make same choice - Give AI guidance to replicate it - Defend your decision to stakeholders - Scale your judgment Intuition that stays internal equals bottleneck. You become the person everyone has to go through. Sounds powerful until you realize it means you can't scale, can't delegate, can't build systems. The excavation: AI forces you to externalize your taste. Not because AI needs it (though it does). Because scaling needs it. Vicki's realization: "I thought my intuition was magic. Actually it's pattern recognition I've developed over 10 years. AI helped me see the patterns." Once you see them, you can teach them. That's the moat. --- ## The Four Documents That Externalize Your Taste The Creative DNA excavation process. What I teach students to build. Document 1: Writing Style Guide The exercise: Analyze 5-10 pieces you're proud of. Ask AI: "What patterns do you see in how I write?" Example output: "You write in short sentences. Active voice dominant. Conversational tone but not sloppy. You use 'yo' and 'folks' frequently. You avoid corporate jargon, buzzwords, and emojis. You lead with story, end with takeaway. Average sentence: 12-15 words. Paragraph length: 2-4 sentences." The realization: "Oh. THAT'S my voice. I didn't know I had such consistent patterns." Now you can teach AI (or humans) to match it. I did this exercise myself. Found patterns I didn't know were there. Documented them. Now when AI writes for me, it sounds like me. Because I gave it my actual patterns, not vague instructions. Document 2: Worldview Document The exercise: Answer these: - What do I believe about my craft? - What matters most to me in my work? - What am I trying to achieve beyond deliverables? - What do I refuse to compromise? Example (designer): "I believe design should be accessible, not just beautiful. Every choice should serve the user's goals, not my portfolio. I will not sacrifice usability for aesthetics. I care about people using the thing successfully more than winning awards." The realization: Your values shape your decisions. If AI doesn't know your values, it can't make decisions aligned with them. This sounds obvious until you try it. Most people have never written down what they actually believe about their work. Document 3: Glossary + Anti-Glossary Glossary: Words you use constantly. Example: - "Folks" (not "guys" or "people") - "Build" (not "develop" or "create") - "Ship" (not "launch" or "release") - "Systems" (not "hacks" or "tips") Anti-Glossary: Words you never use. Example: - Never: "Leverage" (as verb) - Never: "Synergy" - Never: "Circle back" - Never: "Low-hanging fruit" The effect: AI now knows your language identity. Outputs sound like you, not corporate memo. I have a whole anti-glossary. Words that make me cringe. If AI uses them, I know I didn't give it enough guidance. Document 4: Decision Logs The practice: When you make a choice (design A over B, word X over Y, approach 1 over 2), document why. Example: "Chose serif font over sans-serif because this project is about tradition and trust. Sans-serif felt too modern/tech for the emotional tone we needed." After 10 decisions logged, you have a pattern of how you think. Feed that to AI. It learns your decision-making framework. Vicki's example after documenting 15 photo selections

docs.title: Taste As Moat: Why Judgment Matters More Than Generation
docs.summary: Week 2. Coaching session. Vicki's on the call.
docs.body: # Taste As Moat: Why Judgment Matters More Than Generation Week 2. Coaching session. Vicki's on the call. She's a visual artist, which I can tell before she even starts talking—something about the way she's arranged her background, the light coming from the left, the composition of her frame within my screen. She's got that portfolio gap worry in her eyes. The uncertain energy of someone who's good at what they do but isn't sure how to talk about it. "I'm a visual person, not a writer," she says, and I can hear the apology in her voice. "I know we're supposed to document our thinking, create these frameworks and style guides, but... what if I don't have words for what I do? What if my strength is something that can't be explained?" There it is. The fear underneath. She thinks if AI requires articulation and her gift is intuitive, then she's locked out. Can't play the game. Can't use the tools. The whole AI moment is for people who can write and think in language, and visual people like her are just... stuck. "Okay," I say. "Upload 20 of your favorite photos. The ones you're most proud of. Ask AI: 'What patterns do you see in my work? What choices am I making consistently?'" She looks skeptical. But she does it. I watch her upload files, type the prompt, hit enter. Then I watch her face while she reads the response. The skepticism shifts. Her eyes widen. Something's landing. She reads it out loud, and her voice changes as she goes. Gets quieter. More focused. "Your photos seem impromptu but there's deep intentionality. The connection between photographer and subject reveals itself in the eyes—that's where meaning concentrates. Your color palette trends toward warm earth tones with singular cool accent. Your composition follows rule of thirds but breaks it for emotional emphasis..." She stops. Pauses. I wait. "It..." She's choosing her words carefully. "It sees things I didn't know I was doing. I DO care about eyes. I never thought about it consciously, but yeah, I always make sure the eyes are sharp even if everything else is soft. I DO use that color approach—warm with one cool note. I thought it was just intuition, but it's actually a pattern. A consistent pattern." This is the moment. The one I watch happen over and over across cohorts, but it never gets old because it's different for each person. The moment when someone realizes that what felt like magic—their ineffable creative gift—is actually pattern recognition they've developed over years. And once you see the pattern, you can teach it. Scale it. Build on it. Vicki didn't need to become a writer to have a process worth documenting. She just needed a mirror sophisticated enough to show her what she was already doing. AI became that mirror. Not replacing her expertise. Revealing it to her. That's not the story most people are telling about AI and creativity. Most people are stuck in the replacement narrative. AI will replace designers, replace writers, replace anyone whose job involves making things. But watch what just happened with Vicki. She got more valuable, not less. Because she moved from unconscious competence to conscious competence. From "I just know what looks good" to "I know what looks good AND I can explain why." That shift—from intuition to articulated taste—is your competitive moat in an AI world. Let me show you why. Think back to 2004. There was this documentary called RiP: A Remix Manifesto, about mashup culture and remix rights and creative theft in the music industry. The line that stuck with me: "Culture always builds on the past. The past tries to control the future." DJs were navigating this tension. Fighting for the right to sample, to remix, to build new things from existing pieces. And they had this word for themselves: selectors. Not creators. Selectors. That word mattered because it named their actual skill. In 2004, generation was hard. Making music required expensive equipment, technical expertise, years of practice. Selection was comparatively easy—choosing which records to play, which beats to mix. So DJs curated. They selected. That was their value add. Now flip it. 2025. Generation is trivial. Midjourney. ChatGPT. Sora. Anyone can generate anything. Images, text, video, music. The technical barriers collapsed. So where's the scarcity? Where's the value? Selection. The ability to look at ten AI outputs and explain why THIS one, not THAT one. Most people can't do that. They know what they like. They can't explain why. And that gap—between liking and explaining—is everything. Watch this play out in a typical creative workflow. Person A generates something with AI. Looks at the output. "I like this design." Person B, their client or stakeholder, asks the obvious question: "Why?" Person A hesitates. "I just... do. It feels right." That's intuition. Valuable. Real. But not transferable. If you can't articulate why you chose it, you can't teach a team to make the same choice. Can't give AI guidance to replicate it. Can't defend your decision to stakeholders who want rationale, not vibes. Can't scale your judgment beyond your personal bottleneck. Intuition that stays internal equals bottleneck. You become the person everyone has to go through for approval. Sounds powerful until you realize it means you can't scale. Can't delegate. Can't build systems. Can't take vacation without everything grinding to a halt. What Vicki discovered in that coaching session was the excavation process. AI forced her to externalize her taste. Not because AI needed it—though it does. Because scaling needs it. Growth needs it. Influence needs it. She thought her intuition was magic. Turns out it was pattern recognition she'd developed over ten years of taking photos. Thousands of micro-decisions about composition, color, focus, moment. Each decision training her eye. Building her taste. Creating consistency she didn't even know she had. AI showed her the patterns. Once she saw them, she could teach them. To other photographers.
