docs.body: # Both Hands Full: Walking Forward Through the Age of Synthetic Everything Speaker: Kris Krug Event: World AI Film Festival (WAIFF) -- Sao Paulo Edition Duration: 44 minutes + Q&A Audience: Filmmakers, industry professionals, AI cinema creators Context: Sao Paulo is one stop on the road to the Cannes Grand Finale (April 21-22, Palais des Festivals). WAIFF President: Gong Li. Honorary President: Claude Lelouch. Jury President: Agnes Jaoui. Ambassador: Jean-Michel Jarre. --- ## PROLOGUE: "Cinema Novo Did Not Ask Permission Either" (0:00-2:00) [NO SLIDES. House lights still warm. Walk to center stage. No podium. Take a breath. Look at the room.] Sao Paulo. On the road to Cannes. A festival built around the question every filmmaker in this room is already living: what happens when AI enters the frame? [Pause. Let the room feel itself.] You know who else asked dangerous questions about cinema? Cinema Novo. Glauber Rocha didn't have a playbook. He had a camera and something to say about Brazil. He didn't build a product. He built a movement. [Beat.] I'm not here to teach you. I'm here to share what I've learned -- and to learn from you. But first, I need to tell you something honest. [Transition to Act I. Walk stage left.] --- ## ACT I: THE LEFT HAND -- Naming What Is True (2:00-12:00) ### The Non-Consensual Opening (2:00-4:00) [SLIDE 1: "I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual." -- Cool blue tones, stark white text.] [Pause. Direct eye contact across the room. Serious tone.] I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual. [Let that land. Silence.] LaSalle College. January 2026. 200 art and design students. I said those words and the room went quiet. It didn't ask me to steal my work. It didn't ask me to take over this stuff. It didn't ask me if it could change everything around me. All I'm trying to do is keep my head above water and reinvent myself. [Beat. Personal testimony.] I released thousands of photos on Flickr over two decades under Creative Commons. Shared them freely -- that was the deal. When I checked a dataset search tool, I found roughly 1,800 of my images in a major training corpus. [Pause.] Nobody asked me. They just took it. [Turn to audience.] If you've ever released a film, posted a photograph, written a script, published your work online -- it may already be in training data. You weren't asked. That's not hypothetical. That's what happened. And everyone in this room knows it. ### Three Fears That Are Real (4:00-6:30) [SLIDE 2: "Three Fears That Are Real" -- Cool tones. No decoration. Just the weight of it.] Not hypothetical fears. Real shit happening right now. One: Stolen work without consent. Training data equals humanity's creative output scraped without permission. Your photos. Your writing. Your scenes. Used without asking. No compensation. No attribution. Call it what it is. [Beat.] Two: Race to the bottom. "Good enough" AI outputs devalue human craft. Clients are already saying it out loud: "Can't you just use AI for this?" Translation: "Why should I pay you when a tool can approximate it?" That pressure is messing with people's livelihoods. [Beat.] Three: Junior pipeline collapse. Entry-level jobs are vanishing. The roles that trained the next generation are disappearing. How do people become directors if they can't be assistants first? How do cinematographers learn their craft if nobody hires camera assistants anymore? Every experienced filmmaker in this room benefited from a pipeline that's collapsing under their feet. [Pause. Let them sit with this.] I teach AI for a living. I'm going to be honest with you: I don't have the junior pipeline problem solved. Nobody does. ### Name What You're Seeing (6:30-8:00) -- The Framing Problem [SLIDE 3: "STOP SAYING 'BIAS.' NAME WHAT YOU'RE SEEING." -- Red stencil on black.] [Direct. No softening.] And there's a fourth thing we need to talk about. What they built with our stolen work. I wrote something about this recently that I keep coming back to. We've said the word "bias" so many times it's lost meaning. Algorithmic bias. Training data bias. Gender bias. Generic. Safe. Abstract. Stop saying bias. Name what you're seeing. When a colleague of mine asked an image generator to create a "marketing professor," it gave her a man in a power pose -- Harvard blazer, authority, gravitas. Then she asked it to make the professor female. She got a timid school teacher. Not the same authority. Not the same context. A diminished version. [Beat.] It took her multiple rounds of prompting to get a woman with the same authority the system gave a man by default. That's not bias. That's misogyny embedded in image generation systems. Name it. [Direct.] When facial recognition fails on darker-skinned women at 40 times the rate it fails on lighter-skinned men -- 34.7% error versus 0.8% -- that's not a "performance gap." That's racism and sexism embedded in code. Dr. Joy Buolamwini proved it. She calls it the coded gaze: AI systems reflect the gaze of their creators. [Beat. Then the filmmaker bridge:] You're filmmakers. You understand this instinctively. What's missing from the frame matters as much as what's in it. Every shot is a decision about who's included and who's erased. Training data works exactly the same way. What data gets collected? Who's represented? Who's missing? Whose experience becomes the baseline? Those aren't technical decisions. They're values decisions. I call it bias laundering -- discrimination that looks like math. "The algorithm said so" carries weight that "my gut feeling says so" doesn't. But when you train that algorithm on centuries of discrimination, you're not creating objectivity. You're automating inequity. [Pause.] One more thing to carry in the left hand. Don't drop it. ### The 55% (8:00-9:30) [SLIDE 4: "55% / 12%" -- Stencil. Gallup data.] Here's a number that changed how I think about all of this. Gallup data: 55% of US workers have never used AI at all. Only 12% use it daily. Half

docs.body: # Both Hands Full -- Slide Deck Outline Total Slides: 25 Design System: DIY punk zine. Collage. Handmade. Hardcore. Photocopied aesthetic. NOT corporate. NOT polished. Looks like it was assembled at 3am with scissors, glue, a Xerox machine, and something to say. Aspect Ratio: 16:9 Palette: BLACK, WHITE, RED. Nothing else. Ever. --- ## DESIGN BIBLE ### The Aesthetic: Punk Zine Collage Think Crass Records sleeve art. Dischord Records insert. Riot Grrrl zine taped to a bathroom wall. Brazilian Tropicalia album art meets Sao Paulo street paste-ups. The slides should look like they were made by hand, photocopied badly, and stapled together with conviction. This is not a TED deck. This is not a startup pitch. This is a zine projected at 30 feet wide for 600 filmmakers in Sao Paulo -- on the road to Cannes. It should feel dangerous, honest, and handmade. ### Colors -- THREE ONLY | Color | Use | Feeling | |-------|-----|---------| | BLACK | Backgrounds, heavy type, borders, ink splatter | Weight. Authority. The dark room before the projector hits. | | WHITE | Text, cut-out shapes, negative space, Xerox grain | Raw truth. The page. The light. | | RED | Accents, strikes, underlines, blood, urgency, the hand | Anger. Love. Aliveness. The only color that matters. | No gradients. No transparency. No pastels. If it can't be screenprinted in a garage, it doesn't belong here. ### Typography - Headlines: Heavy bold sans-serif OR hand-drawn blocky caps. Imperfect. Slightly crooked. Like rubber-stamped or stenciled. Think Black Flag logo energy. - Quotes: Typewriter font. Courier. Monospaced. Like it was hammered out on an actual machine. Or handwritten in thick marker. - Data/Stats: Stencil font. Military surplus meets protest sign. The numbers should feel urgent, not corporate. - Body text (where needed): Cut-and-paste ransom note style OR clean typewriter. Never both on the same slide. - Portuguese subtitles: Handwritten below English text. Different hand. Like a friend translated it in the margin. ### Textures & Techniques - Xerox grain: Every slide should have some photocopy noise. Not clean. Degraded. Like it's been copied 10 times. - Cut-and-paste collage: Text and images should look physically cut out and glued onto the background. Visible edges. Tape marks. Glue residue. - Hand marks: Crossed-out text done BY HAND with thick marker, not a clean strikethrough font. Underlines are jagged. Circles are imperfect. - Ink splatter / spray paint: Occasional. Not on every slide. When something needs to feel raw. - High contrast photos: If photos are used, they're black and white, high contrast, halftone dot pattern. Xeroxed to hell. Grainy. Like a flyer for a basement show. - Red as punctuation: Red appears sparingly. A single red word. A red X. A red underline. A red hand print. When red shows up, it MEANS something. ### What This Is NOT - Not minimalist tech aesthetic - Not startup pitch deck - Not TED talk clean - Not infographic - Not corporate anything - Not "design-forward" -- it's "message-forward" - Not trying to be pretty. Trying to be TRUE. --- ## SLIDE-BY-SLIDE BREAKDOWN ### PROLOGUE (0:00-2:00) No slides. Black screen. Or screen OFF entirely. Just KK and the room. The first thing they see on screen should hit like a brick through a window. --- ### ACT I: LEFT HAND -- CRITIQUE (2:00-12:00) #### SLIDE 1: The Non-Consensual Opening Time: 2:00 Text: I FIND MY RELATIONSHIP WITH AI COMPLETELY NON-CONSENSUAL. Visual: Black background. White stencil-stamped text, slightly uneven. The word NON-CONSENSUAL in RED. Nothing else. No logo. No name. No decoration. Texture: Heavy Xerox grain across entire slide. Like this was photocopied from a protest flyer. Design note: This is the brick through the window. The audience's first visual. It should feel like someone spray-painted it on a wall. #### SLIDE 2: Three Fears That Are Real Time: 4:00 Text: 1. STOLEN WORK WITHOUT CONSENT 2. RACE TO THE BOTTOM 3. JUNIOR PIPELINE COLLAPSE Visual: Black background. White typewriter text. Each line numbered by hand (hand-drawn numbers, not typed). Red X marks or red check marks next to each -- not tidy, scrawled. Like a checklist on the back of a flyer. Texture: Paper grain. Coffee stain in corner. Xerox artifacts. #### SLIDE 3: Name What You're Seeing Time: 6:30 Text: STOP SAYING 'BIAS.' NAME WHAT YOU'RE SEEING. Visual: Black background. RED stencil text -- the only slide where the headline is fully red. Feels like it was spray-painted on a wall in anger. Below in white typewriter, smaller: "misogyny. racism. sexism. classism. not 'algorithmic challenges.'" Design note: This slide should feel like a call to action that interrupted the deck. Like someone broke in and added it. The red headline is a departure from the rest of Act I's white-on-black -- it should feel like the talk just got more dangerous. #### SLIDE 4: The 55% Time: 8:00 Text: 55% in MASSIVE stencil font, white on black. Below in smaller typewriter: "have never used AI at all." Sub-text: Handwritten in red marker at an angle: "if the ethical people leave, the opportunists write the rules" Visual: The 55% should dominate. Feels like it was wheat-pasted on a construction wall. The red handwriting feels added later, like graffiti on top of a poster. #### SLIDE 5: The First Protester Time: 9:30 Text: None. Or one word hand-scrawled: "GOOD." Visual: High-contrast black and white photo -- crowd energy, a microphone, stage perspective. Halftone dot pattern. Xeroxed. Grainy as hell. Like a photo from a zine about a punk show. If no photo available: black background with a single red circle (like a recording light on a camera). Design note: This is where the red starts to creep in. The recording light. The anger that's also love. #### SLIDE 6: Holding the Left Hand Time: 11:00 Text: "DON'T DROP THESE." in white stencil. Below, smaller typewriter: "they keep you honest." Visual: Black background. A hand-drawn or high-contrast photocopied image of an open left hand. Th

docs.title: Both Hands Full -- Speaker Notes & Quick Reference Card
docs.summary: For: Kris Krug Event: World AI Film Festival (WAIFF) -- Sao Paulo Edition Talk: "Both Hands Full: Walking Forward Through the Age of Synthetic Everything" Duration: 44 min + Q&A
docs.body: # Both Hands Full -- Speaker Notes & Quick Reference Card For: Kris Krug Event: World AI Film Festival (WAIFF) -- Sao Paulo Edition Talk: "Both Hands Full: Walking Forward Through the Age of Synthetic Everything" Duration: 44 min + Q&A --- ## TIMING CHEAT SHEET | Time | Section | One-Word Reminder | |------|---------|------------------| | 0:00 | Prologue -- Cinema Novo | GROUND | | 2:00 | Non-consensual opening | HONEST | | 4:00 | Three Fears | WEIGHT | | 6:30 | Name What You're Seeing | NAME IT | | 8:00 | The 55% (Gallup) | STAKES | | 9:30 | First Protester | VALIDATE | | 11:00 | Hold Left Hand | PIVOT | | 13:00 | "What's Also True" | SHIFT | | 14:00 | Luke / 80/20 Flip | STORY | | 18:00 | Kevin / Conductor | METAPHOR | | 21:00 | Belgium | GUT-PUNCH | | 24:00 | Transformation Montage | PATTERN | | 26:00 | Both/And Statement | CENTER | | 28:00 | Hold Right Hand | BRIDGE | | 29:00 | Binary Trap | CHALLENGE | | 31:00 | Vibe Coding + KB | PROOF | | 33:30 | Brazilian Opportunity | PRIDE | | 36:00 | Soul Dimension | QUIET | | 39:00 | The Choice (3 options) | FRAME | | 40:30 | The Stakes (founding members) | WEIGHT | | 42:00 | Invocation | PERFORM | | 44:00 | "You coming?" / Obrigado | HOLD | --- ## KEY STATS (Memorize These) - 1,800 -- KK's Flickr photos found in training corpus - 55% -- US workers who have NEVER used AI (Gallup) - 12% -- US workers who use AI daily (Gallup) - 80/20 -> 20/80 -- Luke's grind-to-creative ratio flip - 2-3 weeks -- What used to take Luke a year - 25 years -- Kevin Friel's Hollywood VFX career - 18 months -- Belgium bedridden before AI avatars - 85% -- Kevin's "get it there with AI, then craft finishes" - 40x -- Facial recognition failure rate: darker-skinned women vs lighter-skinned men (Joy Buolamwini, Gender Shades) - 34.7% vs 0.8% -- Error rates: darker-skinned women vs lighter-skinned men - 11 of 13 -- People who saw the problem in Fernanda's professor/teacher comparison (10 women, 1 man) - 600 -- Filmmakers and industry pros in this room - 850+ -- BC + AI Ecosystem members --- ## KEY NAMES & DETAILS ### Luke Minaker (80/20 Flip -- Centerpiece) - Who: Animator, 30 years in industry - Origin: 12 years old, Jurassic Park, "bring monsters to life" - Problem: Barbie commercials, cheap plastic gags - Flip: 80% grind/20% creative -> 80% creative/20% grind - Studio: Tiny Ghost (with Mayumi Rollings) - IP: "Blood and Glitter" -- greenlit and funded - Quote: "I don't care about the money. I am so excited that I get to do art again." - Landing: "The 12-year-old is back. After 30 years." ### Kevin Friel (Conductor) - Who: 25 years Hollywood VFX - Credits: Dune, Detective Pikachu - Origin: Mom took him to symphonic orchestra at age 6 - Metaphor: Conductor still important after hundreds of years - Quote: "Get it 85% there with AI, then use craft to finish" - Key: Taste and judgment = what makes output art, not noise ### Belgium (The Gut-Punch) - Who: Actress, goes by Belgium - Story: Bedridden 18 months. AI avatars let her keep telling stories. - Quote: "AI film is my hope to be able to continue to exist and to tell something." - Landing: For some people, AI = access, not advantage ### Transformation Montage (Quick Hits) - Larissa -- Book editor. "Almost hostile attitude." Now building tools. - Louise -- Laid off from Brown. AI = "a colleague who has their problems but comes up with great ideas." - Sam -- Cut production time AND raised prices (first in 4 cohorts) - Kara -- Recent grad. School banned AI. "I want to learn as much as I can." ### Name the Bias (The Framing Problem) - Fernanda Ave -- Co-panelist at WiT Regatta - Story: Asked AI for "marketing professor" -> got man in power pose. Asked to make female -> got timid school teacher. Multiple rounds to get equal authority. - Instagram test: 11 of 13 saw the problem. 10 women, 1 man. 2 men saw nothing wrong. - Joy Buolamwini -- Dr., founder of Algorithmic Justice League. "The Poet of Code." - Gender Shades study: 34.7% error for darker-skinned women vs 0.8% for lighter-skinned men. 40x difference. - Coined terms: "The coded gaze" (Buolamwini). "Bias laundering" (KK) -- discrimination that looks like math. - The filmmaker bridge: "What's missing from the frame matters as much as what's in it." - Source: kriskrug.co/2026/02/03/name-the-bias/ --- ## KEY QUOTES (Exact Words) The Opening: "I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual." The Both/And: "AI is trained on stolen work without consent. And I'm more creative, more productive, and more powerful than I've ever been in my whole life. Both statements are true." Name the Bias: "Stop saying bias. Name what you're seeing. Misogyny. Racism. Sexism. Not 'algorithmic challenges.'" Bias Laundering: "Discrimination that looks like math. 'The algorithm said so' carries weight that 'my gut feeling says so' doesn't." The Framing Bridge: "What's missing from the frame matters as much as what's in it. Training data works exactly the same way." The Governance: "If all the thoughtful, ethical, critical people opt out -- governance gets made by opportunists and true believers. That's worse." The Protester: "If you're not 20 years old, pissed off, and challenging things, you're not really paying attention." The Mirror: "AI is a mirror. It shows us who we've been -- our patterns, our discrimination, our historical inequities -- and amplifies them at planetary scale. The mirror doesn't lie. But it only shows what we put in front of it." The Soul: "Feed your soul, not the machine." The Closing: "I'm here. Both hands full. Walking forward. You coming?" --- ## BRAZILIAN REFERENCES (Cultural Credibility) ### Cinema Novo - Glauber Rocha -- built a movement, not a product - Nelson Pereira dos Santos -- trained generations - Frame: ecosystem building before anyone called it that ### Contemporary - Kleber Mendonca Filho (Aquarius, Bacurau) -- builds from Recife - Anna Muylaert (The Second Mother) -- Sao Paulo stories - Karim Ainouz -- Ceara roots + international reach ### The Paras

docs.body: # Creative Rebellion in an Age of Synthetic Everything ## World AI Film Festival Brazil 2026 Speaker: Kris Krüg Event: World AI Film Festival - São Paulo Edition Date: September/October 2026 Duration: 45 minutes Audience: Filmmakers, producers, and creative professionals exploring AI in cinema --- ## Talk Overview ### Original Context This keynote was originally developed for Electronic Arts (EA) Vice Presidents in October 2025 - a private session for 8 executives focused on innovation in the age of AI and synthetic media. It has never been presented publicly. This adaptation transforms the executive-focused content for filmmakers and creative professionals at the World AI Film Festival in Brazil, maintaining the core philosophy while adding film-specific examples and Latin American cultural context. ### Core Philosophy (Unchanged) The Big Question: How do you stay creative and bring humanity to your work when AI can generate anything? The Answer: You don't compete with the machines. You build ecosystems of interconnected creative entities that require care, attention, and human vision over time. You rebel creatively - not against technology, but against the soulless application of it. --- ## Talk Structure (45 minutes) ### Part 1: The Problem (10 minutes) We're living in an age of synthetic everything #### Opening Hook - The Moltbot Moment While we're here talking about synthetic everything, 70,000 people just signed up to work FOR AI agents. Not with AI. FOR AI. This isn't future. This is happening right now. The question isn't whether AI will change creative work. It already has. The question is: will we build ecosystems that protect human creativity, or let consolidation consume it? #### The Current Reality - AI can now generate video, images, music, scripts, even entire film concepts - Hollywood studios are experimenting with AI-generated actors and scenes - Film schools worldwide are debating whether to teach AI tools - Brazil's creative industry (Rede Globo, independent studios) is already using these tools #### The Five Fears (Acknowledged) Before I offer any solutions, let me name what you're probably feeling. These fears are legitimate: 1. Stolen Work - AI was trained on mankind's creative output without consent. Your work, your teachers' work, generations of artists - fed into machines without permission. 2. Race to the Bottom - A logo that cost $5,000 now costs $0.30. Where does that leave you? 3. Junior Pipeline Collapse - Entry-level tasks are vanishing. The jobs that taught you craft are disappearing. I'm going to be honest with you: I teach AI for a living, and I don't have this one solved. 4. Cognitive Dependency - If you outsource your thinking, you lose the muscle. What happens to creativity when you stop exercising it? 5. Commodification - If everyone can generate, why hire creatives at all? These fears are real. Anyone telling you otherwise isn't paying attention. #### Walking Forward with Both Hands Full When we polled our most recent cohort - creative professionals from three continents - asking "Are you feeling more excited or more concerned about AI?" - most answered "equally both." That's the right answer. That's walking forward with both hands full. To lean too far into either camp is to miss the moment. #### The False Choice What they tell you: - Either embrace AI and lose your soul as an artist - Or reject AI and become irrelevant What I'm here to tell you: There's a third path. And it's more punk rock than either of those options. #### The Corporate Innovation Trap Traditional Model (What EA and Hollywood are doing): 1. Build a product 2. Launch it 3. Extract value 4. Move to next thing 5. Repeat Why this fails for creativity: - Products have lifecycles. Art has legacies. - Launches create hype. Communities create culture. - Extraction depletes. Care sustains. - Moving on abandons. Tending to builds depth. Film Example: How many streaming services launch shows, get one season, then cancel based on metrics? Compare that to filmmakers who build a body of work over decades, each film informing the next. --- ### Part 2: The Alternative Model (15 minutes) Ecosystems, not products #### What Is an Ecosystem? Think of a rainforest. Not one tree - thousands of species interconnected. Each one needs the others. Some are visible (the canopy), some work underground (mycelial networks). Remove one piece and the whole system changes. Your creative practice should work like this. #### The BC + AI Ecosystem Example From Personal Experience (Vancouver, Canada): - Started with 80 people in January 2024 - Didn't "scale" to 850+ by building bigger - Multiplied by creating conditions for communities to form: - Vancouver AI → Surrey AI → Squamish AI - Film Club exploring generative video (127 members, 1,174 messages in 6 months) - Mind AI & Consciousness (deep philosophical questions) - AI & Education (culturally responsive teaching) - Each community has its own culture, leadership, direction Not a product. An ecosystem. #### The Mycelial Network Metaphor Underground. Interconnected. Feeding the whole forest. For Filmmakers, This Means: - Your film isn't the only thing you make - Your creative relationships aren't transactions - Your audience isn't a "target market" - they're a community - Your career isn't a ladder - it's a web of interconnected projects, collaborations, learnings Brazil Example: Look at how Cinema Novo built an entire movement. Not one film, one director - a whole ecosystem of filmmakers, critics, musicians, writers who created a cultural shift. Glauber Rocha didn't just make films - he built the conditions for a generation of cinema. #### The Orchestration Era Here's what the best AI filmmakers have figured out: We're in the orchestration era now. It's not about one model doing everything. It's about coordinating multiple tools like a conductor orchestrating musicians - scripts, scenes, music, continuity all working together. The human is the conductor. The AI

docs.body: --- title: "How to Keep Our Souls Intact When the Machines Get Really Good at Making Everything" event: WAIFF Brasil 2026 — Friday Opening Keynote speaker: Kris Krüg duration: 48 minutes + Q&A audience: 600-700 film students and emerging filmmakers venue: FAPI/FAAP University, São Paulo, Brazil status: final-v1 datecreated: 2026-02-25 sources: - "Both Hands Full (LaSalle College, January 2026)" - "Dear AI: The Intervention (Bass Coast 2025)" - "Both Hands Full script (brazil-uai-2025)" - "How to Keep Our Souls Intact script (uai-film-festival-brazil-2026)" --- # HOW TO KEEP OUR SOULS INTACT WHEN THE MACHINES GET REALLY GOOD AT MAKING EVERYTHING --- ## PROLOGUE: CINEMA NOVO (0:00 – 2:00) [NO SLIDES. House lights still warm. Walk to center stage. No podium. Take a breath. Look at the room.] São Paulo. On the road to Cannes. A festival built around the question every filmmaker in this room is already living: what happens when AI enters the frame? [Pause. Let the room feel itself.] You know who else asked dangerous questions about cinema? Cinema Novo. Glauber Rocha didn't have a playbook. He had a camera and something to say about Brazil. He didn't build a product. He built a movement. [Beat.] I'm not here to teach you. I'm here to share what I've been figuring out — and honestly, to learn from you. Because here's the thing. I need to tell you something honest. --- ## ACT 1: LEFT HAND — WHAT'S ACTUALLY HAPPENING (2:00 – 14:00) [Walk stage left. Energy shifts. This is where we name what's real.] ### The Non-Consensual Opening (2:00 – 4:00) [SLIDE 1: "I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual."] [Pause. Direct eye contact. Serious tone.] I find my relationship with AI completely non-consensual. [Let that land. Silence.] I said those words to 200 art students in Vancouver last month and the room went quiet. It didn't ask me if it could steal my work. It didn't ask me if it could change everything around me. All I'm trying to do is keep my head above water and reinvent myself, you know? Just responding to this moment that I didn't ask for. [Beat.] I released thousands of photos on Flickr over two decades under Creative Commons. Shared them freely — that was the deal. When I checked a dataset search tool, I found roughly 1,800 of my images in a major training corpus. Nobody asked me. They just took it. [Turn to audience.] If you've ever released a film, posted a photograph, written a script, published anything online — it may already be in training data. You weren't asked. That's not hypothetical. That's what happened. ### Three Fears That Are Real (4:00 – 6:30) [SLIDE 2: Three Fears] Not hypothetical fears. Real shit happening right now. One: Stolen work without consent. Training data equals humanity's creative output scraped without permission. Your photos. Your scripts. Your scenes. Used without asking. No compensation. No attribution. Call it what it is. [Beat.] Two: Race to the bottom. "Good enough" AI outputs devalue human craft. Clients are saying it out loud now: "Can't you just use AI for this?" Translation: "Why should I pay you?" [Beat.] Three: Junior pipeline collapse. This is the one that keeps me up at night. Entry-level jobs are vanishing. The roles that trained the next generation are disappearing. How do people become directors if they can't be assistants first? How do cinematographers learn their craft if nobody hires camera assistants anymore? I teach this stuff for a living. And I'm going to be honest with you: I don't have the junior pipeline problem solved. Nobody does. And anyone who stands up here and tells you they've got it figured out? Don't listen to them. They're lying. ### Name What You're Seeing (6:30 – 8:00) [SLIDE 3: "STOP SAYING 'BIAS.' NAME WHAT YOU'RE SEEING."] [Direct. No softening.] And there's something else we need to talk about. What they built with our stolen work. We've said the word "bias" so many times it's lost meaning. Algorithmic bias. Training data bias. Gender bias. Generic. Safe. Abstract. Stop saying bias. Name what you're seeing. A colleague of mine asked an image generator to create a "marketing professor." She got a man in a power pose — Harvard blazer, authority, gravitas. Then she asked it to make the professor female. She got a timid school teacher. Not the same authority. Not the same context. A diminished version. [Beat.] It took her multiple rounds of prompting to get a woman with the same authority the system gave a man by default. That's not bias. That's misogyny embedded in image generation systems. Name it. [Direct.] When facial recognition fails on darker-skinned women at 40 times the rate it fails on lighter-skinned men — that's not a "performance gap." That's racism and sexism embedded in code. Dr. Joy Buolamwini proved it. You're filmmakers. You understand this instinctively. What's missing from the frame matters as much as what's in it. Training data works exactly the same way. I call it bias laundering — discrimination that looks like math. ### The 55% (8:00 – 9:30) [SLIDE 4: "55%"] Here's a number that changed how I think about all of this. Gallup data: 55% of US workers have never used AI at all. Only 12% use it daily. Half the workforce is still on the sidelines. [Pause.] Think about what that means. If all the thoughtful, ethical, critical people opt out of AI — if they decide it's too problematic to touch — governance gets made by opportunists and true believers. The people who understand the risks best aren't in the room when the decisions get made. [Direct.] That's worse. That's worse than engaging with critique intact. ### The First Protester (9:30 – 10:30) [SLIDE 5: "GOOD."] I had my first protester at a keynote recently. Back row. Video camera rolling. Took the mic during Q&A. Wouldn't give it back. Made YouTube soundbites. [Beat. Then warmth:] And here's the thing — she's right to be concerned. If you're not 20 years old, pissed off, and challenging things, you're not really paying attention to what's

docs.body: # World AI Film Festival (WAIFF) -- Sao Paulo 2026 -- Keynote Materials ## Both Hands Full: Walking Forward Through the Age of Synthetic Everything Speaker: Kris Krug Event: World AI Film Festival (WAIFF) -- Sao Paulo Edition Date: February 27-28, 2026 (Sao Paulo stop on the road to Cannes Grand Finale, April 21-22) Duration: 44 minutes + Q&A Audience: Filmmakers, industry professionals, AI cinema creators Festival Leadership: President: Gong Li. Honorary President: Claude Lelouch. Jury President: Agnes Jaoui. Ambassador: Jean-Michel Jarre. --- ## Overview This directory contains materials for Kris Krug's keynote at the World AI Film Festival (WAIFF) Sao Paulo edition. The talk evolved from two sources: "Creative Rebellion in an Age of Synthetic Everything" (originally developed for EA executives, Dec 2025) and "Both Hands Full" (the signature keynote on holding critique and curiosity simultaneously). The synthesis leads with vulnerability and story over frameworks, includes character-driven transformation arcs, integrates the soul dimension from Bass Coast, and ends with a performed invocation rather than rhetorical questions. Related GitHub Issue: #778 - Brazil WAIFF: Both Hands Full Keynote --- ## Documents in This Directory ### PRIMARY: New "Both Hands Full" Materials #### 1. BOTH-HANDS-FULL-SCRIPT.md Complete 44-minute speaker-ready script The full keynote with stage directions, timing marks, and transition language. Structured as: - Prologue: Cinema Novo as creative rebellion precedent (no slides) - Act I -- Left Hand: Non-consensual opening, three fears, the 55%, first protester - Act II -- Right Hand: Luke/80/20 Flip, Kevin/Conductor, Belgium, transformation montage, Both/And pivot - Act III -- Third Path: Binary trap, vibe coding + knowledge bases, Brazilian opportunity, soul dimension - Closing: The choice, the stakes, the invocation ("You coming?") Includes Q&A prep, timing table, and stage direction summary. Use: Primary script for rehearsal and delivery. #### 2. BOTH-HANDS-FULL-SLIDES.md 24-slide deck outline with visual direction Slide-by-slide breakdown in DIY punk zine aesthetic (BLACK, WHITE, RED only), stencil/typewriter typography, collage production method, image/asset needs list, and production notes for large-venue delivery. Use: Brief for slide designer. Hand to visual team. #### 3. BOTH-HANDS-FULL-SPEAKER-NOTES.md Quick-reference card for backstage and onstage Timing cheat sheet, key stats to memorize, character details (Luke, Kevin, Belgium, montage names), exact quotes, Brazilian cultural references, physical/vocal reminders, backup examples, Q&A one-liners, and pre-talk checklist. Use: Print this. Keep it backstage. Glance at it before you walk on. --- ### LEGACY: Original "Creative Rebellion" Materials These documents contain the earlier framework-heavy version. Still useful for reference, Q&A depth, and supplementary examples. #### 4. KEYNOTE-OUTLINE-FILM-STUDENTS.md Original 45-minute talk structure (Creative Rebellion version) Ecosystem thinking, mycelial network metaphor, orchestration era framework, practical AI applications by production phase. Now superseded by the Both Hands Full script for delivery, but retains useful detail on the 80/20 Flip, Moltbot hook, and five fears framework. #### 5. TALKING-POINTS-AND-EXAMPLES.md Detailed talking points, examples, and storytelling techniques Brazilian film context, cultural bridges, emergency backup examples, personal anecdotes. Useful as supplementary prep material alongside the new speaker notes. #### 6. FILM-VIDEO-CASE-STUDIES.md Real-world examples and case studies for film/video AI integration Nine detailed case studies (Vancouver Film Club, Alfonso Cuaron's Roma, Runway AI Film Festival, Indigenous AI, etc.). Useful for Q&A responses and follow-up discussions. --- ## Core Philosophy (Unchanged from EA Talk) ### The Big Question How do you stay creative and bring humanity to your work when AI can generate anything? ### The Answer You don't compete with machines. You build ecosystems of interconnected creative entities that require care, attention, and human vision over time. You rebel creatively - not against technology, but against the soulless application of it. ### Key Concepts Ecosystem Thinking vs. Product Thinking: - Not one perfect film - many interconnected projects - Not individual achievement - community building - Not extracting value - creating culture - Not optimizing for metrics - optimizing for meaning - Not sprint mentality - marathon mentality The Mycelial Network: Like a forest, your visible work (films) is supported by underground networks (relationships, community, ongoing learning, collaborations). Tend to the whole ecosystem, not just the visible product. Human Elements AI Can't Replace: 1. Cultural context and lived experience 2. Curation and taste 3. Intention and meaning 4. Relationships and collaboration 5. Values and vision --- ## Adaptation Strategy: EA Executives → Film Industry Professionals ### What Changed Audience: - FROM: 8 EA Vice Presidents (corporate executives) - TO: 600 filmmakers, industry professionals, and AI cinema creators at WAIFF Sao Paulo Context: - FROM: North American corporate innovation - TO: Brazil/Latin America film culture Examples: - FROM: Enterprise software, corporate case studies - TO: Film industry, Cinema Novo, Brazilian filmmakers Language: - FROM: Business jargon (ROI, KPIs, product-market fit) - TO: Creative language (vision, community, artistic legacy) Framing: - FROM: "How to innovate in your company" - TO: "How to build a sustainable creative practice" ### What Stayed the Same Core Philosophy: - Ecosystem thinking over product thinking - Community over competition - Care over extraction - Long-term building over short-term wins Central Metaphor: - Mycelial network (works universally) Call to Action: - Start small, build community, think decades - Technology serves vision, doesn't replace it Personal Stories: - Kris's journey building BC + A

docs.body: # Brazil AI Film Festival 2026 — Talk Titles & Abstracts DRAFT Generated: 2026-01-09 Status: Ready for KK selection Voice: Creative rebellion + bridge-builder (per your request) --- ## Session 1: Opening Keynote (Friday Morning) Slot: 45 minutes | Audience: 600-700 film students and emerging filmmakers Context: Second presentation of the festival. Sets the philosophical tone. Should be optimistic, high-energy, punk rock but not cynical. --- ### Option 1A: Creative Rebellion in an Age of Synthetic Everything Subtitle: Why the soul of your work isn't something AI can generate Abstract (140 words): AI can now create video, music, scripts, even entire film concepts. So what's left for you? Not a strategy talk. Not a tools demo. This is an intervention. The choice they're selling you — embrace AI and lose your soul, or reject AI and become irrelevant — is a lie. There's a third path. It's more punk rock than either option. Drawing from two years building Canada's largest grassroots AI community (850+ members), and decades as a photographer who watched darkrooms become algorithms, Kris shares what he's learned: You don't compete with machines. You build creative ecosystems that require care, attention, and human vision over time. You rebel — not against technology, but against its soulless application. The question isn't whether AI will change filmmaking. It already has. The question is: what kind of filmmaker will you become? 1-line blurb: An intervention on keeping your soul when machines can generate anything. --- ### Option 1B: The Machines Can Make Anything — So What's Your Job Now? Subtitle: Building creative ecosystems when content becomes commodity Abstract (145 words): When AI can generate your B-roll, write your coverage, and score your film — what exactly is left for you to do? Everything that matters. This isn't a talk about tools. It's about the shift from maker to selector, from content producer to culture builder, from individual hustle to ecosystem tending. Twenty-seven years of pointing cameras at people taught me one thing: technology changes, but the human elements — taste, intention, lived experience, community — don't get automated. They get amplified. From Vancouver's AI Film Club to Cinema Novo's movement-building, the pattern holds: The filmmakers who thrive don't compete with machines. They create conditions where their vision, relationships, and cultural knowledge become irreplaceable. Your generation gets to answer the question Hollywood won't: What does cinema look like when anyone can generate anything? This is how you start building the answer. 1-line blurb: What remains irreplaceable when machines can generate anything — and how to build from there. --- ### Option 1C: Original Intelligence Subtitle: You're the source. AI is just the amplifier. Abstract (130 words): Every keynote on AI tells you what the machines can do. This one's about what you can do. You're not competing with AI. You're not being replaced by it. You are the original intelligence — your memories, your culture, your relationships, your specifically Brazilian perspective that no dataset contains. AI amplifies what you bring. If you bring nothing, you get content. If you bring vision, lived experience, and cultural depth, you get cinema. This isn't optimism. It's observation. From favela stories that Hollywood can't imagine to ecosystems built one monthly meetup at a time, the pattern is clear: human creativity isn't threatened by AI. It's revealed by it. The tools are powerful. Your vision is irreplaceable. Here's how to build a practice that makes that true. 1-line blurb: You're the source. AI is the amplifier. Here's how to build a creative practice that proves it. --- ### Option 1D: Don't Make Content. Build Culture. Subtitle: Why ecosystems outlast algorithms Abstract (135 words): Streaming platforms cancel shows after one season based on metrics. Studios chase trends instead of developing voices. "Content creation" is replacing storytelling. Meanwhile: Cinema Novo built a movement, not products. Agnès Varda made films for sixty years, each informing the next. Vancouver's AI Film Club grew from five panicked photographers to hundreds of creators sharing what works. The difference? Ecosystems versus extraction. This keynote is about shifting from product thinking (make it, launch it, move on) to ecosystem thinking (build conditions where your community and craft sustain each other for decades). AI makes this both urgent and possible. Urgent because content is now commodity. Possible because the same tools let you build global community from São Paulo while staying rooted in Brazilian stories. Don't make content. Build culture. 1-line blurb: Shifting from product thinking to ecosystem building — why community outlasts the algorithm. --- ### Option 1E: Cinema After the Flood Subtitle: Making movies when the machines learned to dream Abstract (140 words): November 2022. A flood hit. Machines learned to dream — to generate images from text, then video, then entire worlds. Every creative industry woke up underwater. What now? Some filmmakers are building arks. Gatekeeping. Resisting. Others are drowning — outsourcing vision to prompts, generating slop, racing to the bottom. There's a third response. Not ark-building or drowning. Growing gills. Adapting without abandoning what made cinema matter in the first place. From the favelas of City of God to the streets of Roma, from the experimental edges of Vancouver's AI Film Club to Runway's creator community — filmmakers are discovering that human creativity isn't threatened by synthetic media. It's clarified by it. This is a talk about what remains essential when everything else becomes generative. And how to build a practice — a community — around what can't be automated. 1-line blurb: Growing gills, not building arks — adapting without abandoning what makes cinema human. --- ## Session 2: Hub Small Group Talk (Saturday) Slot: 20 minutes with 6-10 peo

headings.text: 1. Title Slide — "How to Keep Our Souls Intact"

docs.body: ## 1. Title Slide — "How to Keep Our Souls Intact" For: Opening slide Prompt: Chaotic punk zine cover, dense overlapping collage with ripped magazine clippings, torn film strips, circuit boards, camera parts, newspaper fragments piled on top of each other with visible tape and glue. Halftone dots, ink smudges, coffee stains, staple holes. Black white and blood red only. Xerox grain everywhere. The following text MUST appear prominently in mixed cut-out ransom-note typography at various angles and sizes: "HOW TO KEEP OUR SOULS INTACT" as the largest text, and "WHEN THE MACHINES GET REALLY GOOD AT MAKING EVERYTHING" below it, and small stamped text "WAIFF BRASIL 2026" in a corner. Raw handmade zine energy. ## 2. Binary Trap — Boosters vs Doomers For: Act 1 Prompt: Punk zine spread ripped down the middle with torn paper edge. Left: corporate figure with exclamation marks. Right: figure with arms crossed, resistant. Both sides covered in xeroxed clippings and scratchy handwriting. Dense collage, halftone grain. Black white red. The following text MUST appear: "BOOSTERS" on the left side, "DOOMERS" on the right side, and a red banner across center reading "BOTH OF THESE ARE LAZY". Photocopied political broadsheet aesthetic. ## 3. Both Hands Full — Thesis For: Core framework Prompt: Two photocopied hands palms up, cut from a magazine, pasted onto black background with visible tape. Surrounded by torn text fragments and handwritten scrawls. Halftone dots, ink blots, xerographic artifacts. Black white red. The following text MUST appear: Left hand area labeled "STOLEN WORK" "NO CONSENT" "REAL HARM". Right hand area labeled "TRANSFORMATION" "CAPABILITY" "AGENCY". Between the hands in large text: "BOTH THINGS ARE TRUE". Below: "walk forward holding both". Anarcho-punk zine page. ## 4. The Five Fears For: Act 1 fears list Prompt: Punk zine list page with five numbered items in mixed media typography — stencil, typewriter, marker, cut-out letters. Margins filled with angry annotations and question marks. Red rubber stamp marks. Cheap newsprint texture, staple rust, xerox grain. Black white red. The following text MUST appear as the five items: "1. THE THEFT" "2. THE RACE TO BOTTOM" "3. THE PIPELINE COLLAPSE" "4. THE DEPENDENCY" "5. COMMODIFICATION". Corner stamp reads "DON'T DROP THIS HAND". Margin note: "I teach this for a living and I don't know". ## 5. Doing It Backwards For: Provocation Prompt: Stencil-sprayed text on concrete wall texture, dripping paint, photocopied badly with blown contrast. Below: torn collage of a robot painting art alongside a human doing paperwork. Taped down crooked. Ink splatters, marker scrawls. Xeroxed punk flyer aesthetic. Black white red. The following text MUST appear: Large dripping stencil text "WE'RE DOING IT BACKWARDS" at top, and annotation "automating art. keeping the drudgery." and red stamp "FLIP IT". ## 6. Better Humans Not Better Machines For: Pivot moment Prompt: Almost entirely typographic punk zine page. Mixed media text assembled collage-style: typewriter, stencil spray, newspaper cutouts, hand-scrawled marker, all at different angles creating visual chaos. Red underlines and circles. Xerox grain, paper texture. Ransom note manifesto aesthetic. Black white red. The following text MUST appear: "THE NEXT EVOLUTION ISN'T ABOUT BEING BETTER MACHINES" at top, and larger below with red emphasis: "IT'S ABOUT BEING WORSE MACHINES AND BETTER HUMANS" with "BETTER HUMANS" biggest and in red. ## 7. Transformation Stories For: Evidence Prompt: Punk zine interview page. Three rough rectangular frames in a column, each with a high-contrast photocopied silhouette portrait. Typewriter text and handwritten annotations next to each. Cheap xerox paper, staple holes, tape marks. DIY music zine layout. Black white red. The following text MUST appear next to each frame: "3 WEEKS → 4 HOURS", "25 YRS VFX → VIBE LOUNGE", "2,000 PORTRAITS → OWN MODEL". Red banner at bottom: "NOT REPLACED. TRANSFORMED." ## 8. The Ecosystem — Mycelium For: Ecosystem thinking Prompt: Hand-drawn network diagram in thick black marker on cheap paper, photocopied. Organic branching mycelium lines connecting nodes. Some nodes circled in red. Annotations in tiny handwriting. Newspaper clippings pasted around edges. Conspiracy board meets punk scene map. Xerox grain, tape marks, coffee ring. Black white red. The following text MUST appear: nodes labeled "FILM CLUB" "MIND AI" "EDUCATION" "ETHICS" "SURREY" with central node "850+". Below: "NOT A PRODUCT. AN ECOSYSTEM." Corner: "Cinema Novo did this first". ## 9. AI Is A Mirror For: Core insight Prompt: Stark photocopied image of a hand holding a small mirror, high contrast with halftone dots breaking apart. Mirror reflection scratched over with red marker. Sparse page with breathing room — the quiet page in the zine. Xerographic artifacts, fourth-generation photocopy look. Black white red. The following text MUST appear: large stencil text below the image "SELF-KNOWLEDGE IS THE PREREQUISITE". Small annotation: "AI is a mirror". Dark, moody, intimate. ## 10. Selector — Taste Is Your Moat For: Selector principle Prompt: Punk music fanzine page. A vinyl record photocopied into heavy halftone, pasted at angle. Surrounding: torn gig flyers, handwritten setlists, cut-out reviews, all layered and overlapping. Red marker highlights. Tape holding things down. Dense collage, xerox grain. Black white red. The following text MUST appear: "GENERATION IS CHEAP. TASTE IS NOT." as main text, "DJs WERE CALLED SELECTORS" nearby, margin notes "Tropicalia / Bossa Nova / Cinema Novo", red stamp "YOUR TASTE IS YOUR MOAT". ## 11. Write For The Bot For: Cultural activism Prompt: Photocopied typewriter on cheap paper, high contrast halftone. Paper emerging from typewriter with dense text. Surrounded by torn newspaper clippings taped at angles, urgent handwritten notes, red rubber stamps. Punk zine editorial page. Xerographic grain, staple holes. Black white red. The following text

docs.title: Who Sets the Direction Now? How a Grassroots Community Became Essential Stakeholders in 20 Months
docs.summary: By Kris Krüg Published: January 2026 Reading Time: 9 minutes

headings.text: From Meetups to Political Power (The 20-Month Journey)
The Creative Mornings Lesson (Relational Power Building)
The Anti-Keynote That Became a Movement
The Showing Up Checklist (How We Did It)

docs.body: # Who Sets the Direction Now? How a Grassroots Community Became Essential Stakeholders in 20 Months By Kris Krüg Published: January 2026 Reading Time: 9 minutes --- October 2025. Office Hours. Loki unmutes. He's just back from All-In Montreal—Scale AI's big conference, 6,000 people. "Minister Solomon announced a 30-day AI strategy sprint," he says. "There's a task force, 25-29 members, who have 30 days to revise the pan-Canadian AI strategy. Good luck with that." I'm listening. Thinking: Wait. Who's on this task force? Who's shaping Canada's AI future? Loki continues: "Three BC representatives: First Nations Tech Council, Gail Murphy from UBC, Diane Gutiv from CGI." I notice: All women. Three voices from BC at the table where Canada's AI direction gets set. Then Loki drops the line that hits me: "BC was the biggest delegation. It made an impression. Minister Solomon toured all over the place, but he made a point of coming to the Team BC booth." Here's what matters: BC + AI didn't need to lobby to be part of this conversation. We were already essential stakeholders they had to consult. Not because we're the richest. Not because we're the biggest. But because over 20 months, we built something undeniable. ## The Question Everyone's Asking Who gets to shape AI's future? Three forces are fighting for control: Force #1: Markets. Silicon Valley. Tech companies with billions in capital and compute infrastructure. They shape AI by building it. ChatGPT drops, world changes overnight. No consultation. No democratic process. Just: Here's the technology. Figure it out. Force #2: Policy. Governments trying to regulate something moving faster than they can understand. Slow. Bureaucratic. The EU AI Act took years to develop and was obsolete by the time it passed. Canada's 30-day sprint? Ambitious. But 30 days to revise national AI strategy? As Loki said: "Good luck with that." Force #3: Culture. Communities. Grassroots organizing. Values-first development. Building alternatives that neither markets nor policy imagined. The question: Which force wins? The answer: Whichever one shows up most effectively. And I can show you exactly how that works. ## From Meetups to Political Power (The 20-Month Journey) December 2023. I host Meetup #1 at the Space Center. Maybe 80 people show up. Those first 20 monthly meetups weren't just programming. They were community development infrastructure. Each event built trust, established values, tested formats, refined messaging, and created the relationships that made nonprofit organizing possible. By August 2024, we'd done 20 consecutive months. That's when we launched the BC + AI Ecosystem Association. 34 founding members. The nonprofit as political strategy. One month later: 130+ paying members. No VC funding. No investor pitch decks. Just: "Join us. Build something better together." And 130+ people across BC said yes. October 2025: Minister Solomon announces the 30-day AI strategy sprint. BC + AI has a seat at that table. The progression: Cultural organizing (20 meetups) → Economic power (130+ members) → Political influence (government consultation). Twenty months. Zero to essential stakeholder. ## Why Association Structure Matters Think about legitimacy. "We're a startup" → Government says: "That's nice. Good luck." "We represent 130+ members across BC" → Government says: "We should probably talk to you." Associations have political legitimacy corporations don't. We're not lobbyists pushing an agenda. We're stakeholders representing constituents. When government develops AI policy for BC, they need to consult the actual humans doing AI work in BC. That's us. The power: We didn't ask to be included. We became essential by building something government couldn't ignore. ## The Creative Mornings Lesson (Relational Power Building) October 3, 2025. Same Office Hours call. I'm telling the community about Creative Mornings. Mark Busussy runs it. 14 years. Monthly creative meetups. 200+ people every month. Standard sponsorship: $1,500. Mark's pitch: "You can pay as little as you want, as low as 100 bucks." I'm thinking: "Bro, I don't think the first thing our association should spend money on is a big sponsorship." But Mark sees something I don't fully see yet. We gave him $200 to buy breakfast. Just between us on the call, you know, he counted our $200 as a $1,500 sponsorship and made us a full partner. Result: Booth space. Speaking slots—me plus Kevin Friel. Printed postcards with our names on them. 200 creative professionals reached. The math doesn't make sense in transactional capitalism. But in relational power building, it's perfect. My exact words from that call: "You got to give more than you take in order for these scenarios to work." Not transactional. Relational. That $200 bought us something VC millions can't buy: Credibility with 200 creatives who now see BC + AI as aligned with their values. Some will join. Some will collaborate. Some will advocate for us when policy questions come up. That's political capital. ## The Anti-Keynote That Became a Movement August 2024. Meetup #20. Space Center. 250 people. That night, I was supposed to give a keynote. I canceled it. Instead of one person talking at 250 people, I created space for Jos Duncan's transformative love-and-justice framework, Kevin Friel's SIGGRAPH connections, the hackathon winners' breakthrough creativity, and genuine community announcements. I call it "sampling little bits" instead of delivering a traditional keynote. Why cancel my own keynote? Because the point wasn't me talking AT you. The point was us building together. By canceling the keynote, I was modeling the collaborative leadership approach that makes our community sustainable. No single person—including me—should dominate the conversation or define the community's direction. That night, we launched the BC + AI Ecosystem Association. The keynote I never gave became the structure we built instead. ## The Container Philosophy At the launch, I sai
